A war memorial near the ancient settlement of Tropaeum Traiani (modern Adamclisi)
A hilltop sanctuary, in use from the Bronze Age until the time of the Roman Empire.
A small Hellenistic town excavated on the Trypitos promontory E of Siteia, Lasithi Crete. It is probably to be identified with Polichna, attested in ancient literary sources.
A large Early Helladic cemetery excavated at the Leoforos Plataion near Marathon, Attiki.
The foundations of a Hellenistic farm located near the shores of Lake Boibe in Thessaly.

The small Aegean island of Tsimintiri, located between Despotiko and Antiparos, has produced archaeological evidence for a large sanctuary and/or other public complex of archaic date, likely to be associated with the extensive archaic sanctuary of Mandra, located on the island of Despotiko, to which Tsimintiri was connected in antiquity.
The archaeological site of Tsoungiza shows evidence of human activity since the Early Neolithic period. Modern excavations began under Carl Blegen in 1924.

An ancient settlement located in the modern Soummam river valley at a place called Tiklat, which is near modern El Kseur in Algeria's Kabylia province. Here the Roman emperor Augustus established a veteran colony. Bishops of Tubusuctu are attested in the fifth century CE.
Tugunbulak was a Medieval city on the Silk Route covering an area of ca. 120 hectares (300 acres).

Ancient textual sources, including Dionysius of Halicarnassus and Plutarch, place the purported dwelling place of Romulus near the Scalae Caci at the southwestern corner of the Palatine Hill in Rome. Whether the place cited by ancient texts and maintained in antiquity until at least the fourth century CE corresponds to the archaeological remains of archaic huts excavated near the Clivus Victoriae remains a matter of debate.
Mound where glazed brick has been found, currently overbuilt.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 F4 Tulul Abu Jawan

Tulul al-Baqarat is an archaeological site located seven kilometers northeast of Tell al-Wilayah, Iraq. The settlement was occupied from the fourth millennium BCE to the Islamic period. It may have been the Early Dynastic city of Kesh.
Two mounds from Parthian and Sassanian periods where Old Babylonian bricks have been found.
Mound where stamped bricks have been found.
Group of kassite mounds, some of which have been completely obliterated or covered by residential buildings.
Mound and find place for cuneiform inscription.

An archaeological site in northern Sudan that includes Tombos Island and the adjacent riverbank. The site is located at the Third Cataract of the Nile River and was occupied form the mid-18th Dynasty of Egypt and through the 25th Dynasty.

A settlement of northwestern Sardinia, located at the mouth of the Mannu River. The site served as a port in Phoenician, Carthaginian, and Roman phases of occupation. The modern Porto Torres stands on the site, about 10 miles distant from Sassari.
Now unlocated, Turrita was a station (statio) near Pisae.
A chain of islands between the Ligurian Sea and Tyrrhenian Sea.
An ancient city of Cappadocia that was the capital of the Neo-Hittite kingdom in the first millennium B.C.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 B2 Typaneai
A Middle Helladic settlement excavated near Nemea.
Uchi Maius was a township of Africa Proconsularis where Marius settled veterans. It did not achieve the status of colonia until the third century.
The Uffington White Horse is stylized prehistoric hill figure dating to the later prehistoric period in England.

At West Hill, Uley, Gloucestershire, excavations revealed the remains of a multi-period ritual site. This site remained in use from the Neolithic period to the Middle Ages. An Iron Age sanctuary was replaced by a Romano-British temple dedicated to Mercury in the early second century A.D.
An ancient settlement mound located 25 km west of İzmir with occupation ranging from the Early Neolithic to Late Roman and Early Byzantine periods.
The remains of a monument marking the symbolic navel of the city of Rome that dates to the reign of Septimius Severus.
Mound, find place for inscribed brick.

Umm al-Wawiya is an archaeological site in present-day Iraq. It is located in the vicinity of the ancient site of Uruk, and some scholars have argued that Umm al-Wawiya could be the site of the ancient town of Durum.
Bronze age city in northern Syria, located approximately 50 kilometers east of Aleppo.
A necropolis at Abydos, Egypt, with royal tombs from the Pre-dynastic period as well as from the Early Dynastic (First and Second dynasties) period.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 78 C3 Umm Towat

Umma was an important ancient city of Sumer but has not been scientifically excavated. The mainstream of scholarly opinion associates the ancient site with Tell Jokha, Iraq, located in Iraq's Northwestern Dhi Qar Governorate. This area is remote and is associated with a network of ancient canals. Tell Jokha measures about 1,500 meters in diameter.

While Arthur Evans gave this archaeological site the moniker "Unexplored mansion", it was in fact an annexe of the "Little Palace" and post-dates it. The "Unexplored Mansion" was built in MM IIIB or LM IA.
Although never completed, the 'unfinished obelisk' at Aswan in Egypt is the largest known ancient obelisk.
Unit IV-1 is a large, megaron-style building constructed during the 11th century BCE at the site of Nichoria in Messenia, Greece.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 K2 no. 75 (Opuk UKR)
An ancient road linking Nomentum and Eretum. Portions of the road are extant, particularly within an archaeological area at Monterotondo.
An unnamed Persian rock relief located near Sundurlu.
An unnamed Gallo-Roman settlement and temple, located near modern Champallement-Compierre in France.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D4 unnamed unlabeled ((7 monuments, 1 cemetery, 2 walls))

An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 C1 unnamed unlabeled ((5 monuments, 2 cemeteries))

A major Sumerian city-state founded during the Ubaid period, ca. 3800 B.C. In archaeological terms, Ur is particularly well known for the lavish tombs of its Royal Cemetery, its massive Ziggurat. The site served as the capital of the Ur III dynasty.
A stone circle measuring 33 by 26 feet with 11 standing stones located near Gleninchaquin Park, Tuosist, County Kerry, Ireland.
A town mentioned in Neo-Assyrian sources that is likely to be identified as Tell ʿĀmūdā.

An ancient settlement located on an island in the Nile just south of the Second Cataract, now in the north of Sudan. An ancient fortress dating to the reigns of the Middle Kingdom pharaohs (19th century BC), Senusret I and Senusret III, is found there.

Uruk was an ancient Sumerian (and later Babylonian) city located on the Euphrates river. At its height (ca. 2900 BC), Uruk's population may have topped 50,000 people and its walls enclosed an area of more than six square kilometers, making it the largest city in the world at that point in time.
A Bronze and Iron Age site in Yozgat province, Turkey, tentatively identified as the Hittite town of Zippalanda (Çadır Höyük is another candidate for the location of Zippalanda).
A Punic center located west of Carthage.

A Bronze Age archaeological site with Deer Stones located at Uushigiin Ovor (UO), Mongolia, that is included in the cultural landscape "Deer Stone Monuments and Related Bronze Age Sites", inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2023.

Vagniacis was a small Roman site of the first to fifth centuries with likely specialized religious functions. A series of five temples are situated within a temenos along the Watling Street at Springhead.

Located in the Lombardy plain, the Valcamonica assemblage constitutes one of the world's greatest collections of prehistoric petroglyphs – created over a period of 8,000 years. The site was enlisted by UNESCO in 1971.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 H3 Valetium/Balesium
The "Valle dei Templi" of Agrigento is an archaeological site in Sicily located on a ridge, as opposed to the false suggestion of the conventional name, outside modern Agrigento.
Van Fortress is an ancient citadel of Urartu built during the ninth through seventh centuries BC.
Vani (7th -1st centuries BCE) is an ancient city located in the Colchis Lowlands on the western bank of the Sulori River.
Vasio (modern Vaison-la-Romaine) was the civitas capital of the Vocontii and eventually received Roman status by the late first century BC.

An obelisk from Heliopolis in Egypt that the emperor Caius erected in the Circus Gaii et Neronis. It now stands in St. Peter's square in Vatican City, having been re-erected there by Domenico Fontana in 1586.
A significant Hellenistic city that served as a capital of both the Seleucid and Parthian empires. The city was founded by Seleucus Nicator.

Veliki Preslav was a fortified site that served as the capital of the Tsar Simeon I of the Bulgarian Empire. The site flourished during the ninth and tenth centuries CE. The site includes both 'inner' and 'outer' fortified areas.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 B1 Veneticae Inss.
A Roman theater at modern Ventimiglia in Italy, dating to the early second century CE.
Originally a settlement of the Peucetti, Venusia became a Latin colony in 291 BCE following the Third Samnite War.

Vessagiriya is the site of an ancient Buddhist forest monastery in Sri Lanka. It is considered part of the ruined site of Anuradhapura. The monastery was initially built in the middle of the third century BCE and was expanded in the late fifth century CE. A Roman bronze coin, as well as punch-marked coins and/or ingots, were excavated at Vessagiriya in the first decade of the 20th century.

An ancient necropolis located in the modern Parco della Serenissima that was initially discovered via construction works in 1997. The necropolis is located, in ancient terms, in a landscape between the line of the via Collatina and that of the via Praenestina. The excavations also revealed traces of first-millennium BCE agricultural activity.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 D3 Vicus Martis
A complex of civic and sacred buildings in the urban area of ancient Caere, including the so-called "elliptical building".
A Late Republican to early Imperial coastal villa located at the mouth of the Fosso delle Guardiole near Castrum Novum. Two artificial fish ponds are associated with the villa.
A palatial Roman villa dating to the reign of Maximian.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 A2 Vlithias
Volcei (modern Buccino) was an ancient city of Lucania that was subjugated by the Romans in 209 BC.

Volsinii, also referred to as Volsinii Novi, is thought to be the settlement where Rome resettled the survivors of the Volsinian rebellion of 264 BCE. This episode is discussed by Zonaras (8. 7. 8). A late republican center at Bolsena, Italy, with civic architecture (forum) and domus has been excavated since 1946 by the École Française and is identified as Volsinii Novi.
A town of Mauretania Tingitana and a Roman colony.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Vounari
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 F4 Vroulia
A Bronze Age settlement located on the south coast of the island of Tinos to the west of Agios Phokas.
Two inscribed reliefs of a Neo-Babylonian king (probably Nebuchadnezzar II) are carved on a rock face in the Wadi es-Saba (“Valley of the Lion”), southwest of Akroum, Lebanon.

The Wall of Media, also known as Mēdías teîchos or the Wall of Semiramis, at Habl as-Sahr is mentioned by Xenophon in the Anabasis as a defensive ditch at the Euphrates built on the orders of Artaxerxes in the fifth century BC.

One of two Roman forts located at modern Welzheim in Germany, the excavated remains of the "Ostkastell" are largely preserved. The west gate and a portion of the associated defensive wall was reconstructed in the 1970s.
The so-called West House is an architectural complex built as an annex to a larger building, the so-called Red House, in an Early Minoan settlement at Vasiliki on Crete.

Fortification at the western edge of the settlement of Serdica, constructed in the 2nd century CE and disused by the end of the 6th century CE. The site was excavated in 2011-2013 and is now an archaeological park.
Stone archway constructed as part of the expansion of the Temple Mount in Jerusalem during the early Roman period.

Woodhenge is the site of a "Neolithic Class II henge and timber circle monument" located within the Stonehenge UNESCO World Heritage Site in Wiltshire, England. It is located approximately 2 miles (3.2 km) northeast of Stonehenge (Durrington parish).
Facilities built by the workers of the village of Deir el-Medina to serve as a place of rest and worship on the road linking the village of Deir el-Medina to the Valley of the Kings.
An ancient city of Lycia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 B5 Xenagori Inss.

The Western Xia mausoleums are located in northwestern China in the Ningxia Hui Autonomous Region. This UNESCO World Heritage Site includes nine imperial mausoleums and 250 tombs of imperial relatives and officials of China's Western Xia dynasty.
Extensive groups of Iron Age tumuli in the Lesser Caucasian mountains. A Neo-Assyrian seal bearing the name of Adad-nirari II (911-891 BCE) was found here during excavations in 1895.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Yaat
An archaeological site with multiple settlement horizons.

The Yaoshan Site is part of the larger UNESCO cultural heritage landscape, "Archaeological Ruins of Liangzhu City". According to UNESCO, the Yaoshan Site dates ca. 3300-3100 BCE and covers an area of some 66.56 hectares (164.5 acres).
Yarim Tepe was the site of a Neolithic settlement located in Iran's Gorgan plain. Archaeological fieldwork was first undertaken by V. E. Crawford in 1963.
Yarim Tepe (Iraq) is an archaeological site in Iraq's Sinjar Valley that emerged ca. 6000 BCE. Its later phases are associated first with the Hassuna culture, and then the Halaf and Ubaid cultures.
Mound and find place for cuneiform inscription.
A mound site in Kırşehir province in Turkey containing a palace or temple structure dated to the first half of the 2nd millennium BCE or the first half of the 1st millennium BCE.

Yazılıkaya (also known as 'Midas City') is an archaeological site in Eskişehir Province, Turkey, known for its Phrygian rock-cut monuments, facades, altars, and niches dedicated to the Mother Goddess Matar.
Destroyed Armenian monastery in the village of Bakraçlı where Urartian inscriptions have been found.

Yoncalı is located near the modern cities of Malazgirt and Bulanık, at the western end of the Bulanık-Malazgirt plain. The Assyrian king Tiglat-pileser I placed an inscribed, rock-cut relief here to commemorate his victories in the region.
Site for Urartian palace and citadel.
Rural settlement of the Roman and Late Antique periods in Thrace, located near the modern village of Stroyno, Bulgaria.
This Sumerian/Babylonian city (also known as Zabalam, now as modern Tell Ibzeikh) was a cult centre of the goddess Inanna/Ishtar. It was located near the Iturungal and Ninagina canals.
A Minoan "villa" along the road leading south from Zakros, Lasithi, Crete.
An ancient settlement on the Aegean island of the same name.
Zaldapa, located near Abrit, Bulgaria, was a fortified Late Roman settlement in Moesia Inferior. It was restored under Justinian I.
Zama Regia/Zama *Maior in Africa Proconsularis was the site of the Battle of Zama on October 19, 202 B.C., at the close of the Second Punic War fought between Rome and Carthage.
Evidence for second millennium B.C. copper working were discovered at the site.

Zāwiyah (also Zawiyeh) is a site in the middle Euphrates region in what was part of the ancient kingdom of Suhu. The city was situated on the north bank of the Euphrates, a few kilometers west of Sur Jureh and Gleieh. Like many towns and cities of the ancient kingdom of Suhu, Zāwiyah is now underwater; the area was flooded after the construction of the Haditha dam. The city's ancient name is not known.

Zela (modern Zile, Turkey) was an important ancient city of north-central Asia Minor; Strabo claimed Semiramis had founded the site. Iulius Caesar prevailed in the Battle of Zela against Pharnaces II of The Kingdom of Pontus, coining the phrase "veni, vidi, vici".
The Khuriya Muriya Islands are a group of five islands in the Arabian Sea.

An ancient place in Van province, Turkey. It had a street grid, fortifications of mud brick on a polygonal masonry foundation, and at least 14 building inscriptions in Aramaic from the Parthian period. Professor Rafet Çavuşoğlu began a program of excavations in 2021, which appears to be unpublished as of October 2025.

An ancient settlement whose site is located in the Gaziantep province of modern Turkey, at the modern village of Belkis (a.k.a. Kavunlu). The majority of the ancient remains were covered by water in the year 2000, due to the construction of the Birecik Dam.

Ziwiye is the name of a modern village in northwestern Iran. It is associated with an archaeological site from the 7th century BCE that was looted in the mid-20th century and subsequently partially excavated in a more scientific manner. The site's ancient name is not known.
Mound and find place for cuneiform inscription.
A Minoan settlement dating from ca. 1800 BC onwards, Zominthos is well known for a large Minoan building discovered in 1982.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 51 F3 Zone
Zorats Karer is a prehistoric archaeological site of the Middle Bronze and Iron Ages located in Armenia. Over 200 rock-cut tombs and monuments have been discovered at the site.

A seventh century AD church built by Catholicos Nerses the Builder (AD 641-653) and located in the Armavir Province of Armenia. The ruins of the church were listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.
Late antique mud-brick church located at Ain el-Sabil in Egypt. The exact dating of the site is still unknown.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 'Evron
A pre-Roman site in the valley of the Sangro river that was later converted to a Christian church.
A church (but not a basilica) in Ravenna, Italy, established by Bishop Ecclesius in 526 AD and consecrated ca. 547 AD.
The so-called "Great Basilica" is an early Christian basilica dating to the sixth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 63 F3 [Hagios Prokopios]
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Chrononum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Iciacum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 *Manatum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Marciacum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 *Maroialum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H3 *Melita
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F1 *Novivicum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 *Pauliacum, Mon.
*Porsulae/Maximianopolis, also known as Mosynopolis, was a Byzantine town along the Via Egnatia in Thrace; its position is close to the modern Greek city of Komotini.
A monastery constructed in the fifth century CE on the site of a cave previously inhabited by St. Paul the Anchorite.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 *Randanum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F3 *Siroialum?

Extensive Roman ruins identified by Gsell (AAA, whence BAtlas) at a site he called Oum Sekak in the Hassenaoua district of modern Algeria. He estimates the site extent at 13 hectares and reports the remains of a church. An inscription that seems to provide the name of the "genius loci" (Genius Vanisnesi) was found in the vicinity. The site is not clearly visible in twenty-first century aerial/satellite imagery and most of the modern toponymy Gsell provides does not appear in the open-source, digital gazetteers and map tools. The site may now lie under modern development in the area.

name: Abbazia di San Giovanni in Venere

props.description: A Christian monastic complex that is located in the municipality of Fossacesia, Italy. The site occupies a hilltop at 107 m above sea level on the Adriatic Sea coast. The basilica dates originally from the thirteenth century.

name: Abbazia di San Vincenzo al Volturno

props.description: An historic Benedictine monastery located in the Province of Isernia, near the source of the river Volturno.
The church of San Claudio al Chienti is a Romanesque church building located in the municipality of Corridonia in the Chienti valley. It was founded in the eleventh century.
A multi-period hilltop settlement with evidence for ancient terrace construction that now supports an abbey of the Benedictine order.
A ruined Medieval Cistercian abbey founded in 1190.

An ancient station on the Roman road to Gades recorded in the Antonine Itinerary, ad Herculem must have been associated with the Herakleion on the southern end of the island of Cotinussa. Scholars differ on the precise location; their efforts are complicated by significant landscape change since antiquity.
A Classical-Hellenistic period settlement on/around the site of an earlier Mycenaean citadel on the island of Siphnos, now partially occupied by the church of Agios Andreas.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 inset Ag. Dimitrios
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Ag. Isidoros
The Sanctuary of Agios Lot is located at the southeastern end of the Dead Sea.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Ag. Mavri
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 C4 Ag. Nikitas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D2 Ag. Panagia
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Sophia
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Ag. Stephanos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Thomas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 E1 Ag. Trias
A sixteenth-century chapel in Athens built atop an ancient shrine of Herakles.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 Ain el Aouad
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Ain Nagila
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Akoimeton Mon.
A ruined and now largely dispersed fifth-century CE church on the outskirts of al-Bara, Syria.
Monastery founded by  Alexander the Acoemete in 782 CE on the left bank of the Euphrates, near the modern village of Halawa.
An ancient settlement and early Christian site, now occupied partly by an Armenian Apostolic monastery and its 18th century church.

The high altar of the present, Gothic-era Pfarrkirche (parish church) of modern Ampass (near Insbruck in Austria) was built over a series of earlier Christian churches, the earliest of which appears to date to the fifth century CE, based on excavated remains of a reliquary vault dating back to that period.
The Roman amphitheater of Durrës dates to the reign of Trajan. A small chapel built inside the amphitheater has mosaics likely dating to the 6th-8th centuries CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Apa Abraham, Mon.
A Coptic monastery founded ca. 385/390 located 80 km north of Asyut, near the village of Dashlout, Egypt.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Apa Apollo, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Apollo, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Apa Bane, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Apa Djoore, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Apa Epiphanius, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Apa Ioannes, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Makrobios, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Apa Palemon, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Apa Psoi, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F4 Apa Psote, Mon./Amba Bisada
The "White Monastery", located near modern Tahta and Sohag in Egypt.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E3 Apa Souroutos, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 B5 Apollo Asgelatos, T.
Two early Christian basilicae were superimposed over the ancient sanctuary of Apollo Dalios.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D2 Apostoli, Mon.
A pre-Constantinian Christian church located at ancient Aila in Jordan.

A mountain of the Argolid with a higher western peak (Profitis Ilias, 1199 masl) and a lower eastern peak (Mavrovouni or Arna, 1139 masl). Material from the Mycenaean, Archaic, and Classical periods has been found on the western peak and identified with altars of Zeus and Hera described by Pausanias. Aeschylus mentions its use as a signal station.

Badly damaged foundations of the peristyle of a large Doric temple of the 6th or early 5th c. BCE, the center of which is now occupied by the modern chapel of Profitis Ilias on a rise NE of ancient Kyreneia. This temple was probably destroyed during the earthquake of 373 BCE and replaced by a smaller temple to the west, built from its rubble. A monumental great altar has also been excavated immediately east of the temple.
A private, early Christian oratory in Ravenna, Italy, erected by Peter II as archbishop in A.D. 495.
Baptistry built by Theodoric the Great in the late fifth or early sixth century AD

An early medieval settlement in Spain (9th to 13th centuries CE) listed in contemporary Latin documents. This settlement is now identified with a second phase of occupation of an archaeological site known as El Forau de la Tuta near modern Arieda in Zaragosa province where a previously unknown Roman settlement has also been discovered. In addition to archaeological finds in situ, spolia from both the Roman and medieval phases have been identified in the more recent Ermita de San Pedro.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Asabaa

Ashqelon/Ascalon was an ancient city in coastal Palestine, located midway between Azotus and Gaza. It became independent in 104 B.C. and remained the only free city in Palestine. Strabo refers to it, while Pliny calls it a free city (oppidum libera).
The Aula Palatina is a Roman Imperial Basilica of the emperor Constantine (German: Konstantinbasilika) located at Trier, Germany. It was built ca. A.D. 310 as part of a Roman palace complex.
A ruined fifth-century CE basilica on the Karpass peninsula of Cyprus.
Village where fragments of a Urartian stele have been found (one in a church, one in a fortress).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Baldersdorf
A fortified Medieval site located in Puglia.
The Baptistery of Butrint is a round structure, with a diameter of 14.5 meters. It was built in the sixth century A.D. and was excavated in May 1928 by Luigi Maria Ugolini.

An octagonal brick structure erected by Bishop Ursus (late fourth or early fifth century AD) as part of a Basilica (destroyed in 1734). Bishop Neon completed the baptistry at the end of the fifth century AD.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Baqirha
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A2 Barlaamus
A large structure near the northeast corner of the Athenian Agora, built early in the second century AD, which may have been a basilica and perhaps served as a court.
An important civic building in Pompeii and an early example of the building type, built between ca. 120 and 78 B.C. (VIII, 1, 1).
A basilica located at the juncture of the Sacra Via and the Argiletum flanking the Forum Romanum that was initially built in 179 B.C.E.

name: Basilica and convent of S. Ippolito

props.description: An important paleochristian complex located in the area of Portus.
The remains of a Roman Basilica at Aspendos.
The Basilica at Saepinum is positioned at the intersection of the cardo and the decumanus.

name: Basilica Benedettina di S. Michele Arcangelo

props.description: An abbey church located in Capua on the western slopes of Monte Tifata.
The Casa Herrera Basilica is part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida". The paleochristian basilica was built in either the late fifth or early sixth century CE.

The Basilica Constantini, also referred to as Basilica Nova ("New Basilica"), Basilica of Maxentius or Basilica of Maxentius and Constantine, is a colossal fourth-century basilica located along the Sacra Via in Rome. It is considered the largest ancient structure in the valley of the Forum Romanum and is typically deemed the final Roman civic basilica built in the ancient city.
The basilica and oratory of Damasianus, as well as the martyr tombs of Simplicius, Faustinus, Beatrice, and Ruinianus.
A Christian basilica of the Byzantine period located in Carthage.

The Basílica de Santa Eulalia is a paleochristian site that is included as part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida". The early Christian features were superimposed upon previous, preexisting Roman features.
A Paleochristian site dating to the fifth century A.D.
Basílica des Fornàs de Torelló, a sixth-century CE paleochristian basilica with mosaic pavements located in Mahon, Minorca.

The ruins of a Christian basilica located near the Via Ardeatina on the grounds of the Calixtian complex have been connected with the Basilica of Saint Mark. The site was discovered accidentally in 1991 and is associated with the Catacomb of Balbina.

name: Basilica di Sant'Agnese fuori le mura

props.description: Pope Honorius I commissions the Basilica of Saint Agnes in the seventh century, replacing the earlier Constantinian structure.

The Basilica of Sant'Anastasia al Palatino was founded ca. 325 CE on the slopes of the Palatine. It is now a minor basilica of the Roman Catholic church. It is the national church for the Peruvian community of Rome and the Indian community of the Syro-Malabar rite. The original basilica was adjacent to the Circus Maximus and came to be dedicated to Saint Anastasia of Sirmium (281-304 CE.), a Roman woman martyred under Diocletian. In the acts of the synod of 499, the church is listed as the titulus Anastasiae.
The basilica of Santa Balbina is a paleo-Christian basilica in Rome, located on the "little Aventine".

The Basilica of Santa Francesca Romana, also known as Santa Maria Nova, dates originally from the ninth century when the basilica was erected on the site of a preexisting oratory during the papacy of Paul I.
A minor Paleochristian basilica located in Guidonia Montecelio that was dedicated to the patron saint of Tivoli.

name: Basilica di Santo Stefano in via Latina

props.description: The now-ruined basilica of Santo Stefano in Via Latina was a fifth-century CE early Christian basilica. It is now located within the confines of the archaeological park of the Via Latina tombs.
A basilica erected on the Caelian Hill in Rome under the patronage of Manius Publicus Hilarus and connected to the cult of Cybele and Attis.
The Basilica Julia was built on the south side of the Forum Romanum in 46 B.C. on the site of the former Basilica Sempronia.
An important extra-mural Christian site at Solina. The site includes the basilica and the cemetery of Manastirine, associated with a Christian martyr.
A building in the Campus Martius that originally dates to ca. 25 BCE.
The ruins of a three-aisled basilica of the sixth century CE located at Salamis in Cyprus.
The civic Basilica of Carsulae is located on the forum.

The presence of a basilica created with funds donated by Constantine I in region 5 of ancient Ostia was known from textual sources and then confirmed via geophysical prospection and, ultimately, excavation.
The Basilica of Cresconius is a church with a nave-and-side-aisle plan located at Cuicul (Djémila), Numidia, Algeria.
A basilica dating to the time of the Vandals.
The basilica of Iomnium (Tigzirt) likely dates to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.

The Basilica of Junius Bassus (basilica Iunii Bassi) was a civil basilica on the Esquiline Hill in Rome. Its remains were demolished in 1930 to make way for the Seminario Pontificio di Studi Orientali, in via Napoleone III, 3. Junius Bassus held the consulship in 331 CE. The basilica is particularly known for its opus sectile decorations. In the fifth century, the basilica was remodeled into the church of Sant'Andrea Catabarbara.
The basilica of Lechaion was dedicated to Leonides, the bishop of Athens. It is one of the most important early Byzantine monuments in the Peloponnese.
The Basilica of Lucus Feroniae communicates with the forum and is linked to the apsidal space identified as the Augusteum.

name: Basilica of Saint Augustine in Campo Marzio

props.description: The Basilica of Saint Augustine of Hippo in Camp Martius is a titular minor basilica of the Roman Catholic church located in the Campus Martius of Rome. The basilica was conceived in 1286 and the current basilica was completed in 1483.
A Christian basilica that incorporates the remains of a fourth-century AD Roman gymnasium.

name: Basilica of San Clemente al Laterano

props.description: The eleventh-century Basilica of San Clemente al Laterano is a Roman Catholic basilica dedicated to Clement I. It sits atop a three-tiered series of architectural remains stretching back to the Republican period. The earliest phase is represented by the foundations of a building destroyed by the fire of A.D. 64.
A basilica begun in the sixth century AD by Bishop Ursicinus with financial support from the Greek banker Iulianus Argentarius.

name: Basilica of Santa Maria in Trastevere

props.description: The Basilica di Santa Maria in Trastevere is a titular minor basilica located in the Trastevere district of Rome, Italy.

A palaeochristian church dedicated to St. Pudentiana located in Rome and, perhaps, the earliest site of Christian worship in the city. The church is located along the Via Urbana, which follows the course of the ancient vicus Patricius.
The Basilica of Septimius Severus belongs to the Severan Forum at Lepcis Magna.
The Basilica of St. Achillios at Larissa is an early Byzantine basilica dedicated to the city's patron saint, St. Achillios. The site was discovered and excavated in 1978.
The fourth-century Basilica of St. Crispinus is one of the largest known in Africa.

name: Basilica of St. Francis of Assisi

props.description: A papal basilica begun ca. AD 1228.
The Basilica of St. John the Evangelist is located in Ephesus on the Ayasuluk Hill. It was built between 548 and 565 CE.
A Christian basilica of the mid-sixth to seventh centuries.
12th-century church in Paray-le-Monial, France. This site was a popular pilgrimage site and is an important example of Cluniac architecture.
A fifth or sixth century AD (Early Christian) triple-aisled basilica including mosaic floors.
A civil basilica built in the Forum Romanum ca. 121 BCE by Lucius Opimius. This basilica was likely razed in 7 BCE.

The Basilica Porcia is considered the first named civil basilica in Rome. The elder Cato is responsible for its construction in 184 BCE. It seems to have been a small structure that took the place of several earlier private atria located between the Clivus Argentarius and the Curia Hostilia. The Basilica Porcia was destroyed by fire in 52 BCE.

The Basilica Ulpia is located in the Forum of Trajan at Rome and derives its name from the emperor Trajan's gentilician nomenclature. The basilica was considered a marvel of ancient Roman architecture by observers, including the historian Ammianus Marcellinus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Bau/Pboou
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Bell-Loc d’Aro
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 E3 Belmonte
A "fortified basilica" in Cyrenaica, probably dating to the 6th century CE and associated with nearby farmsteads and tombs, the latter dating to the Roman period, but reused in late antiquity.
Bethabara, in modern Jordan, is an ancient site connected with John the Baptist. Bethabara is also attested on the sixth century A.D. "Madaba Map".
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Biclaro?
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 C2 Binbirkilise
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Bir Abu Swera
An Early Christian pilgrimage complex and baptistry at Carthage.
The Bishop Genesius Church at Jerash (Jordan) may be the earliest Christian church on the site. The church was built in the early seventh century, reusing a preexisting Roman temple.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 A4 Bıçkıcı Kalesi
A fourteenth century monastery.

This site, discussed in scholarly literature in the 20th century, seems from satellite imagery to have been impinged or destroyed in the construction of the Rocade Tipaza/Autoroute Tipaza - Cherchel in the early years of the 21st century. It seems to have been a villa with a central courtyard and primary entrance facing east, with a row of rooms with arched openings overlooking the wadi to the north. Vats and presses, possibly for processing grapes for wine, were discovered. Anselmino hypothesized that another, unexcavated structure immediately to the east was a church.
The Brampton Old Church stands on the site of a Roman fort located 13 km northeast of Carlisle and 0.8 km south of the Stanegate.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Breviglieri
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D1 Britaniensis/Britonensis Ecclesia
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 H4 Brittablo
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 Cantobennum, Mon.
A twelfth-century chapel located near the Castrum Perticae. The site is located in the municipal territory of modern Finale Ligure in Italy's Ligurian Alps.
Situated along the via Severiana are the remains of an anonymous Paleochristian basilica that was in use between the second and the seventh centuries AD.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 Castellnou de Basella
A priory founded in 1089 by William de Warenne and consecrated sometime between 1146 and 1148. Home of the first Cluniac order in England.
A Medieval fortification that incorporates and repurposes the Roman mausoleum of Caecilia Metella.
The Cathedral of Santa Maria Assunta on the island of Torcello is a Roman Catholic basilica initially founded in 639. The second basilica was begun in 1008.
A 12th-century Armenian church in Jerusalem, the principal church of the Armenian Patriarchate of Jerusalem.

name: Cathedral of the Holy Cross at Aghtamar

props.description: Armenian church built on the island of Aghtamar by King Gagik I Artsruni between 915-921 CE.
Byzantine church remains at Çatıören in Rough Cilicia

name: Cattedrale di Santa Maria di Amiternum

props.description: The ruins of the cathedral of the fifth century are located in an area of the archaeological site of Amiternum that was occupied in the second and third centuries CE.
A cave in the outskirts of Antioch that was converted into a church.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Chafagi Aamer

Theopompus mentions Chalia as a town of Boeotia. It is also attested epigraphically and may have been a member of the Boeotian league. Fossey locates the city in the vicinity of the church of Agios Minas in modern Drosia. Archaeological finds in the area indicate human activity from as early as the Bronze Age.
The chapelle Sainte-Agathe was originally a Roman-period thermal complex that was converted to a Christian church by the seventh century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Chariton

name: Chiesa di San Paolo di Peltuinum

props.description: The church of San Paolo at Peltuinum likely dates to the seventh or eighth century CE.

name: Chiesa di Sant'Ilario a Port' Aurea

props.description: A sixth-, seventh-, and eighth-century Christian church located near the Port'Aurea of Benevento was built during the Longobard period. The church was built atop Imperial remains and was an apsidal hall.
The church of Santa Pudenziana near Narni, Italy, dates from the ninth to eleventh centuries CE and reuses spoliated architectural elements from a Roman villa.

A poorly understood paleochristian site located on the grounds of the Inviolata park in the comune of Guidonia-Montecelio in the greater metropolitan area of Rome. The cave site may originally have functioned as a mithraeum.

The so-called Christian Basilica, located in Regio III of ancient Ostia, was discovered by Guido Calza in 1939. The structure likely dates to the fourth and fifth centuries CE. The interpretation of the structure and its remains has proven difficult, resulting in numerous theories that are often at odds with one another. One possibility is that the complex functioned as a xenodochium (a guest house for pilgrims).
Church adapted from a private house building in Dura-Europos.

name: Church and early Christian necropolis at Vaste

props.description: An early Christian complex that included a cult building with several construction phases and a vast cemetery.
The church, well visible in Google Earth, is in the SW sector of the archaeological area
A structure at the city of Trimithis (Amheida) in the Dakhleh Oasis, identified as a church and dated to the 4th century CE.

Ruins of a church likely built around the fifth century AD, on the summit of the low hill of Sidi Khiribech in Berenike. The character of the building and its position has suggested to some archaeologists a defensive as well as a religious purpose.
7th-century Armenian church in the Kars region of Turkey. This church is now ruined, but still is the second-largest domed basilica from the 7th-century Caucasus still extant.
An eleventh-century A.D. Byzantine church in Thessaloniki.
A fourteenth-century AD church in Thessaloniki.

name: Church of Saint Alexander Nevsky, Jerusalem

props.description: The nineteenth century Russian Orthodox church preserves within it remains of Constantine's original fourth century church of the Holy Sepulchre.
A late Byzantine church in Thessalonica.
A seventh century AD church dedicated to the patron saint of Thessalonica; the present basilica stands on the site of a precursor with phases beginning in the fourth century AD.
A paleochristian sanctuary located along the Via Francigena.

name: Church of Saint Gregory of Tigran

props.description: Armenian church in Ani, in modern Eastern Turkey, completed in 1215.

name: Church of Saint Martin at Moissac

props.description: A Christian church built, at least in part, atop the ruins of a Gallo-Roman building that include a hypocaust.
The Church of Saint Panteleimon (Thessaloniki) is a late Byzantine church.
The Church of Saint Sophia, also referred to as the Old Bishopric, is an Eastern Orthodox church located in Nesebar, eastern Bulgaria. The church originally dates to the sixth century.
The so-called Church of San Primitivo in Monte Compatri. The church's bell tower was converted to a defensive tower around 1200.
The Church of Santa Fosca on the island of Torcello is a Romanesque church dating initially to the ninth century CE.

A church in Athens, excavated and demolished between 1859 and 1861, which was built on top of a section of the post-Herulian wall incorporating several hundred inscriptions and sculptures, mostly from the Diogeneion gymnasium.

name: Church of St. Etchmiadzin at Soradir

props.description: Sixth-century Armenian church in the town of Soradir (modern Yanal, Turkey).
One of a group of eleven rock-hewn monolithic churches located in Lalibela, a city in the Amhara Region of Ethiopia. The church likely dates to the twelfth or thirteenth century.
A Paleochristian sanctuary in Portugal.
A fourteenth-century A.D. church in Thessalonica.
A twelfth-century AD Christian church was built atop the remains of the Roman temple of Apollo.
A fifth century A.D. Byzantine church.

The church of the Annunziatella is a Catholic church located in Rome's Ardeatino district. It is situated between the via Ardeatina and via di Grotta Perfetta. The church was established in the thirteenth century, as an inscription of Pope Honorius III attests.

name: Church of the Archangels at Sige

props.description: Church in the village of Sige (modern Kumyaka) on the southern shore of the Sea of Marmara. Constructed under Constantine VI in 780, the structure was restored twice, in 1448 and 1819. It remained in continuous use until it was ultimately abandoned in 1922.
A fourteenth century church at Thessalonica.
17th-century church behind the Monastery of Saint John the Theologian in Patmos.

name: Church of the Holy Apostles at Constantinople

props.description: Construction of a church dedicated to the Twelve Apostles at Constantinople began in 330 CE under Constantine the Great, and was completed during the reign of his son Constantius II (331-367). A larger church was established on the same site in 550 by Justinian I. The church was demolished by Mehmed II in 1462, and the Fatih Mosque was constructed on its site.

A church that many claim marks the birthplace of Jesus Christ is located in Bethlehem, now in the State of Palestine in the West Bank. The original church built on the site was commissioned by Constantine I and dedicated in May 339 CE. The church was destroyed by fire, likely in 529, and replaced by Justinian I.
The Church of the Saviour, Thessaloniki, is a fourteenth-century Byzantine church.

The Civil Basilica at Aphrodisias was a three-aisled public hall measuring approximately 145 by 30 meters. It opened off the southwest corner of the South Agora. It dates to the late first century CE.

Civita di Bagno is the modern name for a small settlement that dates back at least to the Roman Late Republican period. Within the modern town, several key monuments are known, such as a podium temple (late 2nd c. BCE?), identified as that of Feronia by an inscription from the reign of Tiberius, a bath complex beneath Palazzo Oliva, and the medieval remains of the cathedral of San Massimo which reuses much ancient material, as well as funerary remains along the ancient road network. On the adjacent Moritola hill is a vast system of terracing, which in its earliest phase dates to the 2nd c. BCE, that supported a monumental complex of difficult interpretation, but with an important hydraulic infrastructure. This complex underwent multiple modifications over the centuries; in the 5th c. CE the complex was transformed by the installation of two kilns and a limekiln. By the 6th c. CE Civita di Bagno was the seat of a bishop.
Col. Ruscino was an Iberian oppidum and town of Narbonese Gaul.
The abbey was first founded in 815 by Charlemagne's cousins, Wala and Adelard.
A Roman mansio on the via Iulia Augusta. An Early Christian complex (basilica, baptistery, cemetery) developed nearby and lasted until the Middle Ages.

name: Crypt and church of San Lussorio

props.description: The Romanesque church of San Lussorio dates to the early twelfth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Cyriacus, Mon.

The current Daphni Monastery in Attica dates to the eleventh century, yet its earlier Christian phases belong to the sixth century AD when a Christian site grew atop the abandoned and destroyed sanctuary of Apollo Daphnaios.
A Coptic Orthodox monastery located in the Wadi El Natrun northwest of Cairo.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Abu Fam
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Abu Harmina
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Abu Hennes
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F4 Deir Abu Masis
Late antique settlement, possibly monastic, with substantial remains of a church, mud-brick structures, and tombs.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Am-Nasara
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 C4 Deir Amba Baramos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 74 C4 Deir Amba Bishoi
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Anba Bakhum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Anba Pschoi/Deir Anba Bishai
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Antush
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Deir Dronkeh
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G1 Deir el Qal'a
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir el-Adra
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Deir el-Bakhit
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir el-Dik
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D2 Deir el-Kosseir
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 G4 Deir el-Malek
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Deir el-Muttin
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 A3 Deir el-Qattal el-Bizanti
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir el-Shuhada
A monastery located in the modern Wadi El Natrun in Egypt, likely founded in the 6th century CE. The ancient church and some of its early decorative program survive.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Deir Madoud
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Deir Magara
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Rumhan
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Deir Sombat
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Umm Arad
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Deir Umm Butme
An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of a monastic chapel. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."
A Christian basilica of Byzantine Carthage.

The house of Lucius Fabius Cilo, praefectus urbi in 203, consul in 193 and 204, and a close friend of the emperor Septimius Severus. The house was a gift of the Severans and appears on the Severan marble plan. Its site is now occupied by the basilica of Santa Balbina on the "little Aventine".
Doss Trento is a small hill that lies along the course of the Adige River. A Paleochristian basilica was located on the hillside.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 G2 Dranda
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 ed-Deir
A 12th-century CE church thought to have been built on the site of a Gallo-Roman villa. It is located in the modern French commune of Marimbault (Gironde).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 El Bovalar
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 el-Arba'in
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 el-Fra'iyeh
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 el-Khirbet
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 el-Ma'yen
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D3 Epaone

name: Episcopal complex of Eio-el Tolmo de Minateda (Héllín, Albacete, Spain)

props.description: The episcopal complex of Eio at El Tolmo de Minateda dates to the terminal sixth century CE and was in use during the following two centuries. Its construction may have been directed by Visigothic officials in Toletum.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Erebe

The cavern of San Michele was a hermit's dwelling place that is located near a cavern known as the "cave of San Colombano". This hermitage lies in a relatively solitary area of the valley of the Curiasca di San Michele stream, a tributary of the Trebbia river (Italian municipality of Coli, province of Piacenza).
A rock-cut Christian sanctuary located on Monte Altino in the Monti Aurunci that was begun ca. A.D. 830.

A fourth century A.D. church located in Ejmiatsin, Armenia. This is the central cathedral of the Mother See of Holy Etchmiadzin of the Armenian Apostolic Church and was enrolled as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.
An Early Byzantine Christian basilica located in Poreč, Croatia.

The cathedral in Faras, founded in 620 and rebuilt in the 8th and 10th centuries, respectively, was the seat of the Diocese of Faras. The site had well-preserved wall paintings from the 8th to 9th centuries. The cathedral was flooded by the creation of Lake Nasser in the 1960s.
A Benedictine abbey located ca. 60 km from Rome.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 Farsh el-Qasr
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D5 Farsh Habash
The forum-basilica at Venta date to the first and second centuries A.D. and are of the so-called "principia" type. The basilica measures ca. 54.6 x 20.1 m and was of the Corinthian order.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C4 Forum Cassii

A vicus of the Vestini located in the upper Aterno valley in Abruzzo. The ancient settlement was located some 15 km south of modern L'Aquila, in the vicinity of S. Maria di Forfona near Barisciano. Furfo lay on the Via Claudia Nova between Amiternum and Peltuinum.
An early Christian church in Ireland, originally built between the sixth and the ninth centuries.

A Gallo-Roman structure including hypocaust and other remains of a bath, located beneath the later Church of Saint Martin in modern Moissac, France. The bath may have belonged to a villa of the fourth-fifth centuries CE.
13th century Armenian cathedral located near the village of Vank in the disputed region of Nagorno-Karabakh/Artsakh.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 F1 Gantiadi
One of two mountains in the immediate vicinity of the West Bank city of Nablus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 B1 Gasr el-Gatres

Gasr Silu, Libya, is an ancient site documented by the British School at Rome under John Bryan Ward-Perkins in 1969. Field study was conducted by Sheila Gibson and Margaret Browne. Photographs in the BSR archive document architectural remains at the site that include productive installations and the remains of two Byzantine churches. A Byzantine fort (gasr) was also identified.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 13 (Gasr Stablous)

name: Gastaldaga area and the Episcopal complex

props.description: A Longobard complex in Cividale del Friuli.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D4 Gebel Tafnis
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D2 Gebel Teir
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 H2 Gerasimos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D4 Germeshin
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 E4 Geth Rabbi
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D2 Glak, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B6 Golyamo Belovo
The Great Church of Hippo Regius is located in the Christian quarter of Hippo. The church is associated with Saint Augustine, who was bishop in Hippo from 395 until he died in 430 CE.
A major paleochristian, then Byzantine, church in Istanbul; subsequently an Islamic mosque, and today a museum.

Hagia Sophia, Iznik, Turkey, was founded as a Christian basilica in the fourth century CE. Justinian I rebuilt the site in the mid-6th century CE. An earthquake in the eleventh century destroyed that church, and it was then substantially rebuilt following 1065. It is to that rebuilding that much of the present structure pertains. Following the Ottoman conquest, the church became a mosque in 1331. The site served that function until ca. 1935, when it became a museum. Following 2011, it again served as a mosque.
A church to Saint Sophia dating originally from the third century A.D.
Hasanaliler Church is a 6th-century Byzantine church.
The temple of Hephaistos located on the northwest side of the Agora of Athens dates to the middle of the fifth century B.C.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Horeb Mons, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B3 Horvat 'Erav
A tenth-century walled monastery in Boeotia, Greece.
Hyrkania was an ancient fortress in the Judaean Dessert that was rebuilt as the Byzantine monastery of Kastellion (ca. A.D 492).
The church and convent "I Cappuccini" in Frascati was the site of a Roman villa.
An early Christian basilica on the Ilissos River in Athens that may date to the fifth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Ilıca
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 C2 Imst
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Iohannes Urtius, Mon.
A church and monastery in Tao-Klarjeti, first constructed in the 7th century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B5/ inset Isieiou Oros
An island in the Ligurian Sea.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C4 Itea
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 F3 Itos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Jebel Serbal
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 E4 Kabaköy

An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of Roman praetorium, a temple and grottoes for pagan worship, a Byzantine basilica, and a large communal reservoir. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B1 Kalabaka

Kalecik (formerly known as Aralesk or Lesk) is a modern village situated ca. 5 km north of Van Kalesi in southeast Turkey. In the village church, a re-used cylindrical stone (presumably a column base) with a building inscription of the Urartian king Išpuini, son of Sarduri I (ninth century BCE), was found. Furthermore, a re-used stone slab with an inscription of the Urartian king Argišti, son of Minua and grandson of Išpuini (eighth century BCE) has also been found here.

An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of an ancient church and residential buildings. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."

An archaeological site located 4.5km west southwest of modern Dolichi in Greece's Larisa regional unit (Thessaly). Here, a proto-byzantine walled settlement with three basilicas was built partly with spolia from a temple of Herakles and the ancient city of Doliche. On this basis, the excavators have argued for identification of the archaeology with the attested name; however, the lack so far of in-situ evidence predating the 6th century CE has led other scholars to continue to identify the original Classical-Roman polis of Doliche with the archaeological site at Sarandoporo.

Abundant spolia, artifacts, and building foundations provide evidence for an ancient settlement in the area of modern Kastro ano Diakopto and the church of Ag. Athanasios on the hill above it to the west. It is probably to be identified with ancient Boura, an attested settlement of Achaia.

On the small island of modern Kephalos in the Ambracian Gulf, archaeologists discovered a settlement, a pair of Christian basilicas, and other associated structures and small finds. The site may have begun as a refuge settlement in the late fifth / early sixth century CE and seems to have continued in use, possibly with a late monastic function, into the 10th century.

A rural location now in the modern Algerian municipal territory of Djouab (Médéa province) where Gsell and his correspondents (AAA, whence BAtlas) observed Roman ruins including a large building with columns that G. speculated might have been a church.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G3 Khirbet el Quneitira
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 Khirbet Jedora
A Byzantine church located within the fortified acropolis of Aperlae.
A cluster of Neolithic and Bronze Age earthworks with a twelfth-century Norman church built in the center.
Modern name for ruins of a Byzantine church and surrounding village in eastern Rough Cilicia
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Kosmas kai Damianos, Mon.
A late-antique rural church now lacking decoration located about 6km north of the modern village of Tozeur in Tunisia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D3 Kraneia?
An Early Christian Basilica located near the grove of Kraneion that dates to the sixth century CE.
An early Christian basilica was excavated at Kursi, near the Sea of Galilee in the Golan Heights.

name: La Temple de la Madeleine, Geneva

props.description: The oldest Christian church in the city of Geneva, the Temple de la Madeleine site has been in continuous use as a church since the 5th century. The current structure on the site dates to the 15th century.
Labraunda is an archaeological site located in Caria, just west of Ortaköy, Turkey. The site was sacred to both Carians and Mysians.

name: Lateran Basilica, Palace, and Castra Nova equitum singularium

props.description: Site of a basilica and palace from early Roman imperial times, as well as an military installation built by Septimius Severus for the Roman imperial cavalry bodyguard. Now the site of the Archbasilica of St. John Lateran (rebuilt in the 16th century): Complesso dei San Giovanni in Laterano (Basilica, Palazzo Apostolico Lateranense, edifici annessi) Holy See; listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1990.
A late fifth century AD church, later associated with a small monastery in Thessaloniki, Greece.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Laubendorf

An ancient site located on the northern plateau of the Wadi Mujib in modern Jordan. Under excavation since the 1970s, the site has so far provided evidence for human activity from the prehistoric period through the Islamic.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B3 Limani Ozia
former Greek Eastern Orthodox church in Istanbul converted to a mosque

The Little Metropolis church or Panagia Gorgoepikoos is a Frankish, Late Byzantine, or Ottoman-period domed cross-in-square church in central Athens. It is decorated with spolia from earlier structures.
A Benedictine abbey that is located 7 km south of Poitiers and was founded by Martin from 361 to 371.

A civic basilica in ancient Londonium, the remains of which the Museum of London archaeology service (MOLA) has documented at a site located at 85 Gracechurch Street, London. The site is dated ca. 70 CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C3 Loutra Hypatis
Church dating to the 7th century CE in Nubia that was succeeded by St. Raphael Church II. The site is located in the modern Sudanese village of Banganarti.
Located in France's Burgundy region, the Luxeuil Abbey was founded ca. 585–590 by the Irish missionary Saint Columbanus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Lysi
An ancient city in Aetolia, located 3km south of the modern city that now bears its name.
Madonna del Parto is a Christian church at Sutri, dating to the thirteenth and fourteenth centuries. The rock-cut sanctuary repurposes a Mithraeum dating to the first and second centuries.

The sanctuary site of Madonnna del Canneto is located at Settefrati within the confines of the Abruzzo national park in Italy. It has a long history as a sacred site, beginning with sacred activities dedicated to Mefitis in the later first millennium BCE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C3 Magnetum?
Medieval Armenian church and monastic complex dated to the 9th-13th centuries.
An Early Christian basilica and martyrium at Halmyris.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 C3 Mar Abay
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Abrohom
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Awgin
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 F4 Mar Matta
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Melki
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 H2 Mar Yaqub
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 D3 Mar Yohannon
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F4 Marcianum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 G4 Marcianus Fundus
Marialba Paleochristian Basilica dates to the fourth and fifth centuries.
The 12th-century Eglise Saint-Vincent de Marimbault was built atop a probable Gallo-Roman villa.

The Roman Tetrakionion at Aphrodisias has been identified as a Martyrion. Its date of construction is uncertain, ranging from as early as the late second century CE to as late as the 5th-6th century CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Martyrios
A mausoleum built to entomb Diocletian at his retirement palace that was constructed around 295 CE and is located in modern day Split, Croatia.

An ancient funerary chapel, part of the Santa Croce complex in Ravenna, Italy, possibly built as an imperial mausoleum (ca. AD 417 - 425). The empress Galla Placidia (d. AD 450 ) is the presumed, likely patron of the building, though she is very unlikely to have been entombed within one of three sarcophagi found inside.

A monument built in Ravenna, Italy, ca. AD 520 by Theodoric the Great as his future tomb. After Belisarius reconquered Ravenna in 540, the tomb was converted into the church of Sta. Maria della Rotonda.
An Eastern Orthodox Christian monastery overlooking the Kidron Valley located between the Old City of Jerusalem and the Dead Sea.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C3 Meroth

An extensive ancient sanctuary complex on the island of Lesbos, thought to have been dedicated to Zeus, Hera and Dionysos and dating back to at least the 7th century BCE. A Christian basilica was built on the site in the late fourth century CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Metanoia Mon.

According to Polybius, this ancient settlement of Aetolia was razed by Philip of Macedon. Stephanus of Byzantium places Metapa in Acarnania. Pritchett (SAGT) meticulously describes the vicinity and identifies the site of Metapa based on ancient stone blocks in conditions of reuse and abandonment, as well as plentiful ceramic sherds in the vicinity of an older and modern church of the Analipsis on the lake shore just east of the modern village of Daphnias.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Molzbichl

name: Monastery of Saint John the Theologian

props.description: The Monastery of Saint John the Theologian (also known as the Monastery of Saint John the Divine) is a Greek Orthodox monastery dating to AD 1088 on the island of Patmos.

The monastery of Saint Simeon Stylites, located ca. 30 km northwest of Aleppo, Syria, is one of the oldest surviving Byzantine churches and dates to the fifth century. The church is built on the site of the pillar of St. Simeon Stylites.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F3 Monte Monaco di Gioia
The remains of a villa were excavated near the church of San Pietro ad centum muros in the nineteenth century. Based on epigraphic evidence, the site is connected with M. Vipsanius Agrippa.
The eleventh century monastery of Santa Maria di Monteverde sits atop and reuses elements of Roman and pre-Roman settlements on the hilltop.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Morulls

Excavations at Tell Museifneh (Eski Mosul) in the 1980s revealed remains of a well-built stone building that was identified as a Christian basilica dating to the seventh century A.D. The apisdal basilica measured ca. 50 x 50 meters.
The remains of a well-preserved Byzantine church (ca. 470) located five km west of the Monastery of St Simeon. The ruins lie near the town of Daret A'zzeh.
The remains of an Early Christian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries.
A rock-cut church located about 5km west of the modern town of Omar Mukhtar in Libya.
An eleventh-century monastery on Chios built by Constantine IX Monomachos. The site was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1990.

Site of a modern church and monastery atop Mount Nebo in modern Jordan on a site commemorated by earlier churches built on a site traditionally held to be the burial location of the prophet Moses. The first ancient church here is mentioned earliest in the itinerary of Egeria.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D2 Neklone
An Early Christian basilica located southeast of ancient Nemea.

Nessana is an ancient site located in the Negev Desert. Established during the Hellenistic period, Nessana has numerous occupational phases including as the site of a Roman military garrison in the fourth century CE. The site flourishes in connection with the Christian pilgrimage route linking the Holy Land to Mount Sinai (sixth and seventh centuries CE).
A basilica in Ravenna, Italy, erected by Theodoric the Great in the first quarter of the sixth century AD.

A monument to the Battle of Marathon. Eugene Vanderpool describes the location of the monument as lying in the northeastern part of the plain of Marathon. A Christian chapel known as the Panagia Mesosporitissa marks the vicinity of this monument. In the area of the chapel are a well that reuses ancient marble and the ruins of a medieval tower that was built from re-used ancient blocks, including a massive Ionic column capital and two large column drums.
The Northern Cluster of rock-cut churches at Lalibela.

Nostra Segnora de Mesumundu ("Our Lady of Mesumundu") is located in the territory of Siligo, Sardinia, Italy. This archaeological site preserves remains of multiple architectural phases. A mid-Imperial (likely second-century CE) Roman complex, which included baths, was reused, in part, for the construction of a Christian church in the sixth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Oberlienz
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Onia
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Orbaniacum
Modern name for ruins of a Byzantine church and surrounding rural settlement in eastern Rough Cilicia

The ancient synagogue located in Ostia Antica is one of the oldest known synagogues in the world. The structure's earliest phase dates to the reign of Claudius and it continued in use until the fifth century.
A fortified port located on the Gulf of Corinth near Alepokhori.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D2 Palaiomonastero

A Paleochristian basilica was discovered at the eighth mile of the Via Tiburtina in 2003. The subterranean basilica reused an earlier quarry and may be related to San Sinforosa. The site dates from the late fourth to the middle of the fifth century CE.
The Paleochristian Basilica in the Villa Gordiani Park is located adjacent to the mausoleum and immediately post-dates the tomb.
An early Christian basilica located west of ancient Brauron in Attica.
The paleochristian basilica of Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges.
The Paleochristian basilica located on Illa del Rei dates to the sixth century.
A paleochristian church located in Lin, Korçë County, Albania, with fourth- and fifth-century mosaics.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 H2 Pampligua

The church of Panagia tis Angeloktistis or Panagia Angeloktisti is a Byzantine church located in the village of Kiti, Cyprus. Its origins lies in the fifth and sixth centuries. It was listed as a tentative UNESCO World Heritage site in 2015.
Panagia Ekatontapiliani or Panagia Katapoliani is an important Early Christian church complex in Parikia town, on the island of Paros, Greece, that was founded in 326 CE.

A twelfth-century CE Greek Orthodox church located north of modern Livadia on Cyprus' Karpasia peninsula. Its apse had been adapted from part of an older building and contained a 7th-century mosaic of the Theotokos (i.e., Mary, mother of Jesus).

A temple in the Campus Martius of Rome that was first built by Marcus Agrippa (ca. 27-25 B.C.) and then re-built by the emperor Hadrian (after A.D. 126). In A.D. 609 the structure was consecrated as a Christian church, S. Maria ad Martyres.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 E2 Paphor, Mon.

name: Parco delle Tombe di via Latina

props.description: A cluster of Roman Imperial tombs along the Via Latina excavated in the nineteenth century.

name: Patriarchal Basilica of Santa Maria Assunta of Aquileia

props.description: The Basilica di Santa Maria Assunta, Aquileia dates originally to the fourth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Patriasdorf
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B6 Peroushtitsa
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Petrera
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Petrera
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D2 Pfaffenhofen

An ancient settlement not far from Piazza Armerina, in the municipality of Mazzarino. Archaeological finds suggest it was inhabited from the early Roman Imperial period to the third century A.D., when it was abandoned. The site was then re-occupied during the Middle Ages.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 64 B3 Phlabianai
A large Christian basilica discovered in 1976 several hundred meters southeast of the Porta Laurentina of Ostia. The original phase dates to the end of the fourth century A.D.

name: Pieve di San Giovanni Battista a Sillano

props.description: A ruined church that was once part of the diocese of Volterra, San Giovanni Battista a Sillano is unique in Tuscany for having a Romanesque-Norman façade. The ruined edifice likely dates to the twelfth and thirteenth centuries although the parish exists as early as the mid-tenth century.

name: Pieve di San Pietro ad Mensulas

props.description: A Romanesque church located in the Italian comune of Sinalunga (Siena). Saint Donatus of Arezzo may have founded the church in the fourth century.
Pieve di Sant'Antonino a Socana, a Medieval parish church, is built atop the remains of an Etruscan sanctuary.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B6 Pirdop
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F4 Pompeiacum/Vernemetis

The so-called underground basilica near Rome's Porta Maggiore is situated 12 meters below ground level. This vaulted space with a triple nave design is decorated with an elaborate stucco work program. The "basilica" was discovered accidentally in 1917. The site has been dated to the first century BCE and is likely linked to the activities of the neo-Pythagoreans.
An ancient settlement on the island of Siphnos. The current church sits on Byzantine foundations and is surrounded by remains of older fortifications and other structures.
Church building in the modern village of Ptghni, Kotayk province, Armenia, dated to the 6th-7th centuries CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D3 Ptoou Nkalamon

name: Qal’at Sem’an/Church of Saint Simeon Stylites

props.description: An ancient church, located in modern Syria. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B4 Qalat el-Baben
An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of an ancient basilica. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 D4 Qalbloze
At Qasr Libya, the ancient Theodorias, two churhes have been identified. The Byzantine settlement was refounded under Justinian in 539 CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E4 Qasr Serij
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 G3 Qenneshre
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 B3 Ras Abu el Daraq

Reccopolis is an urban, Visigothic, ex-novo foundation made under king Liuvigild in A.D. 578. The archaeological remains at the site include a palace complex, public area, basilica, walls, and aqueduct. The site is known today by the Spanish version of its Latin name (Recópolis); it is located near the modern village of Zorita de los Canes in the Spanish province of Guadelajara.

A Roman temple built above the Selinus river was originally constructed to worship Egyptian gods, most likely Isis and/or Serapis. In the Byzantine era, it was converted into a church of St. John. It was subsequently ruined, although the rotunda on the north side is still an active mosque.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Rephidim
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 D2 Riva S. Vitale
The Roman forum and civic basilica at Iulium Carnicum (modern Zuglio)

An ancient place, located in modern Syria, with remains of an ancient basilica, residential buildings, and several tombs. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011 as part of the "Ancient Villages of Northern Syria."

S. Agapito, the Palestrina Cathedral also known as the Cattedrale di Sant'Agapito martire, was built atop the ruins of Praeneste's civic forum and its temple. The relics of Saint Agapitus were transferred to this location in 898. The present basilica was consecrated on 16 December 1117 by Pope Pasquale I.

A titular church on the Via Nomentana, S. Agnese houses the remains of the eponymous saint. The present church was built by Pope Honorius I in the seventh century AD, on the site of a fourth century basilica built by Constantine I.
The basilica and catacombs of S. Alessandro are located at the seventh milestone of the Via Nomentana.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 F3 S. Autonomos, Mon.
A monastery originally established by St. Benedict of Nursia in A.D. 529/530.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 E2 S. Clemens
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 B1 S. Cornelia, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 S. Cugat
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 B3 S. Desideratus, Mon.
S. Giovanni Lippioni is the site of a pre-Roman sanctuary that was later repurposed as a Christian church.

The Papal Basilica of Saint Lawrence outside the Walls (Basilica Papale di San Lorenzo fuori le Mura) is one of the Seven Pilgrim Churches of Rome. The basilica serves as the shrine and tomb dedicated to Saint Lawrence and its earliest phases likely belong to the fourth century AD.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 S. Maria d’Artès
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 B1 S. Maria di Galeria
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 H2 S. Maria di Minerbe
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 S. Martinus
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 B3 S. Pantaleón

S. Passera is a Christian church on the Tiber river in Rome, opposite the Basilica of Saint Paul Outside the Walls. The current church dates to the ninth century and incorporated the remains of a Roman tomb. The church is traditionally connected to a community of local miners.

One of Rome's four major papal basilicae, S. Paulus was founded by Constantine I in the early fourth century A.D. and expanded by Valentinian I in the 370s. The basilica, with many renovations, repairs, and improvements, stood until the 19th century, when it was destroyed in a fire. The reconstructed basilica only generally resembles the original structure.

The Basilica of St. Peter in Vatican City is currently a Renaissance edifice standing on the site of Old St. Peter's Basilica, a church built by Constantine I between AD 319 and 333. The holy site is located on the Mons Vaticanus in connection with the purported site of the burial of the Christian Saint Peter.

On the hill of Grignano, located on the outskirts of Montebuono, the remains of a Roman villa were first excavated in the nineteenth century in the area of the church of "S. Pietro ad centum muros". Excavation in the 1990s revealed further villa remains beneath the church and in the vicinity of its cemetery. Epigraphic evidence attesting to the consulship of M. Vipsanius Agrippa was discovered immured in the church (CIL 9.4677 and 9.4779).
The oldest Orthodox church in Gaza, sacred to the fifth century Saint Porphyrius whose tomb lies within the church.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I3 S. Quiricus, Mon.
A convent founded within the area of the Villa Neronis.
S. Sebastianus (San Sebastiano fuori le mura) is a Christian basilica outside the walls of Rome; the earliest phase belongs to the fourth century AD.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 S. Sinforosa
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 D4 S. Thekla, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 S. Theodora
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F3 S. Victoriano
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 C5 S. Zion, Mon./Pharroa
A seventeenth century Benedictine nunnery located on a site that shows occupational evidence stretching back to the Neolithic period and that includes substantial Roman and Late Antique phases.

The Monastery of Saint Benedict or Monastero di San Benedetto Sacro Speco. The Shrine of the Sacred Cave of St. Benedict (Sacro Speco di San Benedetto) at Subiaco is a significant monastic shrine connected to the Roman Catholic saint Benedict. At the site is found a fresco-adorned cave linked to the saint, dating to the eighth century.
A seventh century AD church located in Vagharshapat (Etchmiadzin), Armenia and built by Catholicos Ezra I. The church was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.

A fourth-century AD church was rebuilt in the seventh-century AD to house the remains of the eponymous saint. The church is located in the city of Vagharshapat (Etchmiadzin), in the Armavir Province of Armenia. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2000.
Remains of Gallo-Roman substructures that support the choir of the present church.

San Crisogono (sacred to San Crisogono) is one of Rome's early parish churches (tituli). Pope Sylvester I (314–335) likely builds the first iteration in the fourth century AD; later reworkings occur in the twelfth and seventeenth centuries, respectively.

A church in Venice, Italy. San Giacomo di Rialto is claimed to be the oldest church in the city, with AD 421 as the traditional date of its consecration. The first documented attestations of the church belong to AD 1152.
A church dedicated to St. George located in Rome's Velabrum. Legend connects the church's location with the spot where the Roman she wolf found the abandoned twins, Romulus and Remus.
A church of the eleventh and twelfth century built along the line of the Roman Via Flaminia. The church incorporates Roman remains, including those of a bridge.

A sixteenth-century chapel near the Porta Latina that is believed to commemorate the location where the Christian apostle John was reportedly unharmed after being immersed in a vat of boiling oil in 92 CE.

The church of San Marcello al Corso is located adjacent to the Via del Corso (the ancient Via Lata) in Rome. Originally known as the Titulus Marcelli, it was established by Pope Marcellus I before 418 CE. The current edifice was completed in the seventeenth century following a destruction by fire of the earlier church in 1519.
San Martino ai Monti is a minor basilica in Rome. The basilica was founded on a site donated by Equitius during the reign of Pope St. Sylvester I in the fourth century.
