The church of San Menna in Rome was recorded by the anonymous pilgrim author of the Einsiedeln in the eighth century CE.  It was located along the Via Ostiensis, most likely between the Porta San Paolo and the Basilica of Saint Paul. Likely founded in 589, the church is not attested after the tenth century.
A Medieval church on the island of Sardinia that is dated to 1133.

San Nicola in Carcere is a titular church in Rome's historical district (rione) Sant'Angelo, close to the Forum Boarium. The twelfth-century church is built atop the remains of Roman temples of the ancient Forum Holitorium and incorporates their remains into its structure.

The Catholic church of San Paolo alla Regola located in the modern Rione of Rome named "Regola", to which it gives its name. The current edifice of the church dates to the seventeenth century. Excavations carried out from 1978 to 1982 documented multiple layers of architecture dating from the imperial period when this district was used primarily as one of warehouses (horrea) located near the Tiber River.
An abbey with tenth century origins connected to the pilgrimage route known as the Via Francigena.
A Romanesque-style Roman Catholic church and Benedictine monastery consecrated in 1117 but with possible origins in the eighth century.

name: San Pietro di Cantoni Italic sanctuary

props.description: An extra-urban Italic sanctuary located in the territory of ancient Saepinum.

San Pietro in Vincoli is a Roman Catholic titular church and minor basilica in Rome, Italy. It is located on the Oppian Hill and it houses relics connected with the Catholic Saint Peter. The basilica was founded as the Basilica Eudoxiana ca. 432-440. Pope Sixtus III consecrated the site in 439. The basilica has undergone numerous stages of building and rebuilding, notably under Pope Adrian I and Pope Julius II.

The Basilica of San Saba is located on the "Piccolo Aventino" in Rome. The basilica's origin is traced to 645 when fugitive monks from Mar Saba in Palestine traveled to Rome to attend the Lateran Council. They subsequently remained on the Aventine.
A monastery founded in A.D. 753 by Desiderius, future king of the Lombards. Located in modern Brescia, Italy.
A church on the Palatine Hill in Rome, sacred to Saint Theodore of Amasea, dating originally to the sixth century AD.

San Trifone in Posterula is a no-longer-extant titular church of Rome. It was located at the corner of Via dei Portoghesi and Via della Scrofa in the Campus Martius. The church likely originates in the eighth century; it appears in documentary records beginning in 1006. The ancient church was destroyed in 1746 when the Basilica of Sant'Agostino was enlarged.

The basilica of Saints Vitalis, Valeris, Gervase, and Protase, commonly known as the Basilica di San Vitale, is a titular minor basilica church in Rome. The church was built ca. AD 400 by Pope Innocent I.
A Medieval Christian sanctuary along the Via Appia, perhaps best known as the Church of Domine Quo Vadis.
A Catholic sanctuary on Mount Gargano, in northern Apulia, Italy. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

A famous ancient sanctuary of Poseidon located at the southern end of the modern Mani peninsula of the Peloponnese. Literary and archaeological evidence indicate that the sanctuary featured a temple, a cave treated as a mouth of the underworld, and other structural and monumental components. It seems to have served as a sanctuary site for suppliants and a context for the formal manumission of enslaved persons.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 E2 Sannabadae, Mon.
A paleochristian site located at the thirtieth mile of the ancient via Labicana, near to the ancient road station 'ad Bivium'.

A church in Trastevere with origins in the fifth century CE. Pope Urban I likely founded the first church on the same site in the third century CE. Its venerated saint is a Christian martyr of the third century CE. Pope Paschal I rebuilt the church in 822 CE, and moved the relics of St Cecilia to the site from the Catacombs of San Callisto on the Via Appia.

A fourth century AD structure along the Via Nomentana that was built by Constantine I as a mausoleum, originally housing the remains of his daughters Constantina and Helena. It was dedicated as a Christian church in the Middle Ages.

A minor Catholic basilica in Rome, Santa Croce in Gerusalemme is also a titular church in rione Esquilino and one of the Seven Pilgrim Churches. The church was consecrated ca. 325 to house relics of the Passion of Jesus Christ brought to Rome from the Holy Land by Empress St. Helena.
The church of Santa Lucia in Selci was founded by the eighth century and perhaps as early as the sixth century.
A fifth-century A.D. Christian church built in the Forum Romanum at the foot of the Palatine Hill.
The church of Santa Maria dell'Orto is a Roman Catholic church located in Rome's Trastevere district. Its foundation is traced to 508.
An eleventh century church near Aquinum built largely from spolia.
A Paleochristian church (fifth century CE) located near ancient Lavinium.
The church of Santa Maria di Devia is a Romanesque church of the eleventh century with Byzantine frescoes dating to the twelfth through fourteenth centuries.

A minor basilica church in Rome, Italy. Tradition holds that the church was founded in the sixth century to serve the Greek community at Rome. The beginnings of the present edifice lie in the eighth century.

The Minor Basilica of St. Mary in Domnica alla Navicella, also known as Santa Maria in Domnica, is a Roman Catholic church in Rome, Italy. The basilica has ancient origins, with likely foundations in the fifth century A.D.

Site of a large Roman villa, the remains of which were incorporated into the church of Santa Maria in Legarano. The early phases of the villa likely date to the early imperial period. At some point the villa became an Imperial property as it was donated to Pope Sylvester by Constantine.

Santa Maria in Monterone is a Catholic church located in Rome's Sant'Eustachio neighborhood. The church gives its name to the street on which it is located. The church building is situated on first-century BCE ruins in the area of the artificial basin known as the stagnum Agrippae. The present edifice dates to 1682.

A vicus and villa of the Roman period that lay along a route connecting the Via Flaminia to the coast. Archaeological evidence for the production of ceramics and tiles has been identified. A Christian monastery is established in 1090.
A deconsecrated Christian church on the flank of Rome's Caelian Hill, Santa Maria in Tempulo is connected with the ancient tradition of the spring and grove of Camenae.

Santa Maria Maggiore is a major papal basilica as well as one of the Seven Pilgrim Churches of Rome. Located on the Mons Cispius, the origins of the church date to the reign of Pope Celestine I (422–432).
A titular church on Rome's Aventine Hill dedicated to Saint Prisca. A Mithraeum dating to the late first century A.D. was discovered on the site in 1934.

A Christian basilica on the Aventine Hill in Rome was founded in the fourth century A.D. The church takes its name from a Christian martyr who was said to have lived on the Aventine. The church is the oldest extant Roman basilica structure that preserves its original plan in the city of Rome.
A Longobard church in Benevento, Italy, listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

The current Church of Saint Susanna at the Baths of Diocletian located on the Quirinal Hill in Rome dates from 1585. A titular church has existed on the site since ca. A.D. 280. The remains of an elite house (domus) are preserved beneath the church.

An ancient Christian basilica on the Caelian Hill in Rome that was built in AD 398 by will of a senator known as Pammachius. The basilica was located over the home of two Roman soldiers, John and Paul, who had been martyred under Julian in AD 362. The church was thus called the Titulus Pammachii.
Santi Quattro Coronati is an early Christian basilica on Rome's Caelian Hill begun by Pope Miltiades in the fourth century A.D.

The Basilica of Saint Stephen in the Round on the Caelian Hill is an ancient titular church located on the Caelian Hill in Rome. Its earliest phase belongs to the reign of Pope Simplicius (468-483). The early martyr Saint Stephen was honored in the church's consecration. Its circular plan is highly unusual.

name: Santuario dei Tre Santi (Alfio, Filadelfio e Cirino)

props.description: A church of ca. 1200 AD onwards, containing ancient material from the site of Apollonia in re-use.

name: Santuario di Maria Santissima del Lattani

props.description: A Marian sanctuary founded in 1430.

name: Santuario di Santa Maria di Barbana

props.description: The Santuario di Barbana is an ancient Marian shrine that dates ca. 582. It is said that Elia, the Patriarch of Aquileia, built a church near the hut of a hermit from Treviso by the name of Barbanus.

name: Santuario di Santa Maria Regina di Anglona

props.description: A Marian sanctuary located on the hill of Anglona in the province of Matera, Italy, that originates in the eleventh century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 64 B4 Satıköy
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 Seuera, Mon.

A chapel located in the Palestinian village of Beit Sahour that is believed to be the site of the annunciation of Jesus Christ's birth to the shepherds. A Christian monastery was founded on the site in the early fifth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 A3 Shyrvallos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 11 (Sidi Said)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Sigillyah

The principal ancient settlement on the Aegean island of the same name. The ancient site is located where the church of Agia Marina now stands on a prominent height, about 5 km southwest of the modern town of Sikinos.

The remains of a church belonging to the early Medieval period (perhaps the seventh century) that perhaps was a project of Pymenius, Bishop of Asidonia. This edifice is located on the hill that was the site of the oppidum referred to as Silla de Papa.
A church and possible monastic complex identified in 2021/2022 on modern Siniyah Island in the emirate of Umm al-Quwain, UAE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 26 (Siret el-Craat)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 9 (Siret el-Giamel/Gasrin di Beida)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 no. 12 (Siret er-Rcheim)

A large architectural complex on the east side of the acropolis ridge in Cyrene that was probably built at the beginning of the first century AD and used as a gymnasium, but was later repurposed as forum in Flavian times, when the rooms north of the complex were levelled in order to build a large basilica. Various ruins can still be seen, including the podium of a temple in the central area, possibly dedicated to Dionysos. A fragmentary inscription referring to the "portico of Caesar", found over the inner side of the porch, made the place known as Caesareum.

A three-aisled basilical church, with an external apse, situated near the centre of the ancient settlement of Apollonia (Cyrenaica), about half way between the East and West churches. The building has a narthex, a small western atrium and a range of added rooms along the north flank. The mosaics have been dated around the sixth century AD, but the building seems to have been built in earlier times (possibly fifth century AD).

A small basilica located outside the walls of the ancient Greek city of Akragas, at the foot of the so-called Temple of Juno, on the eastern slopes of the Hill of the Temples, in an area previously occupied by the necropolis of the late Roman period.

A Christian church located in the ancient city of Apollonia (Cyrenaica) built approximately in the fifth century A.D., probably on earlier Hellenistic foundations. The site was excavated in 1922 and shows a three-aisled nave with transept at the eastern end, and a single apse. Traces of the mosaic floor are still visible.

A large architectural complex that includes a three-aisled church with a projecting western apse, a small atrium, a baptistery, and other structures built up against the inner face of the western stretch of the city wall of Apollonia.
A three-aisled Paleochristian basilica of the fifth century A.D. located in the village of Son Bou in Menorca, Spain.
Near Parentium (today Porec/Parenzo in Croatia), Cape Sorna (Zorna) first hosted a large villa maritima, and then a small early Christian church.
The Southeastern Cluster of rock-cut churches at Lalibela.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Speos Artemidos

The church of Saints Marcellinus and Peter is a Roman Catholic church located on the Via Merulana in Rome. Pope Siricius built the original church in the fourth century A.D., close to the catacombs of Marcellinus and Peter. The relics of the saints were deposited in the church in 1256.
A ruined Medieval church located on the site of a Roman villa.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 E2 St-Estève-de-Ménerbes

Church in Geneva, Switzerland that was originally a Roman Catholic cathedral but was converted into a Protestant church during the Reformation. The original church was built in the 4th century but the present building began construction around 1150.
Mid-11th to mid-18th century church built on top of an older church that was an important pilgrimage site in Nubia. The church is located in the modern Sudanese village of Banganarti.

The archaeological site of Stancija Blek (Tar-Vabrega, Istria-Croatia) is located on the west coast of Istria. In antiquity, the site would have been located in the territory of Parentium. Archaeological investigation has documented more than 1,500 years of occupation, beginning perhaps with a Roman-period agricultural establishment (villa) that was subsequently followed by Late Antique and Medieval phases that include a so-called tower. Nearby are the remains of the church of Santa Croce.

Remains of this paleochristian church, located at modern Loupian in Hérault, France, were discovered and excavated in the 1980s. The structure lies immediately north of the current church of Ste-Cécile, which dates to the 14th century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Suk el-Oti
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 Symeon, Mon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 A1 Tabennisis
Taberna Frigida was a road station on the Via Aemilia Scauri at the crossing of the Frigidus river, located between Pisae and Luna.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 66 D4 Tagai
A ninth-century monastery located on a large basalt plateau in southeastern Armenia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Tebedut
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D2 Temes

Church in Geneva, Switzerland that has existed in some form since the 4th century CE. The 4th century building was followed by a 10th century Romanesque church which was destroyed by fire in the 14th century. The current church was constructed in the 15th century.

A temple of Apollo on the Aegean island of Lepsia, generally thought to have been located in the vicinity of the modern church of Hagios Nikolaos. It is attested in various ancient sources, including a recently published epigraphic document demarcating the temple lands.
A major ancient sanctuary of the Capitoline Hill, the remains of which are likely to be found beneath Santa Maria in Ara Coeli.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D2 Thaur
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B4 Thekoa
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Theodosios
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 70 G2 Theoktistos

A twelfth-century monastic foundation and fortified church in the modern town of Feres in Greece's Evros prefecture. The structure served as a mosque during the Ottoman empire and was returned to use as a church in the 20th century.
Thisbe was an ancient town of Boeotia located on the southern flank of Mt. Helicon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 76 D4 Tholas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 B4 Thomas, Mon.
An Early Christian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 77 D1 Titkois
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 I4 Torre del Fum
A monastery built by order of Emperor Justinian I. St. Catherine's at the foot of Mount Sinai in modern Egypt.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Tsenti
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 H2 Tura d’ Urhay
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Türkmen Baba
A monastery founded in late antiquity on the island then known as Lerina/Planasia (modern Île Saint-Honorat, France; BAtlas 16 D2 unnamed monastery)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 21 (Ras el-Hilal)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 28 (Siret Bu Hosc)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F3 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 C2 unnamed feature ()
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 F1 unnamed monastery (on Gallinaria Ins.)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 F4 unnamed unlabeled ((1 monastery))
A paleochristian basilica.
A paleochristian church located at Thugga.

name: Villa gallo-romaine de Barat de Vin

props.description: Rediscovered in the 19th century and partially excavated in the 1960s, this Roman-era complex east of modern Sorde-l'Abbaye (Landes, Nouvelle-Aquitaine in France) has been variously identified as a villa and a road station. Discoveries at the site included stone walls, a courtyard, a portico, baths, a mosaic floor, and a bordering Roman-era roadway. A medieval church indicated in documents and early maps seems to have used some of the ancient structures. The site appears to be largely overgrown and inaccessible today.

The church of San Pancrazio was founded in the sixth century A.D. by Pope Symmachus. It stands beyond the Porta San Pancrazio on the Ianiculum. Below the church is a massive catacomb referred to as S. Pancrazio or Ottavilla.
The remains of a Republican temple podium now support a Christian church at Villa S. Silvestro in Umbria. The temple's podium likely dates, in its first phase, to the third century B.C.
The basilica of Cabeza del Griego at Segobriga dates to the fifth and sixth centuries CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Wadi Crema
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 78 C3 Wadi Nagat
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 G2 Zacchaeus?, Mon.
A paleochristian basilica dating to the fifth and sixth centuries CE, located near the harbor of Limani Khersonesou, Pediada District, northern Crete.
*Aquae or pagus Aquensis was a bath site located along the Strei river in the territory of Ulpia Traiana Sarmizegetusa.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 *Evaunum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 *Thermae Privatae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 B3 Ad Aquas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 A2 Ad Aquas Caesaris
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Ad Mediam
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E5 Agamemnoniai Thermai

A famous Euboean spa city in the Hellenistic and Roman periods, located at modern Loutra Aidepsou. The polis' original location may have been 3-4 km north in the area of a modern town that carries the modern name Aidepsos (previously Lipso).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C2 Ain el Hammam
Among various features at Aix-en-Diois are the remains of baths that could either pertain to a sanctuary or a villa.
Remains of a Gallo-Roman bath complex are now incorporated in a modern spa at Amélie-les-Bains-Palada in the French department of Pyrénées-Orientales.
An ancient thermal site known as Aponi fons or Aquae Patavinae.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 D1 Aquae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F1 Aquae *Arnemetiae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Aquae Albulae
A group of springs located west of Tibur (modern Tivoli). The main spring, known as Lago della Regina, preserves traces of an ancient Roman bath.
The Aquae Apollinares were thermal springs located between Careiae and Tarquinii, perhaps to be associated with the Bagni di Stigliano or Bagni di Vicarello.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C4 Aquae Apollinares Novae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 C4 Aquae Aptuccensium
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 A3 Aquae Bormonis?
Therapeutic springs located near Caere.
Aquae Calidae was an ancient spa depicted on the Peutinger Map. Modern scholarship associates it with archaeological remains found in modern Vichy (France).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 E6 Aquae Calidae
An ancient Roman thermal spa in modern Caldes de Malavella
A site of settlement connected to thermal springs, according to Ptolemy.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 Aquae Carpitanae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F2 Aquae Convenarum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 28 C5 Aquae Dacicae
Aquae Helveticae was a Roman vicus on the river Limmat.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D2 Aquae Herculis
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 20 D3 Aquae Iasae
Aquae Onesiorum (modern Bagnères-de-Luchon, France)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 C3 Aquae Originae

Located in Etruria, Aquae Passeris was frequented in antiquity for its thermal springs, as noted by Martial. The site is included on the Tabula Peutingeriana, placing it on the road from Rome to Volsinii. Its location likely corresponds to that of Bacucco, a comune to the north of Viterbo.
Remains of an Etrusco-Roman thermal complex, dating from the third century B.C. to late antiquity, were documented between 2000 and 2003.

A thermal site located near Roman Rusellae in regular use and reuse since at least Roman times. Its ancient name is not known. The first modern excavations and mapping efforts date to the 18th century of the modern era.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 B3 Aquae Segestanae
An ancient settlement at modern Sceaux-du-Gâtinais in France (BAtlas 11 C4 Sceaux-du-Gâtinais)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G2 Aquae Siccae
An ancient Roman thermal spa and site of the Temple of Sulis Minerva.
A thermal site in Etruria northeast of Centumcellae.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D2 Aquae Terebellicae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D3 Aquae Traianae?
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Aquae Vescinae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D4 Aquae?
A place named on the Peutinger map, possibly to be associated with modern Monte Cerboli, Italy.

An ancient road station and/or spa mentioned on the Peutinger Map. It's precise location cannot be determined today; however, some scholars have suggested that it be identified with the modern Terme di Caronte at Lamezia Terme in Italy's Cantanzaro province.
The ruins of a Roman bath complex at Divona Cadurcorum.
A Roman site in modern Jordan, known today as ‘Ayn Gharandal. It includes remains of a late Roman fort and bathhouse.

A Roman-era bathing establishment was identified at modern Arriana in Greece during the 1970s. The facility dates to the second century CE and was destroyed in the third century. Subsequently, the site served as a cemetery through the fourth century.
The Asklepieion at Pergamon is located 3 km south of the Acropolis.
A key element of a well-known area of thermal spas and springs already in use in Roman antiquity, located in modern Italy's Veneto region.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H2 Bad Bertrich
The so-called "Baths of Nero" are located near the Porta a Lucca in Pisa.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Balesmes-sur-Marne
A bath complex known from the Severan marble plan and perhaps also referred to in the Historia Augusta.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C3 Balneum Regis
The Bar Hill Fort Bathhouse was built around 140 CE, at the same time as the Antonine wall, and is a long and narrow stone structure with latrines and two heated rooms.

Dating to the second century, the so-called "Barbara Baths" (Barbarathermen in German) are the largest Roman bath complex known north of the Alps. The bath complex was reused first as a Medieval castle and then as a seventeenth century Jesuit college. The ruins were designated as part of the "Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St. Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier" UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 B5 Batanaia

name: Bath at northwest foot of the Areopagus

props.description: A bathhouse located northwest of the Areopagus Hill and southwest of the Agora in Athens, first built in the late second century BC and remaining in use until the late sixth century AD.
Bath building constructed by Roman soldiers in Block C3.
Bath building located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos, replaced by Bath E3 Phase 2.
Bath building located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos, replaces Bath E3 Phase 1.

Bath building originally occupying all of Block F3, located in the intramuros military sector of Dura-Europos. Excavations reveal that the bath likely had two phases of renovation (one perhaps after 165 CE, and another around c. 200 CE), before it was destroyed by fire. Dura-Europos' amphitheater was built over the remains of Bath F3, and thus the amphitheater's dedicatory inscription, dated to 216 CE, serves as a terminus ante quem for the bath building.
Bath building constructed by Roman soldiers in Block M7.
Bath/gymnasium complex containing a palaestra and a synagogue that was developed in the 3rd century CE.
Public baths at Cremna.
The Baths of Antoninus at Carthage date to the middle of the second century and are the largest of the Roman public bath complexes in North Africa.
Public baths generally dated to the reign of Caracalla.
The Roman military bathhouse at Cilurnum.
A public bath complex built at Palmyra toward the end of the second century CE and perhaps renovated during the reign of Diocletian.
A public bath (thermae) of the Roman Imperial period.

name: Baths of the Old Convent, Tharros

props.description: Roman baths at Tharros known as the "Baths of the Old Convent".

The so-called "Baths of the Philosopher" or "Terme del Filosofo" in region V of the ancient city of Ostia take their modern sobriquet from a bust of Plotinus dating to the middle of the third century CE.
The remains of a stone-built Roman bath at Bearsden on the Antonine Wall.
The largest known Roman iron-making site in the Weald, with the remains of a substantial bath house.
A Roman balneum attached either to a Roman townhouse or mansio.
The remains of a Roman legionary fort located in North Lanarkshire near Glasgow, Scotland, that was built in connection with the Antonine Wall (ca. A.D. 142-165).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I2 Bourbon-l’Archambault
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Bourbonne-les-Bains
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Brestovačka Banja
A Roman fort and associated structures at modern Stolniceni, Romania. An indigenous settlement was likely located a few kilometers to the north.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D4 Buziaş
A thirteenth/fourteenth century Byzantine bath at Thessalonica; listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988.
An ancient Roman thermal spa in modern Caldes de Montbui.
A Roman settlement with public baths that was occupied from the later republican period until the fifth or sixth century CE.
Roman baths dating from the first to fourth centuries.
A bath complex dating to the later second century that were subsequently repurposed as a Christian basilica.

An island of the Bay of Naples off the Sorrentine Peninsula that has, since the time of the Roman Republic, been a popular seaside resort area. The emperor Tiberius maintained twelve villas on the island.
A bath complex (IX, 4, 5-18) located at the intersection of the Via Stabiana and Via di Nola that was built following the earthquake of AD 62.
A public bath complex at Herculaneum, dating to the first century A.D. It was subdivided into "male" and "female" sectors.
The thermal baths of Chassenon are among the best preserved in the Gallo-Roman world. They are part of the ancient city of Cassinomagus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Cortinal de los Baños

An ancient Christian pilgrimage site that grew into an active town centered around the monastery complex devoted to Saint Menas who is said to have been buried there. The town reached its peak as the most visited pilgrimage site in Egypt during the sixth century AD.
Roman fortlet and fort on the Antonine Wall in West Dunbartonshire, Scotland.
A stone-built bathhouse at Exeter connected with the Neronian fortress.

An archaeological site that comprises remains of an urban area of the Roman period ranging in date from the first to the fifth centuries. Among the complexes in evidence are a salting factory, a pottery works, and thermal baths.
Salt springs continuously exploited from the Neolithic period through the early modern era. Also the site of a Roman bath complex.

A bath complex adjacent to Pompeii's forum that was built after 80 BC (Reg VII, Ins 5, 2, 5, 7, 24). The baths are publicly financed by the duovir Lucius Caesius and the aediles Caius Occius and Lucius Niraemius.
A Roman bath complex located at Rottweil, Germany.
A Gallo-Roman bath complex located at the modern town of Mackwiller in the French commune of Waldhambach.
A suite of Roman-period public baths in Lyon, France. The site is located in the rue des Farges on the slope of the Fourvière hill. The baths may have been linked to the cult of Apollo.
The so-called Great City Baths at Salona or Great Thermae were built in the second or beginning of third century A.D.
The Roman baths at Conimbriga.
The largest of the public bath complexes at Aphrodisias dating to the early second century CE.

The so-called Hammam Beni Guecha is a ruined Roman spa located just north and east of the modern Algerian village of Chouara Torche (Beni Guecha municipality). The site may be associated with a road station recorded on the Peutinger Map: Fons Carmerata.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C4 Hammath/Ammathous
A large bath complex adjacent to the ancient harbor of Ephesus that measures ca. 160 by 170 meters and was originally built in the late first century CE.
A circular public bath building dating to the Hellenistic period in the agora of Amathous.

Hierapolis (modern Pamukkale in Turkey's Denizli province) was a major ancient city in Phrygia located at the site of hot springs in southwestern Anatolia. The site was designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988.
The so-called House of the Hotel (Casa dell'Albergo) is an opulent Augustan-period domus that occupies the bulk of the southern end of Insula III at Herculaneum.
An ancient thermal site with both nuragic and Roman remains. Now the community of Benettuti on the island of Sardinia.
The so-called "Jewry Wall" is a substantial segment of surviving Roman masonry at Ratae.
A Roman auxiliary fort with associated vicus and port at modern Vleuten-De Meern in the Dutch province of Utrecht.
Extensive Roman bathhouse associated with the Roman fort at modern Schirenhof in the German municipality of Schwäbisch Gmünd, Baden-Württemberg.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D6 Kuršumlijska Banja
Ancient thermal baths in use from the Etrusco-Roman period onward.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Leontos Kome
A Roman fort and tower in the modern French commune of St-Gemmes-le-Robert (Pays de la Loire).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Menthon-St-Bernard
One of two Roman Bath Houses known at Vindolanda.
The Minor baths of Itálica date to the reign of Trajan. Under Hadrian, the bath complex was expanded to an area of some 1,500 m².
A Gallo-Roman bath complex last occupied during the fourth century A.D.

Remnants of an ancient settlement have been identified south and east of the modern beach village of Nopigeia on Crete that date to at least the 5th century BCE. The church of Agios Georgios Nopigion, which is located between Nopigia and Drapania, was built on the remains of a Roman-era bath complex. A single, late source (Aelian) provides the name Mithymna, which may have belonged to this settlement. Bronze Age remains have also been identified in the vicinity.
The modern-day site of a Romano-British settlement dating from first and second centuries A.D.
The Public Bath at Pella is a bathing complex constructed and continuously renovated between the 4th and the 1st centuries BCE.
The public baths at Saepinum are located along the decumanus and date to the first and second centuries.

The Ravenglass Roman Bath House, also known as "Walls Castle", is linked to the second-century CE fort at Glannoventa. English Heritage dates the site ca. 130 and deems it one of the tallest surviving Roman ruins in Britain.
Roman baths at Ribchester dating ca. 100 CE.
A thermal spring where a Roman bath complex was first built in the third century A.D.
A two-phase complex of public baths at Fregellae dating to the third and second centuries BCE represents an important point in the transition from "Greek" bathing culture to "Roman" bathing culture.
The Roman bath at Lugdunum Convenarum is part of the second urban nucleus found at the foot of the Fourvière hill.
A bath complex located adjacent to the theater of Volaterrae.

name: Roman bath house, Callendar Park, Falkirk

props.description: The remains of a Roman bath house were discovered in Callendar Park, Falkirk in 1980.

The Roman Bath House, Castleford, consists of the now-buried archaeological remains of a Roman military bath house that was built in the mid to late AD 80s. The site was excavated in 1978. The ancient bath was situated within the site of a former defensible annex that was constructed to the north of Castleford Roman fort.
At the Prestatyn Roman Site, a bath complex was identified via excavation (1934-7; 1981). The bathhouse measures approximately 11.7 by 4.5 meters.

name: Roman Bath-House at Little Chart, Kent

props.description: The remains of a Roman bath house were discovered in a field to the west of the village of Little Chart, Kent in 1942. These remains included fragments of mosaic pavement. Excavations in 1942 and later in 1947 identified the site as a Roman bathing complex.
Bathhouse complex in Aquae Sulis, modern-day Bath. This well-preserved site is now a museum and the bathing complex itself is below modern street-level.
Public baths at Parium used from the second century BCE and renovated several times in the late Roman and Byzantine periods, likely being used until at least the seventh century CE.

name: Roman baths at calle Reyes Huertas

props.description: The Roman baths at calle Reyes Huertas date to the third century CE. They were located outside of the ancient city center in an area that has been categorized as industrial. Their exact nature is difficult to determine.
The Roman baths at Carnuntum.
The Roman baths at Fiesole date to the time of Sulla in the first century B.C.
Imperial baths located on Fundi's decumanus maximus.
The Roman baths at Gaujac were begun ca. A.D. 20.

name: Roman baths at Hydata Hypsitana/Forum Traiani

props.description: The Roman baths Fordongianus dates to the first through third centuries.
The Roman baths at Oescus are dated to the middle of the third century.
The Roman baths at Sagalassos date to the early part of the first century A.D.
The Roman baths at Sexi/Saxetanum date to the first century.

The Imperial Baths at Trier (also referred to as "Kaiserthermen") are a large Roman bath complex in Trier, Germany. The site was designated as part of the "Roman Monuments, Cathedral of St. Peter and Church of Our Lady in Trier", a UNESCO World Heritage Site. The complex dates to the fourth century A.D.
The remains of baths at the Gallo-Roman site of Vertillum.
The Roman baths of Acinipo date to the first century B.C.
Roman baths at Aquinum.
The Roman Baths of Como were in use from the later first century until the third century CE.

The site of Coriovallum is known for its well-preserved bath complex. These baths were first built ca. A.D. 40 and then re-built during the reign of Hadrian. They remained in use until the fourth century.
The Roman baths of Ebora were in use during the second and third centuries. The archaeological site was discovered in 1987.
The Roman baths of Taormina are built atop a public building of the Hellenistic period. The Roman baths originally fronted on the forum and were expanded in the second century.
Baths of the early imperial period at Carsulae.
The remains of Roman baths located in the ager of Mesagne.
Roman baths located at Spoletium.
A monumental bath complex constructed in the 2nd c. CE in modern Varna, ancient Odessus.

name: Roman Villa at Għajn Tuffieħa, Malta

props.description: Ruins at this ancient site include a Roman-era bath complex and extensive mosaics. Its ancient name is unknown.

A bathhouse located at Orpington, Kent was excavated between 1971 and 1975. The site flourished between ca. 270 and 400 CE. Its post-abandonment phase includes Anglo-Saxon graves of the fifth and sixth centuries.
Ruins of Roman baths at Royat, France, that were listed as a registered monument in 1889.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F2 S. Calogero
The village of San Casciano dei Bagni, located south of Siena, Italy, has been frequented since at least the first millennium BCE on account of its thermal springs with supposedly healing properties.
A Roman bath complex that was reused first as a monastic complex and then as the Medieval church of San Basilio.
The sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios on Mt. Lykaion.

A multi-period site with medieval and modern remains atop Roman levels, including a medieval farmhouse built atop a Roman cistern. Excavations revealed multi-phase evidence, including a bath building decorated with mosaics. The structures may have been related to the nearby Villa dei Quintilli.

The so-called "Sarno Baths" (Regio VIII, Insula 2, nos. 17–21) at Pompeii resulted from the merger and remodeling of two atrium houses, no. 18 and no. 21. There is considerable debate in the scholarly literature as to the architectural phasing.
A sacred Etruscan thermal site.
A pre-Roman and Gallo-Roman oppidum in the territory of the Volcae Arecomici.
The Roman Bath House at Segontium.
A second to fourth century A.D. Romano-British bathhouse that was excavated in 2002. Findspot of RIB 3012.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 Siminina?

An architectural complex near the sea in Apollonia. It went through different stages and alterations between, roughly, the fourth century B.C. and the seventh century A.D. It was probably used as a private villa, then as public baths and, perhaps, as cisterns in later years.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 D6 Sokobanja

A Roman camp with baths and associated settlement were identified at modern Souk El Arbaa in Morocco in the late 19th and 20th centuries on high ground occupied by a qubba called "Sidi Aïssa". Although ancient structures and settlement extent identified previously are not visible in modern satellite imagery, the site -- bisected by a rail line predating 1941 and occupied by a cemetery and more recent structures -- can still be correlated closely with previously published photographs and plans. Recent scholarly opinion diverges from that reflected in the Barrington Atlas to lean toward identifying this site with ancient (Vo)psiciana (q.v.), attested by Ptolemy, the Antonine Itinerary, and the Ravenna Cosmographer.

A bath complex at Herculaneum that was discovered in the excavations of the so-called "Insula Occidentalis". It was not in use as a bath at the time of the 79 CE eruption as it had been repurposed as a ship-shed.
The Stabian Baths (VII, 1, 8) and (VII,2) are located at the intersection of the Via Stabiana and the Via dell'Abbondanza. They are the oldest baths in Pompeii.
A first century BC bath complex in Pompeii built against the walls near the Porta Marina
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 C3 Suessatium
A Roman bath complex, located in the northeast part of the modern town of Liria in Spain's Valencia province.
One of the 19 Roman bath complexes identified in Mérida.
The Roman Baths of Las Bóvedas are near the mouth of the Guadalmina River.
Remains of Roman baths at Antequera in Spain.

name: Termas Romanas do Alto da Cividade

props.description: A monumental public bath (thermae) at Braga, dating from the first to third centuries CE.
Thermal baths at Catania dating to the fourth and fifth centuries CE. The archaeological remains are located near the present-day Piazza del Duomo of Catania.
A Roman bath located at Aquae Passeris.

The so-called "Baths of the Swimmer" or "Terme del Nuotatore" (V,X,3) in region 5 of the ancient city of Ostia takes its modern name from a partially preserved black-and-white mosaic pavement. These baths were built in the late first century CE during the reign of Domitian. The complex was rebuilt during the Antonine period. The complex remained in use until ca. 250. The site was first excavated during 1938-1942. Andrea Carandini directed another campaign of excavation from 1966 to 1975.
Terme della Regina, Tuscania (Italy).

The so-called Terme della Trinacria in Regio III of Ostia Antica were built in the Hadrianic period at the same time as the Temple of Serapis (III,XVII,4). The modern toponym is derived from a triskelion included in a mosaic decoration.

Mineral springs located at modern Lamezia Terme in Italy's Cantanzaro province. There is some archaeological evidence for exploitation of the springs in antiquity, but the current spa complex is completely modern in date. Some scholars have suggested that the Terme di Caronte may correspond to the "Aquae angae" mentioned on the Peutinger Map.
A site of ancient thermal baths.
The largest known bath complex of Roman Mediolanum, built in the late third to early fourth centuries CE.
Roman baths of ancient Luceria
The excavations carried out following the earthquake of 1908 revealed a number of baths dating to the early centuries of the Roman Imperial period.
These Roman baths in modern Bevagna, Italy, date to the second century CE and were rediscovered in 1891.
The Roman baths of ancient Ferentium are located adjacent to the Roman theater.
The Thermae Antoninianae, commonly known as the Baths of Caracalla, were built in Rome between AD 212 and 216.
Public baths near the Viminal Hill in Rome constructed under Diocletian from 298-306 CE.

The Thermae Helenianae or Thermae Helenae was a thermal complex located in the Horti Spei Veteris, also referred to as the Horti Variani. The baths were situated near the arcus Neroniani adjacent to the Amphitheatrum Castrense. As Helena, mother of Constantine I, restored them following a fire in 323-326, they are named for her.
Thermae Novati, ancient baths of the Vicus Patricius that were located in the vicinity of S. Pudenziana.
The northern bath complex of Hippo Regius.
The site of thermal spings, founded as a sub-colony of Selinus in the seventh century B.C. Thought to be in the area of modern Sciacca.

Thermae Titi (Tïtianae), or Baths of Titus, were public baths built at Rome in 81 CE under the emperor Titus. Located at the foot of the Esquiline Hill, the baths occupy part of the Domus Aurea of Nero. The Baths of Trajan were constructed immediately adjacent to the Baths of Titus in the early second century.
A monumental public bath of ancient Rome that was built between 104 and 109. The architect Apollodorus of Damascus built the baths for Trajan.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 E4 Thermai
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A4 Thermai Theseos
A Gallo-Roman bath complex of the third century CE at Lutetia (modern Paris).
A Gallo-Roman bath complex identified in 1991 at Saint-Romain-en-Galt that originally covered some 8 ha.
A Gallo-Roman bath complex at Pithiviers-le-Vieil.
The ancient Roman thermal baths of Cemenelum.

name: Tomen y Mur Roman bath house

props.description: A bath complex associated with the first-century fort at Tomen y Mur.
Referred to today as "Gisacum", this vast Gallo-Roman religious complex (with major spa), is located largely in the French commune of Le Vieil Evreux.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed bath (SW Siminina?)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 G2 unnamed unlabeled ((3 monuments, 1 bath))
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 G2 unnamed unlabeled ((3 monuments, 1 bath))
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 G3 unnamed unlabeled ((1 bath, 1 cemetery))

Vogüé (1868 and 1877) assumed that the large, square fort and bath facilities at the extinct Jebel Seis volcano in Syria were Roman (whence Dussaud 1927 whence BAtlas); however, excavations in the 1960s demonstrated that the fortification and other structures constitute an Umayyad fortified palace and associated settlement built in the late 7th to early 8th centuries CE, incorporating some elements of a pre-existing settlement, fort and/or monastery dating to the 6th century CE. There is no evidence for activity at the site in the early Roman empire.
A villa maritima in eastern Liguria, inhabited from ca. 100 BC to the fifth century AD.
The Vieil-Évreux Roman Baths of the second and third centuries A.D. have four architectural phases that have been identified.
A first-century A.D. Roman villa and bath complex located in Etruria.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 C6 Vrnjačka Banja
The remains of Roman baths are preserved at Welwyn, where a Romano-British roadside settlement was located near a crossing of the river Mimram.

The "Ponte Sanguinario" at Spoleto is a late republican to early imperial Roman bridge. The remains of the bridge are now below street level in the Piazza della Vittoria, not far from the Porta San Gregorio.
A Trajanic bridge spanning the Danube River at Drobeta-Turnu Severin.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C2 (H)Alesion Pedion

An ancient settlement at modern Mértola (Portugal) on the west bank of the Guadiana river (ancient Anas). The town is mentioned in Roman sources, but most of the monumental archaeology dates to the Muslim period or later (including a number of large piers previously misidentified as a Roman bridge).
A Roman bridge in the northwestern Iberian peninsula that crosses the río Valiñas.
An ancient bridge on the Afrin river.
A ruined Roman bridge near Ain Diwar, Syria, that once crossed the Tigris river.

A Roman bridge with origins in the first century B.C. that crosses the Albarregas River, a tributary of the Guadiana River. The bridge was been declared a Site of Cultural Interest in 1912 and named a part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Mérida Archaeological Ensemble" in 1993.
A six-span stone bridge over the Tagus river, also known as the Puente Trajan.
A Roman bridge at Alebaece Reiorum Apollinarium (modern Riez).
A bridge on the Via Claudia Augusta crossing the Adige river near Maiensis Statio.

A Roman bridge in the modern Barranco de Aluenda (Aluenda Gorge), which runs down from the northwest to the modern village of Aluenda in Spain's Zaragoza province (Aragon). This bridge was east of ancient Bilbilis on the Roman road to Caesaraugusta.

name: Ancient bridge on Via Egnatia at modern Arisvi

props.description: An ancient bridge was reported at modern Arisvi, crossing the river Lissos, in documents in the general state archive of Komotini 1945-1961, which still seems to be known to local community and described as Roman. A modern bridge stands in its place today.
A Roman bridge near Antalya, Turkey.
An arched Bronze Age bridge near the Mycenaean center of Tiryns. The bridge is located along an ancient highway connecting Tiryns to Epidauros.
A Roman bridge crossing the Carapelle stream in the territory of Ascoli Satriano.
A third century AD Roman bridge located near Shushtar, Iran.

A bridge that crosses the Valderaduey river and that has traditionally been considered to be of Roman origin, although it may in fact be more recent in date. The ruins are found in the municipality of Becilla de Valderaduey in the Province of Valladolid, of the autonomous community of Castilla y León, Spain.
Within the Villa Neronis
An ancient bridge over the Licca river, located at Abodiacum.
An unnamed bridge over Eurymedon fl.
An ancient bridge over the Licca river, located at Augusta Vindelicum.
A Medieval bridge of Roman origins on the Vara river.
Excavations of the metro train line exposed piers of an ancient bridge across the Kephissos.
The Bridge of Mantible (also known as Puente de Mantible) crosses the Ebro river northwest of Vareia. It is a Roman segmental arch bridge that likely dates to the second century AD.

name: Bridge on Via Cassia near *Volsinii Veteres

props.description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 C3

name: Bridge over Fosso di Acqua Rossa

props.description: The remains of a Roman bridge that once carried a road over the Fosso di Acqua Rossa. Ward-Perkins posits a date of the second or first century BCE based on comparanda in the vicinity.
An ancient bridge located southwest of Turgalium, over Gibralzo Arroyo.
A Roman bridge over the the modern Jiloca river at Calamocha, Teruel province, Aragon, Spain.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B3 Caneiras do Roxo
A Medieval bridge built by Alfonso XI of Castile and León that possibly rests on earlier Roman foundations.
An unnamed bridge over the river Caparra, just north of ancient Capera, which is west-southwest of modern Casablanca (Guijo de Granadilla, Caceres, Extremadura, Spain)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Capela de S. Águeda
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Casal da Várzea
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Casal do Mouchão

name: Casas de Don Antonio Roman Bridge

props.description: A Roman bridge spanned the modern Río Ayuela at what is now the south side of the Spanish village of Casas de Don Antonio. Scholars believe that the Roman road station "Ad Sorores" was located somewhere immediately north of this bridge.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 E2 Castropodame
A restored (2019) bridge located near Sintra, Portugal.
A Roman bridge
Remains of a Roman bridge and paved road.
A bridge with Roman origins across the río Adaja.
A Roman bridge crossing the Rhyndakos river that was built after AD 258.
The Cyclopean Bridge of Agios Ioannis is located one kilometer south of the citadel of Mycenae.

Sited at an ancient crossing-point over the river Jordan, between Neapolis and Philadelpheia, a Roman-era bridge is thought to have been one of many. The modern bridge and its antecedents are often referred to as the "Damia Bridge".

The remains of a stone, arched Roman bridge at Decimomannu, to the west of Assemini, Sardinia. Three of the original six arches survive today. The bridge may originally have been as much as 160 meters in length. R.J. Rowland considered it to be of Iulio-Claudian date.
An historic bridge built across the Tigris river by Sancaroğlu Ubeydoğlu Yusuf in AD 1065.
A restored Roman bridge at ancient Ad Calceum Herculis (modern el Kantara, Algeria)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 C2 unnamed bridge (Eleutherna)
An unnamed Roman bridge on the Via Aemilia over the ancient Incia river. Located near modern S. Ilario d’Enza in Italy.
The strait between Euboea and Boeotia, located where the modern Greek city of Chalkida now stands. A bridge connected the land masses in antiquity.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Firuzabad
The archaeological site of the Girsu Bridge, located in modern Iraq's Dhi Qar governorate, was identified in 1920 and has been dated to the third millennium BCE.
A Roman bridge at modern Golmayo in Spain's Soria province (Castile and León), crossing the modern river of the same name. The modern N-122 highway still makes use of this originally Roman bridge.
A bridge on the road from to Egin and Harput.
A ruined bridge at Rapallo, Italy, that the local toponymastic tradition links to the third-century BCE military operations of the Carthaginian commander Hannibal.
The site of a Roman fort and an associated vicus along Dere Street, now buried and bisected by the modern A1.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Horta do Vinagre
The Bridge of Justinian or Sangarius Bridge is a Late Roman bridge built by the emperor Justinian I across the Sangarius river. The bridge's original span was ca. 430 m.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Iustiniani/S. Callinici Pons
On Hiera Hodos at Kopros.
A Roman-era bridge spanning the Arabkir Çay south of Hastek Kale in Turkey. Now submerged and inaccessible.
A Roman segmental arch bridge over the Xanthos fl. near the ancient city of Xanthos in Lycia.
A historical bridge near the Mianeh in East Azerbaijan partially destroyed in 1946.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 C3 unnamed bridge (Kleidi, between Methone and Thessalonica)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Le Pouzin
A late-Roman bridge, ancient name unknown, located near the ancient city of Limyra in modern Turkey.

A Roman bridge over the the modern Pancrudo river south of Luco de Jiloca in Teruel province, Aragon, Spain. The bridge crosses the river just below the modern Lechago dam, less than 100m before the Pancrudo's confluence with the Jiloca.
The Roman Bridge of Lurs is located to the northeast of Alaunium (modern Lurs, France). It was a bridge of the ancient Via Domitia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C1 unnamed bridge (Maameltein, N Palaibyblos)

"Magliana Vecchia" is the name of the 40th urban administrative zone of the Comune di Roma. It is located to the southwest of the city center and straddles the Grande Raccordo Anulare (Ring Road) line that circles the city. The compilers of the Barrington Atlas noted that this place includes a "bridge" and a "settlement". In terms of archaeological remains, numerous sites have been identified in and around Magliana Vecchia, including a Roman bridge and several villas.
The Malabadi Bridge spans the Batman River in southeastern Turkey. Construction began ca. 1146/47 CE and continued until 1154.
Roman bridge, Masseria Ponte
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Mélas
An unnamed Roman bridge over the modern river Rhôny. It is located in the modern Nages-et-Solorgues commune in France's Gard department.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 21 E5 Negotin
A Roman bridge at Ilipoula (modern Niebla), spanning the Iberos/Luxia river (modern Rio Tinto).
An Ottoman bridge at Oinoanda on the Xanthos river. A Latin inscription found nearby suggests an earlier Roman bridge existed at the site.
A Roman bridge over the arroyo Pedroches near Corduba, Spain. The bridge lies along the ancient road to Augusta Emerita.
A second-century A.D. Roman bridge across the Penkalas river ( Kocaçay) in Aezani, Asia Minor.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B4 Petra
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Philippi Pons
A Roman bridge that carried Dere Street across the River Tees in northern England.

A Roman bridge crossing the Aufidus (modern Ofanto) river at the border between the modern Italian provinces of Campania and Basilicata. The bridge is one of two candidate identifications for the "Pons Aufidi" shown on the Peutinger map (the Ponte S. Venere), a possibility bolstered by recent comparative archaeological studies of the two bridges. The Pietra Dell'Oglio remains in use today for the Strada Comunale Fs di Monteverde, which connects to the Strada Statale 401 dir dell'Alto Ofanto e del Vulture at the southeast end of the bridge.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Pizões

Pol-e Dokhtar (also Romanized as Pol Dokhtar and Pul-i-Dukhtar) is a city in the Lorestan Province of Iran. The ruins of a Sasanian bridge over the Kashkan River are still visible today a few kilometers north of the city; that structure was part of a Sasanian Royal road that connected the Persian capitals Istakhr and Bishapur with towns in central and northern Mesopotamia. In 1992, a horde of ancient objects were found in the nearby Cave Kalmakareh (also referred to as Cave Kalmakarra).
The Pons Aelius or Aelian Bridge (now Ponte Sant'Angelo) was completed by Hadrian in AD 134 to span the Tiber opposite the new mausoleum of the emperor.
The Pons Aemilius was the first stone bridge across the Tiber river at Rome (241 B.C.). Its remains are known today as the 'Ponte Rotto'.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 A1 Pons Apollinis

A Roman bridge (and possibly coincident road station) on the Via Appia over the Aufidus (modern Ofanto) river in Italy, listed on the Peutinger map and in the Antonine Itinerary. Traditionally it has been identified with a bridge known today as Ponte S. Venere, but recently archaeological work has cast doubt on this view and some scholars now favor an identification with the Ponte romano Pietra dell'Oglio, which lies about 15 miles further south and can be securely dated to the Roman period.

The Pons Aurelius is known only from late Roman documents (fourth and fifth centuries CE) and is most likely the same bridge known as pons Antoninus in the early Middle Ages. The present-day Ponte Sisto (built in the fifteenth century) reused some of the ancient Roman bridge piers.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pons Auruncus
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pons Campanus
A Roman stone bridge on the Tiber river, spanning the river channel from the Tiber Island westward to the Transtiberim (Trastevere). The original bridge dates to the first century BC.
A Roman bridge across the Cendere Çayı that lies west of Arsameia pros Nymphaio (modern Eskikale in Turkey's Adıyaman Province).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 B5 Pons Constantini
An ancient settlement and bridge at the modern village of Pontoux (Saône-et-Loire department, France).

The Pons Fabricius (sometimes referred to as the Ponte dei Quattro Capi) is a first-century BC bridge in Rome that spans part of the Tiber River from the area of the Circus Flaminius to the Tiber Island.

Pons Mulvius (the Milvian Bridge) is a Roman bridge over the Tiber River. The original phase of the bridge dates to the consulship of Gaius Claudius Nero in 206 B.C. The bridge is most famous as the site of a decisive battle between Maxentius and Constantine I fought in A.D. 312.
A bridge across the Tiber river built either by Caius or Nero in order to connect the western part of the Campus Martius with the Campus Vaticanus.
The Pons Probi across the Tiber is attested in the Notitia and, according to Platner and Ashby, was likely built under Emperor Probus in the third century AD.

A bridge (and associated mutatio or other road station) listed on the Peutinger Map. Hammond 1974 (whence BAtlas) localizes the bridge in the modern "Fusha e Kododeshit" plain in Albania, located west of modern Urakë, which he identifies with a mansio named Claudanon listed in the ItBurd. Bitrakova-Grozdanova 1994 would place the bridge at or very near Urakë and Claudanon in the vicinity of moden Radožda in Macedonia.

The Pons Sublicius is traditionally held to be the earliest Roman bridge across the river Tiber. The textual tradition assigns its construction to the king Ancus Marcius in 642 B.C. It crossed the Tiber River near the Forum Boarium just downstream from Tiber Island.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E3 Pons Tirenus
A bridge over the Vidourle and road station on the Via Domitia.
A Medieval bridge spanning the Tech river that dates between 1321 and 1341.
A Roman bridge in the modern French commune of Boucieu-le-Roi (Ardèche department).
A Roman bridge that carries the Via Julia Augusta across the Touloubre river.
The Gallic bridge of Sainte-Catherine is located at Kergonan, between the towns of Plounévézel (Finistère) and Treffrin (Côtes-d'Armor), in the Brittany region. It may date to the seventh century.
A bridge that spans the river known as the Cavalon (French), once part of the Via Domitia.
A Roman bridge on the island of Majorca (Pollença, Mallorca, Spain).
A Roman bridge on the Via Domitia.
A Roman segmental arch bridge in the Aosta Valley in Italy that dates to the first century BC. The bridge spans 31.4 m across the Betuctelum to the NNW of Eporedia.
A Roman bridge along the Via Appia near Cisterna di Latina.
A late Republican Roman segmented arch bridge in Padua, Italy.
The bridge known as Ponte Amato carries the Via Praenestina near Gallicano nel Lazio.
Ponte Apollosa, a bridge of the Via Appia that may have been restored by Septimius Severus in 198 CE.
A second-century Roman bridge on the Riu Brabaciera.
The bridge known as Ponte Buriano is located at the hamlet of Ponte a Buriano, Italy. The bridge spans the Arno River and dates initially from 1277.
A Roman bridge near Narnia that was built over the river Nera ca. 27 B.C.
A Roman bridge of the Via Flaminia.
A Roman bridge on the Via Cassia.

The Ponte Ceccara, also referred to as the ponte di Terra, is a Republican period bridge located on the fosso di Passerano along the Via Praenestina. It is located within the modern comune of Zagarolo, some 30.5 km southeast of the center of Rome.
A Roman segmental arch bridge across the Bacchiglione river in Padua, Italy.
A Roman bridge along the Via Salaria near Ascoli Piceno.
A Iulio-Claudian bridge located in Ariminum.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Ponte da Isna
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Ponte da Ladeira dos Envendos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A2 Ponte da Marinha
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Ponte da Ribeira das Razas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B1 Ponte da Sancha
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Ponte da Senhora da Azenha

name: Ponte de Nossa Senhora da Guia

props.description: A first-century Roman bridge across the Rio Lima at Ponte de Lima that was rebuilt in part in the fourteenth century.
A Roman bridge located by the Rio Coura.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Ponte de Sor
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B4 Ponte de Tôr
The Roman Bridge of Chaves is also referred to as the Bridge of Trajan. It is dated to the late first or early second century CE.
A bridge over the Simeto River with ancient origins, the so-called Ponte dei Saraceni has been rebuilt several times, including after the 1693 Sicily earthquake.
A bridge in the territory of Blera known as the "Devil's Bridge".
Site of an ancient Roman bridge across the Raganello near Civita. The ancient bridge was largely rebuilt in 2005.
A Roman bridge across the Torrente Gran Caso located south of Ascoli Piceno.
A Roman arched bridge on the Via Flaminia near modern Terni.

The bridge known as the Ponte del Viceré de Ribera crosses the Fosso di Sant'Andrea on the line of the Via Appia. The viceroy of Naples, one Parefàn de Ribera, renovated the bridge in 1568. During the Second World War, the bridge was bombed and was eventually restored in 2005.
An ancient bridge over the Fiora river (the ancient Armenta) near Vulci. Ancient name unknown.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 no. 38 (bridge (Ponte dell’Acqua Nera))
A Roman bridge located near Praeneste.

A bridge over the Metauro River built initially in the thirteenth century. The original bridge was destroyed in 1765 and rebuilt in 1782. The bridge was again destroyed in 1944 and was rebuilt in 1946.
A Medieval bridge across the Serchio River. The bridge was built ca. 1080-1100 and was renovated ca. 1300. It is located along the Via Francigena.

The so-called Ponte della Rocca located on the Via Clodia at Blera is situated adjacent to the Necropolis of San Giovenale and Terrone. Some sources suggest an original date in the third or second centuries BCE.
A Roman bridge of the second century A.D. along the Via Traiana that crosses the torrente Buonalbergo.
A Roman bridge with Augustan origins, known either as Ponte Muto or Ponte delle Vozze, associated with the Via Iulia Augusta and located in the Val Ponci in Liguria.
An arched Roman bridge across the Titerno near Cerreto Sannita in Campania.
A Roman bridge known by a variety of local toponyms that crosses the Savuto river. It is located in the modern Italian municipality of Altilia, Calabria.
A Roman bridge.
A Roman arched bridge located near Narni built originally in the Augustan period that remained in use until A.D. 1055.
A Roman bridge crossed the Volturno river at Capua, some elements of which remain in the site of modern bridge.

name: Ponte di Diocleziano, San Lazzaro di Fossombrone

props.description: The so-called "Ponte di Diocleziano" in the local comune of San Lazzaro di Fossombrone was a Roman bridge along the Via Flaminia in the valley of the Metaurus river. The Roman bridge was completely destroyed during the Second World War.

A Roman bridge that is no longer extant. Quilici notes that the bridge was destroyed both by the Second World War and by urbanization that followed in the post-war period. Blake publishes a photo of E. B. van Deman and indicates that the bridge was built in peperino and of "indeterminate date". She compares the Ponte di Malafede with the construction of the Via Appia viaduct that lies just beyond Ariccia, a structure that was Augustan in its date.
A Roman bridge along the Via Appia.
A Roman-period bridge along the via Praenestina built of lapis Gabinus.
A Roman segmental stone arch bridge located near Augusta Praetoria.
A single-arch Roman bridge built during the principate of Augustus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 B2 unnamed bridge (over *Umbro fl., W Arretium at Pogi)
Remains of a Roman Republican bridge crossing the Fosso di Pratolungo along the line of the via Tiburtina.
The Ponte di Quintodecimo is located near Acquasanta Terme.

name: Ponte di San Giovanni de Butris

props.description: The remains of a Roman bridge along the Via Flaminia that have been incorporated into the thirteenth-century church of San Giovanni de Butris.
An early segmental arch bridge, built between 47 and 30 B.C.
A Roman bridge over Torrente della Ginestra near Aequum Tuticum along the Via Traiana.
A Trajanic bridge along the Via Appia that crosses the fosso Striscia.

name: Ponte di Traiano at Tor Tre Ponti

props.description: A Roman bridge located on the Via Appia at modern Tor Tre Ponti.
The Ponte di Vallerano is a second-century Roman bridge that carried the via Ostiensis across the fosso di Vallerano.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Ponte do Coadouro
The so-called "Ponte Etrusco" is an ancient bridge located near Gallese in the province of Viterbo, Italy.
A Roman bridge crossing the river Temo; the bridge is said to have originally had a five-arch span.
Ponte Ezzu or Pont'Ezzu is a Roman bridge of the first century A.D. that crosses the Rio Mannu near Ozieri, Sardinia.
The so-called "Ponte Fabio Massimo" or "Ponte del Diavolo" originally dates to the third century BC with subsequent reworking and reuse continuing to the Middle Ages.
The Roman bridge at Ponte Fonnaia carried a branch of the Via Flaminia over the river Naia. It is located north of modern Acquasparta in Umbria.
The remains of a late Republican bridge near Benevento (in Località Serretelle).
A Roman bridge dating to the late second century B.C.
A Medieval bridge across the river Trebia that is said to have Roman origins.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 D1-C4 unnamed bridge (on Via Flaminia (20))
The so-called Ponte Ladrone was a Roman bridge of the Via Ostiensis located at Acilia. According to L. Quilici, its remaining material vestiges were buried in 1982 by infrastructure works.
A Roman bridge on the Volturno river located near Capriati.
A bridge of Roman origins crossing the Sabato river on the outskirts of Benevento.
A Roman bridge at Lanuvium.
A Roman bridge over the modern Aniene river adjacent to the Tomb of the Plautii, which is located north of the Villa of Hadrian at Tivoli.

Ponte Mammolo is the modern name of an ancient bridge over the Aniene river. It gives its name to a neighborhood and metro station in the northeast part of the modern city of Rome. Archaeological evidence of ancient quarrying and a settlement have also been found in the vicinity of the bridge.

The Roman bridge known as "Ponte Manlio" carried the Via Flaminia across the Bosso torrent, whose main channel is now some hundreds of meters to the south. This is a well-preserved Roman vaulted arch bridge that dates to the second century B.C.
A Roman segmental arch bridge across the Bacchiglione river in Padua, Italy.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 C2 unnamed bridge (NE Laos, at Mormanno)
A Roman bridge known as ‘Ponte Návea’ located near Puebla de Trives, over Rio Návea.
A bridge along the Via Amerina, just south of ancient Nepet (modern Nepi in Italy)
A Roman bridge over the Aniene river.
A ruined bridge located near Nuoro in Sardinia.

Excavated remains of a Roman bridge located on the modern Via Ponte Romano about 2km southest of Ceggia (Veneto), Italy. Aerial/satellite photographs often show crop marking corresponding to the associated Roman road and now-diverted watercourse spanned by the bridge.

name: Ponte Romano Di Largo Impero, Santa Marinella

props.description: The remains of a Roman bridge located in the comune of Santa Marinella.

name: Ponte Romano di Parco dei Medici

props.description: The ruins of a second-century A.D. Roman bridge at Magliana Vecchia.
A Roman bridge dating between the first century BC and the first century AD.
An ancient bridge located to the east of ancient Teanum Sidicinum (modern Teano in Italy).
The ruins of a Roman bridge located in the Viale Roma, Santa Marinella, Italy.
An ancient bridge, now known as "Ponte Romito" crosses the Arno River to the west of Ponte a Buriano.
An ancient bridge over the Cervaro river between Aecae and Herdoniae on the Via Traiana.

A much-repaired medieval bridge crossing the Aufidus (modern Ofanto) river. Long a traditional candidate location for the "Pons Aufidi" shown on the Peutinger map (thus BAtlas), recent survey work has found no evidence in the area consistent with a Roman-era bridge. The Pietra Dell'Oglio bridge (further south on the Ofanto) is of Roman date and now a more likely candidate. The Ponte S. Venere remains in use today for the Strada Statale 303.
The Ponte Salario is a Medieval bridge with Roman origins. It is located at the point where the Via Salaria crosses the Anio, about 3 km north of the Colline Gate.
A Roman bridge, perhaps originally dating to the first century BC, was substantially transformed to carry a provincial road in 1872.
A bridge that leads to the villa of Vibius Varius.
A Roman bridge located near Narni, Italy.
An ancient Roman bridge located along the Via Valeria near Roviano, Italy.
A bridge near Ferentinum (modern Ferentino in Italy) that is traditionally dated to A.D. 260 or after. A substructure in polygonal masonry supports the bridge.

The Ponte Sisto is a bridge that spans the Tiber River at Rome. In the present-day city, it links the Via dei Pettinari (Rione of Regola) to the Piazza Trilussa in Trastevere. The bridge was commissioned by Pope Sixtus IV and was built between 1473 and 1479. Its architect, Baccio Pontelli, reused the piers of the ancient Roman Pons Aurelius, which had come to be known as the Pons Antoninus in Late Antiquity. That bridge was at least partially destroyed in the 770s.
The so-called Ponte Spiano is a Roman bridge located along the Via Flaminia in the modern comune of Sigillo.
A Roman bridge on the Via Amerina between ancient Nepet and Falerii.
A bridge of Roman origins located near Montesarchio.
A Roman bridge located east of Benevento, Italy, and built by Flavius Iulius Valens.
Remains of a Roman bridge over the modern Eyrieux river in the French commune of Saint-Fortunat-sur-Eyrieux.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Porto da Calçadinha

The remains of a Roman bridge known locally as Puente Viejo or Puente de Cañete lying one mile west west of Obulco on the Arroyo Salado. The bridge may have been associated with a conjectured secondary Roman road or route between Corduba and Catulo because although unmentioned in the Antonine Itinerary, the bridge's location lies between two mentioned stations: Calpurniana and Vircao.
Known locally as the Puente Romano or Puente Viejo, this single-arched bridge crosses the modern Río Guadalimar at Puente de Génave in Andalusia, Spain.
A Roman bridge located near the hamelt of Nestar, over the Rio Rubagón.
A Roman bridge at the modern village of Oieregi in Spain's Navarre province. It spans the modern Baidasoa river.
The Puente del Bibey or ‘Ponte Bibei’ crosses the river Bibei at Mendoya.
A Medieval bridge spanning the Jucar stream.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 unnamed bridge (Pont del Diable (Martorell) over R. Llobregat, SE (Ad) Fines)
A Roman bridge over Rio Tormes in Salamanca, Spain.
A Roman bridge, known as the 'Puente Mocho', crossing the ribera de Cañedo.
A Roman bridge located between Chiclana and Beas de Segura, S Mariana.
A five span stone bridge (Talamanca over R. Jarama, W Arriaca).
Relocated remains of a 300m-long Roman stone bridge originally located southwest of modern Tourmogon at Alconétar, over R. Tajo in Spain.

Remains of ancient lime adjacent to the modern Huerva river at Villadoz in spain have been interpreted as a Roman bridge, likely associated with a road or route between Sermonae and Carae, two road stations attested in Roman itineraries.
A bridge, originally of Roman date, over the modern Arroyo Rabanales (a tributary of the Guadalquivir) just east of Cordoba, Spain. The bridge was in use as late as the 1920s.
A bridge of the sixteenth century that rests on Roman foundations.
A bridge of Roman origin in Palencia, Spain, that was remodeled in the fifteenth century.
A Roman bridge located in the Provincia da Coruña, Galicia, Spain.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C1 Ribeira das Taliscas
A Roman bridge over the river Platano, east of Volcei known alternately as Ponte del Diavolo/Ponte di Annibale.
A Roman bridge over the river Tâmega at Aquae Flaviae (modern Chaves, Portugal).
An unnamed Roman bridge just east of ancient Platea at modern Paracuellos de Jíloca over the river Jíloca in Spain.
A hypothesized Roman bridge over the river Arly just above its confluence with the Isére at modern Albertville in France.
Remains of a bridge located near Aperlae.
A fifth-century Roman bridge over the river Nivelle at Ascain in France.
An unnamed ancient bridge, located in modern Buonfornello, Sicily.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 E3 unnamed bridge (at Clastidium)
Structural remains of a Roman bridge located on the River Rede in the vicinity of Habitancum Roman fort. The bridge lay along the ancient Watling Street.

name: Roman Bridge at Moulin de Boisseron

props.description: Ancient name unknown. Located in modern Boisseron, France.
An ancient Roman bridge along the Via Aurelia.
A bridge constructed across the Danube at the Roman fort of Valeriana.
The so-called "Pont Tibère" in the modern French commune of Villevieille, in Sommières canton, France. Its ancient name is unknown.
A Roman bridge over the Torrente Marmore at Châtillon in the Valle d'Aosta, Italy.
A bridge across the Minho River at Lugo with Roman origins. Heavily restored in modern times, the bridge now serves as a pedestrian walkway.
A Roman bridge built across the Cillian stream in the Valle d'Aosta, Italy.
The remains of a Roman bridge that once crossed the river known as Uso are located at San Vito, Italy, in the environs of Rimini.

name: Roman Bridge over Calycadnus at Seleucia ad Calycadnum

props.description: Epigraphic evidence, uncovered in the 19th century, indicates the presence of this bridge, built at the direction of the Emperor Vespasian and his two sons Titus and Domitian by the Cilician governor L. Octavius ​​Memor in 77 or 78 CE. The inscription, now lost, was reportedly found during construction of the current Taşköprü ("Stone Bridge") in the early 1870s. This 19th century bridge is often referred to as the "Roman Stone Bridge," despite its modernity.
Remains of a Roman bridge, north of Makopsisa (modern Macomer in Sardinia).
A first century Roman bridge over the Guadalquivir river.
The Roman bridge (Puente romano) at Mérida spans the Guadiana River. It is included as part of the UNESCO World Heritage Site "Archaeological Ensemble of Mérida".
An unnamed bridge located west of Hybla Gereatis at Pietralunga di Paternò, over the river Simeto.

name: Roman Bridge: Le Vistre de la Fontaine Palaeochannel

props.description: Remains of a Roman bridge spanning a former channel of Le Vistre de la Fontaine south of Nîmes have been excavated. Work at the site also provided significant evidence for Roman-era waste and lead contamination transported down the river from the urban center of Nemausus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 no. 32 (bridges at Tibur (2))

The so-called "Archi di San Lidano" are the remains of a Roman stone bridge in the Pontine plain, near the modern locality of Sezze Scalo. The bridge likely dates originally to the second century B.C. Evidence of an ancient sanctuary have also been detected in the vicinity.
A segmental arch bridge on the Via Domitia in southern France, typically dated to the reign of Augustus.
Ancient tuff quarries and a bridge at Salone on the Anio River.
A Roman bridge crossing the Melfa river in Lazio, Italy.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 52 H4 Sarıcakaya
A Roman bridge on the Rubicon at Savignano sul Rubicone.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D2 Segura
Sextantio was a Celtic settlement at a ford on the Ledus fl. and later became a Gallo-Roman town.
An unnamed Roman bridge at modern Silves in Portugal's Algarve region. The bridge spans the Arade river.
A Roman bridge over the Truentus river.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C2 Sôrinho
An arched bridge that could be of Roman date, but also possibly Medieval.
A two-arched, stone-built footbridge across the Passer in Merano, South Tyrol.
A Roman bridge along the Via Claudia Augusta.
Tarr Steps is a prehistoric bridge over the River Barle in England. The current phase dates to the Medieval period, although the original phase of the bridge is dated ca. 1000 BC.
A large Roman bridge at Adana in the modern country of Turkey. It spans the Psaros/Sinarus/Koiranos river (modern Seyhan).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 unlocated Tautaendia
Tenedo lies at the confluence of the Aare and the Rhine. In pre-Roman times, a key trade route crossed the River Rhine here.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Terrugem
An unnamed bridge located north of Theveste (modern Tébessa, Algeria) on a Roman road between Theveste and Ammaedara (modern Haidra).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 86 C2 Tios/Tieion
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C3 Torre del Padiglione

The remains of a Roman bridge at Tramatza cross the Riu di Mare Foghe. Gosner and Nowlin assert that this bridge likely dates to the first century BCE and that its construction may have allowed for better access to the Sardinian interior in the direction of Forum Traiani (Fordongianus). The bridge was repaired in the Middle Ages and again in 1826.
An unnamed bridge over the Ir(i)a fl., north of Libarna.
An unnamed bridge over the Nure fl., southeast of Placentia.
The remains of a bridge of Byzantine or Turkish construction located northwest of Sardis.
