name: Untitled Villa on the island of Igilium

props.description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 41 D5 unnamed villa (Igilium Ins.)

This ornately-decorated, elevated building might have been the residence of Sammu-ramat (the legendary Semiramis) in the palace of her son Adad-nārārī III (r. 810-783 BC) at Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud). Because these rooms were built noticeably higher than the surrounding area, its first excavator Austen Henry Layard believed that he had uncovered part of an upper floor of an Assyrian palace and, therefore, referred to this area as the “Upper Chambers.”
A Roman bath structure of the fourth century A.D. whose remains were reused to construct the Medieval church of San Lorenzo.
A Roman villa site near Rieti known initially from several cryptoporticus structures. More recent excavations have revealed a series of rooms, including some with mosaic pavement.
A villa, vicus, and necropolis site in modern Puglia, Italy, located near the modern city of Gravina. The site was active from the first to fourth centuries CE.

Architectural remains consistent with a Roman-era villa maritima have been identified on both the north and south sides of a small bay at modern Valbandon in Croatia. Originally thought to represent a single establishment that wrapped the entire bay, more recent interpretations would see two separate architectural complexes.
The Roman villa of Valdonega near Verona dates to the first century A.D.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C3 Vale de Aguieiro
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C4 Vale de Aguilhão
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B4 Vale de Mértola
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 Valerio Messala Corvino
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Variacum?
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B5 Vatsa
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A3 Vendres
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E3 Vendresse
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 47 F4 Ventrelli
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Ver-lès-Chartres
A Roman villa.
The Gallo-Roman villa is located at Bufosse in the northern part of the commune Verneuil-en-Halatte département Oise.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G4 Veroli
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 39 I1 Vettianus Fundus
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Vettweiss-Froitzheim
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 Via delle Piagge
A Roman villa complex that was occupied beginning in the first century BCE.
A large, Roman imperial estate covering ca. 2,000 ha on the shores of Lake Bracciano. The name may derive from Vicus Aurelius, suggesting a connection with Marcus Aurelius.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 E4 Vicus Antoniae Saturninae
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Vieux-Rouen
In 1834, Edward Dodwell documented a substantial tract of polygonal masonry walling at Bevilacqua near Frascati that was thought to be associated with a Roman villa platform.

A Roman villa located at Vigna Spinabella and included in the Barrington Atlas. In the Barrington Atlas directory, this place is included as a "Numbered Feature" and not given a specific title. It is assigned number 44 on BAtlas map 43 C2.
A Roman villa, mansio, and Late Antique settlement along the Via Aemilia Scauri.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B2 Vila Cardílio
A Roman villa located in Čapljina, Bosnia and Herzegovina.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 A3 Vila Verde
The villa dates from the first century B.C. to the third century A.D. when it was transformed into a bath complex. It was partially reclaimed as a villa in late Roman and Medieval times.
A Roman and Visigothic villa located in the modern town of Camós in Girona province, Cataluña, Spain.

The Villa Aldobrandini is located on the outskirts of Frascati. The current villa of the site was built in 1550 by a Vatican prelate named Alessandro Rufini. Subsequently, Pope Clement VIII gave the villa to his nephew Cardinal Pietro Aldobrandini in 1598. The Papal villa and gardens occupy the site of a Roman terrace villa.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Villa Aniciorum
The remains of a Roman platform villa, the first phase of which belongs to the first century BCE.
The so-called "Villa Arianna" at Stabiae is typically considered one of the earliest villas at the site and is often dated to the second century B.C.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 no. 121 (Foz de Lumbier (Liédena))

The so-called “Villa of Augustus” at Somma Vesuviana was first discovered in the late nineteenth century. The villa has been shown to have survived the 79 CE eruption of Mt. Vesuvius but was eventually destroyed by a volcanic event in 472.
Remains of a Roman villa located in the modern Torres Novas Municipality, Santarém District, Portugal.
A villa rustica of about 400 square meters that was discovered at Stabiae in 1963. The site was reinterred in 1998.
Remains of a Roman villa discovered in the Collatino archaeological park in Via Grotta di Gregna consisting of 14 rooms, a cistern, and channelization for water works.

A villa on Lake Como described by Pliny the Younger. Scholars have long thought it must be located in the vicinity of modern Lierna, and 19th and 20th century finds of floor mosaics and other elements have led to conjectures that the villa must have been located either in the peninsular "Castello" neighborhood or in the core of the old town, which has long been known as "Villa" because of this conjecture. A mosaic from the latter area is now conserved in the Archaeological Museum of Lecco.
The so-called Villa Cuomo in the modern Italian comune of Sant'Antonio Abate in greater metropolitan Naples is a Roman villa rustica of the first century CE.
A Roman villa with mosaic pavements located in the modern Spanish province of Malaga.

name: Villa de Santa Catarina de Sítimos

props.description: A Roman villa of the imperial period, in use from the first through fifth centuries A.D.
The so-called 'Villa of the Antonines', located near Genzano di Roma in the Alban Hills, lies just south of the Via Appia between the road's 18th and 19th milestones.

The Villa dei Casoni is sometimes referred to as the Villa di Varrone, based on some scholarly assumptions that it once belonged to Marcus Terentius Varro. This late republican to early imperial platform villa is located near Poggio Mirteto, Montopoli di Sabina, Italy.

A monumental villa complex that is known as the “Villa dei Quattro Venti” is situated on a south-eastern offshoot of the Circeo promontory, approximately 100 meters southeast of the historic center of S. Felice Circeo.

The so-called "Villa of the Jurist" was discovered in 2013 during salvage excavations connected with the expansion of Italy's A24 motorway. A significant villa of the Late Republican period was documented and attributed to the jurist Quintus Mucius Scaevola.
A Roman villa located at the 1.6 km post of the via Tiberina, positioned on a tuff bank, today inside the Prima Porta cemetery.

name: Villa del Cortijo de los Vila at Alameda

props.description: Roman villa at Alameda, located near the modern town cemetery. It is also known as the “Finca del Moral”.
A Roman villa located in the contrada Giuliana at Boscoreale on a side street of via Grotta II. The site was excavated by Vincenzo De Prisco in 1897 and subsequently reinterred.
A large villa at Stabiae covering more than 17,000 square meters.

The Forma Italiae notes the presence of drainage channels, foundations, and a cistern that are taken to be indicative of a Roman villa in the locale known today as Torrenova in the eastern portion of modern Rome.
A large villa rustica inhabited from ca. 100 B.C. to A.D. 500, located near Montelupo Fiorentino in the mid-Arno Valley of northern Tuscany, approximately twenty km west of Florence.
An ancient villa in Guidonia Montecelio, Lazio.
The ruins of a Roman villa rustica located near modern Giardino di Roma, just north of Via Cristoforo Colombo southwest of Rome.

A Roman villa rustica at Boscoreale that was first discovered in 1868 by Modestino Pulzella. The villa is best known as the findspot of the so-called "Boscoreale Treasure". Following excavation, the remains of the villa were reinterred.
A late Republican and early Imperial villa in the ager Cosanus noted for its turreted enclosure wall.
A villa with beginnings in the early Republican period, continuing in use to the middle Imperial period.

name: Villa delle Terme degli Stucchi Dipinti

props.description: An imperial villa, perhaps abandoned in the late first century A.D., located near Tor Vergata.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 no. 110 (Els Munts (Altafulla))

A villa complex at the eighth mile of the Via Cassia, to which it was linked by means of a paved diverticulum. The first phase dates to the first century BCE and the villa remained in use until the Late Antique period.

name: Villa di Faonte a Vigne Nuove

props.description: The so-called the Villa of Faonte was a Roman villa in Rome located between Via delle Vigne Nuove and Via Passo del Turchino. There are remains of a cistern (approx. 29.5 by 14 meters) now set in a garden area.
A Late Roman villa site near Ausculum.
A Roman villa dating ca. 50 B.C. located near Monte Gennaro.
The remains of the so-called villa of Fralana belong to the villa rustica type. Mosaic floors have been discovered at the site, in addition to a productive area that may have included a Torcularium.
The so-called Villa di Grotte di Piastra, ruins of a Roman villa at Castelporziano.
A Roman villa located near Castel di Guido.
Nero built a villa and a port at Antium. The villa was subsequently expanded under Domitian and other later emperors.
A Roman nymphaeum dating ca. A.D. 50-55.

The ruins of a Roman villa rustica were discovered in the 1990s along via Diego Fabbri. The site dates to the first century B.C. and remained in use at least until the fifth century A.D. The remains of a bath complex are substantial.

The Villa di Rufione is a villa rustica located along the via Flaminia Vetus, between the hamlets of Montecchio and Bastardo in località Toccioli. The site begins its life in the late republican period and continues in use until the fourth century CE.

A palatial Roman villa and bath complex that has been connected with Theodoric the Great, with twentieth century interpretations labeling it as Theodoric's "hunting lodge". More recent excavations have uncovered evidence for earlier building phases, dating from the first century B.C. through the third century A.D.

name: Villa di Tor Vergata, località Carcaricola

props.description: An important villa that was excavated during the construction of the University of Rome 'Tor Vergata' in the 1970s, now reinterred. The Carcaricola villa begins in the middle of the second century B.C. and continues in use until the second century A.D.
The so-called Villa of Vibius Varus is located south of the Villa Adriana at Tivoli. The site has three architectural phases stretching from the first century BCE to the fourth century CE

A Roman-period urban 'villa' at Knossos was excavated by R. W. Hutchinson (1935) and M. R. E. Gough (post-World War II). Based on pottery evidence, the villa dates to the second century CE and comes to be destroyed ca. 170-180.
Villa Domitiani, a large imperial villa near the Alban Lake, now the site of the Papal retreat at Castel Gandolfo.
The remains of a fortified Roman villa.

A Roman villa 300 meters east northeast of the eastern tip of Laguna Cernea, north of the modern village Santa Colomba de Somoza. Constructed under Tiberius, it was likely the residence of the official in charge of gold extraction.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 B2 Villa Esmeade
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E2 Villa Euchelia
A Roman villa rustica located on the river Cinca that is richly adorned with mosaic pavements.
A Roman villa at Puente Genil in the province of Córdoba in Spain.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C1 Villa Fulgurita
A third century A.D. villa complex along the Via Praenestina connected with Gordian I, Gordian II, and Gordian III.
The Villa of Hadrian (Villa Adriana in Italian) is a sprawling archaeological complex at Tivoli, Italy. The villa complex was designed and used by the emperor Hadrian in the second century A.D.
A Roman villa at Boscoreale discovered in 1986 and only partially excavated.

A villa site that was active from the middle Republican period to the early Middle Ages was investigated in 2009 and 2010 at via Enzo Tortora, no. 79. The site is located about 500 meters east of the line of the ancient Via Flaminia.
A 7,000 m² palatial villa built atop the island of Capreae by the Roman emperor Tiberius.
A large villa complex that was built sometime in the first century B.C.E. and remained in use until at least the third century C.E.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 D1 Villa Magna
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D3 Villa Magna Variana

name: Villa Maritima di Capo di Sorrento

props.description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F4 Capo di Sorrento
A maritime villa of the first century A.D. adjacent to the port of Cosa.
A coastal villa and associated necropolis.

The Villa Muti in Grottaferrata occupies, at least in part, the site of a Roman republican villa built on a series of terraces and likely dating to the third and second centuries B.C. Lanciani excavated a series of rooms at the site. The site also had an early to mid-Imperial phase that included elaborate floors and decoration in imported marble.
An ancient villa near the Wadi Ghirza
An ancient villa near the Wadi Ghirza
An ancient villa near the Wadi Ghirza
The Villa Neronis was a rural villa of the emperor Nero, likely built early in his reign (perhaps before AD 60).
The so-called Villa (or baths) of Agrippa on Planasia Ins. (modern Pianosa) is the putative location of exile for Agrippa Postumus, grandson of Augustus.

The Villa of Agrippa Postumus was discovered at Boscotrecase in 1903 on land owned by Cavaliere Ernesto Santini. Works for the Circumvesuviana railroad line brought the site to light. Excavations supervised by Matteo Della Corte were carried out from 1903 to 1905. In 1906, however, a new eruption of Mount Vesuvius reburied the site. Della Corte published a plan of the excavated portion of the villa in the Notizie degli scavi di antichità.

A Roman villa excavated at Boscoreale in 1903 and 1904 under the supervision of Vincenzo De Prisco and located on the property of one Giuseppe De Martino. Wall paintings and artifacts were recovered from the site, and the villa was subsequently reinterred. Records as to its precise location are less than optimal.

name: Villa of Caesar and Maxentius at San Cesareo

props.description: The so-called Villa of Caesar and Maxentius at San Cesareo is located at the 18th mile of the Via Labicana.

A 'Villa rustica' associated with one Caius Olius Ampliatus, located in the Ponticelli of Naples, Italy. The site was excavated in 1986-1987, with additional work in 2007. The site covers some 2,000 sq. meters.

A suburban villa of Pompeii located outside the Herculaneum gate, the Villa of Diomedes was excavated between 1771 and 1774 and named after the tomb of Marcus Arrius Diomedes which faces the entrance on the opposite side of the road.

A Roman villa associated with a Caius Furius Octavianus who was a member of the Senatorial order during the Severan period. The villa was identified along the via Aurelia in the locality of Massimina - Casal Lumbroso. Excavations were carried out between 2011 and 2017.

A well-appointed "villa" at Olynthus excavated in 1934, the so-called "Villa of Good Fortune" was considered one of the two "type" houses for the ancient site. It was located in the southeastern part of the city in the so-called "villa quarter". Its remains are no longer visible.
The archaeological remains of a villa near Licenza, Italy, that are traditionally connected with the Latin poet Horace.
The Villa of L. Crassius Tertius at Oplontis (modern Torre Annunziata in Italy) was discovered in 1974, approximately 250m east of the Villa of Poppaea.

name: Villa of Lucius Nonius Calpurnius Torquatus Asprenas

props.description: The remains of a Roman villa on the Via Ostiense have been linked to Lucius Nonius Calpurnius Torquatus Asprenas.

Often inaccurately called "the castellum of Nador", the monumental stone facade and entry of this late Roman farmstead still stands on the south side of Algerian Route nationale 11 in the municipal territory of Nador. The site was extensively excavated in the late 20th century and, among other finds, yielded (in conditions of re-use) a partial milestone with Roman imperial titulature dateable to 312/313 CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 30 D3 no. 6 (Villa of M. Hortensius Gaudentius, S Tipasa)
The Villa of Manius Antoninus at Nicopolis.

A villa site near Tivoli that has long been associated Manlius Vopiscus, who Statius mentions. Excavations began in 1825. In 1835 Pope Gregory XVI began construction of his eponymous Villa Gregoriana on the grounds of the Roman villa.
A Roman villa along the via Herculea near Oppido Lucano.
The Villa of Numerius Popidius Florus dates to the early first century B.C. It was modified several times before its destruction in A.D. 79.

The Villa of Publius Fannius Synistor was built after the middle of the first century B.C. and was ultimately destroyed by the eruption of Mt. Vesuvius. The villa may have been owned by P. Fannius Synistor and/or L. Herennius Florus.
Referred to both as the House and the Villa of the frescoes, this seaside Roman domestic complex with a peristyle garden covered more than 1,000 square feet and dates to the second century A.D.
A Bronze Age house at Amnisos, Crete.
A Roman villa located northwest of Pompeii.
The archaeological remains of a Roman villa on the hill of San Cesareo are speculatively identified as belonging to the gens Octavia, according to Della Portella 2004 op. cit.
A suburban villa located northwest of Herculaneum that was originally owned by Lucius Calpurnius Piso Caesoninus.

name: Villa on the Property of D'Acunzo

props.description: A villa rustica excavated and reinterred in 1903, located near the Boscoreale train station.
Jones describes this place as the site of an 'imperial stud farm' that produced race horses.
A villa rustica excavated by the École française de Rome between 1979 and 1984.
An ancient suburban villa outside of Spoletium, occupied from the Augustan period to the third century A.D. The remains are on the grounds of the modern Villa Redenta complex.
A villa rustica at Boscoreale discovered in 1977. This is the only villa site currently visible and has been subjected to more modern excavation and analysis.
A Roman villa first occupied in the Late Republican period and abandoned ca. the fifth century AD.
The site of a Roman villa that was discovered in 1904 in the Balácapuszta field of Vámos village in western Hungary.

The site of Villaricos lies 5 km east of Mula (Murcia), Spain. The Roman villa at the site was large and well-appointed, including rich mosaics and a bathing complex. There is also an industrial area that includes a torcularium (facility for pressing of olives or grapes). The site's use ranges from the first to the fifth century CE.
A villa with polychrome mosaics, dating from the first to the fifth centuries. It is located at modern Salar, in the Spanish province of Granada.
A Roman "villa rustica" with an olive-oil press (torcularium, hence "Torchio") was discovered in 2006.
A villa dating to the final quarter of the first century BC located on a headland overlooking Portoferraio on the island of Elba.

name: Villa romana di Acque Fresche a Civitavecchia

props.description: Ruins of a Roman coastal villa located at Acque Fresche in the comune of Civitavecchia.
A villa site that includes a bathing complex, dating from the second to fourth centuries CE.
A Roman villa of the first century CE located near Farchetto, Italy.
A villa rustica located in the Sabina, ca. 1 km from Cottanello (Rieti). The imperial villa was superimposed over a villa of the republican period.

name: Villa Romana di Punta San Limato

props.description: The ruins of a Roman villa located in the modern Italian locality of Punta San Limato, province of Caserta.
The partially preserved structural remains of an Imperial villa dating to the third and fourth centuries.
A Roman villa dating to the second century A.D.

The Villa Rufinella stands on the site of a terraced Roman villa. The site occupies one of the most commanding positions on the slope above what is now Frascati, placing it in the environs of ancient Tusculum.
A so-called Roman "Villa Rustica" covering some 5,200 square meters located at the Szabadtéri Néprajzi Múzeum (Skanzen), Szentendre.
Ruins of a villa rustica, located south of modern Brombach in Baden-Württemberg.
A Roman villa of the third and fourth centuries A.D.
The Villa San Marco originates in the Augustan period and was subsequently expanded during the reign of Claudius. Its owner may have been a freedman of Claudius.
A coastal villa in the 'contrada Sora' area of Torre del Greco, a district with a great many archaeological remains.
A coastal villa at Sperlonga associated with the Roman emperor Tiberius.

The extant Villa Torlonia occupies the site of an ancient Roman villa, although, as Marzano notes, the interpretation of this villa and its Roman antecedents has been (and continues) to be confused in scholarship, particularly with reference to villas associated with Lucullus.
A Roman villa that has now been reconstructed.
A large imperial Roman villa known from letters of Marcus Aurelius.
A Roman villa.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 E4 Villar de Brenes

Since antiquity, the 6,000-ha estate at Castelporziano has been an elite seaside compound. Where a modern pleasure villa for the Italian president now sits are remains of Roman villas on the Laurentine shore; these remains have been the object of continued study since 1983.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C2 no. 43 (villas on Via Castrimeniense)

Located 3 km south of the town of Auxerre, France, on the right bank of the Yonne River, archaeologists excavated the remains of an extensive Gallo-Roman villa covering an area of approximately 1.6 hectares. The villa site includes a bath complex. The site has been noted for its archaeological potential since the nineteenth century. While some excavations took place in the 1960s, the open-area excavation of the villa was announced in 2025.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 C3 Villepey
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C2 Villers-sous-Ailly
A villa rustica was excavated at Vittimose in 1969 and 1970.
Remains of a Roman villa.
An extensive maritime villa of the Roman period occupied the western and southern coasts of the Vižula peninsula, located near modern Medulin in Croatia.
A Roman villa commonly associated with Q. Voconius Pollio that was excavated in the nineteenth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 F2 Voerendaal
A villa associated with the Volusii Saturnini built ca. 50 B.C.

Vouni is located on the northwest coast of Cyprus, some 7 km west of Soloi. The rocky hill was occupied in antiquity and the settlement included a fortified palace enclosure, the ancient name of which is unknown. According to PECS, the palace is to be dated ca. 500 to 380 BCE, with two main architectural phases (I: ca. 500 to 450 BCE, II: ca. 450 to 380 BCE).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Ajdab
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi el-Amud
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Wadi Gobbeen
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Wadi Khanafes
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Wadi Mansur
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Merdum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Meseuggi
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Wadi Mimoun
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G2 Wadi Mimoun Darragh
Wadi Nafed or Nefed
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G2 Wadi Sasu
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Wadi Scetaf
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Sofeggin
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Wadi Tessa
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 G3 Wadi Umm el-Kharab
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G1 Walesby
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Walton-on-the-Hill
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E2 Wancennes
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 H3 Wanstead
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Warfusée-Abancourt
A series of villa rustica located along the road to Cambodunum.
A likely villa site known mostly from membra disiecta (chiefly mosaics) as well as the exposure of a bath block and a corridor.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Wellow
Remains of a first through fourth-century corridor villa were excavated at Wendens Ambo in 1853 and 1971-3. The villa had a detached bathhouse and other out buildings.
A villa rustica at modern Wesseling.
An aisled farmhouse dating to the third century and measuring ca. 35 x 15 m.
The site of a third to fourth century Romano-British villa. This is also the findspot of a bronze statuette of Mars and an bronze ansate plate inscribed with a dedication to Mar Rigisamus (RIB 187).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F3 West Dean
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 West Langton
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G4 West Marden
A small Romano-British courtyard villa was excavated at Lippen Wood, West Meon, by A. Moray-Williams in 1906. The villa had 16 rooms, three of which had mosaic floors.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 9 G6 Wharram-le-Street
A 4th-century CE Roman corridor villa near Nunney, United Kingdom.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 D4 White Staunton
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 F3 Whittington
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 Whittlebury
Whitton was the site of a pre-Roman fortified farmstead that gave way to a humble Roman farm. Abandoned ca. A.D. 350.

Whitton Lodge is the site of a later Prehistoric settlement enclosure that developed into a Roman villa. The site was identified in the twentieth century by means of cropmarks. Nothing is visible at the surface today.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Wick

There are remains of a Roman villa at Windmill Hill. Artifact evidence indicates human activity from the second century until the late fourth century CE. The site was also likely occupied earlier in the Iron Age.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 I3 Wingham
A "villa rustica" located at Seeb is situated near the village of Winkel, canton of Zurich, Switzerland. By the first century CE, the villa included a thermal bath.
A Roman villa rustica.
Winterton is the site of a Roman villa and field system. The ancient site, now invisible, is located just to the north of Scunthorpe, Lincolnshire U.K.
The Roman-British corridor villa at Withington was discovered in 1811. The site is located to the south of Withington village along with another Romano-British building/villa complex.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Wittlich
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Woodchester
Excavations of this villa site revealed mosaics, the remains of baths, and a corridor centered around a courtyard.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Worplesdon
A Roman villa built in the second century CE and excavated in the 1980s and the 1990s near Wortley, United Kingdom.
A ruined Romano-British villa located near Hitchin, Hertfordshire, England, in the valley of the River Purwell.
A corridor villa, occupied by A.D. 200, that was part of a complex of buildings arranged around a courtyard.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E4 Yeovil
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 M2 Yubileynoye 1
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 L2 Yubileynoye 2
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 D3 Yvonand
A Minoan site in eastern Crete and the location of a Bronze Age palatial center.
At Zaouia de Sidi-Moussa, a stone-built terrace, ancient structures, and cisterns provide evidence for a large ancient villa.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 B1 Zaviet el-Gsur
At Zou are the remains of a palatial building of the Minoan period, built during MM IIIB.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C2 Zoufaques
The ruins of the Sabaean temple dedicated to Almaqah and its associated burial ground are located near the town of Mārib, Yemen.
A Ghassulian public building dating ca. 3500 B.C. near the oasis of Ein Gedi on the western shore of the Dead Sea.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 [Per-ousekh]
Roman fort near present-day Carrawburgh.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 B3 *Calanta
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 49 E1 *Karistoron

"Sioua" is the Greek reconstruction of an ancient toponym (found in Egyptian sources: S·t-wȝḥ) that encompassed some part of the western end of the Dakhla Oasis in Egypt (certainly the site now known as Deir al-Haggar was included, and possibly also Amheida).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 5 D4 *Templum Augusti

The site of two temples constructed by Ramesses II, one dedicated to the triad of Amun, Ra-Horakhty, and Ptah, as well as the deified Ramesses, and another dedicated to Hathor and the pharaoh's wife Nefertari. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.
As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Adad.

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Adad and the goddess Šala. Although its location is not known, the Adad-Šala temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.
The temple sacred to the god Apollo on the Palatine Hill in Rome.
The Temple of Ceres, located on the slope of the Aventine Hill in Rome, was originally dedicated by Sp. Cassius in 494/3 B.C.
A temple dedicated to Faunus located on Tiber Island, the Aedes Fauni was dedicated in 196 BCE by Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus and Gaius Scribonius Libo.
A temple of Hercules located near the Circus Maximus.

While consul in 311 BCE, Caius Iunius Bubulcus Brutus vowed the temple of Salus; he would later dedicate it as dictator in 302 BCE. While no physical remains of the temple are known, textual and epigraphic testimony suggest that it would have been located near the present-day Piazza del Quirinale.

Caracalla dedicated a temple to Serapis in Regio VI. Its dedication is confirmed via epigraphic elements and some of its architectural elements are also known. It was located on the Collis Latiaris, which is the southern most part of the Quirinal Hill.
A temple on Rome's Capitoline Hill where the carts that carried the exuviae in festival parades were stored.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C3 Ag. Demetrios
Ayia Irini, located on Keos, was occupied from the Late Neolithic until the Mycenaean period. The site was extensively excavated under J.L. Caskey.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Ioannis Rodakis
The Doric temple at Agios Elias, above Asea in Arcadia, dates to ca. 500 B.C.
The agora of the ancient city of Halaesa (second century B.C.), now within the archaeological area of Alesa Arconidea, Sicily.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 G3 Agueneb
Sanctuary of the Roman period

The Ain Dara temple, an archaeological site located near the village of Ain Dara, in Afrin, Syria, was the location of an Iron Age Syro-Hittite temple. The structure was noted for the similarities it bore to Solomon's Temple in Jerusalem. The site was in use from ca. 1300 until 740 BCE. It finds comparison with the temples at Emar, Mumbaqa, and Ebla's Temple D. In January 2018 the ruins of the temple were substantially damaged by air strikes carried out by the Turkish Air Force during the Afrin offensive. The site also subsequently suffered further damage, wanton destruction, and looting.
Ain Hersha is the site of a Roman temple located in the environs of Mount Hermon.
An ancient place, cited: None

Temple in Babylon, not located, but probably not far from Eḫursagtila, for it is mentioned together in the ritual texts of the "Divine Love Lyrics". Šarrat-Nippuri "The Queen of Nippur" was a hypostasis of Ištar. It is difficult to ascertain the precise reason for the existence of an Akītu temple dedicated to this deity in the city of Babylon.

The New Year’s House (or Akītu Temple) of Nineveh's tutelary deity Ištar appears to date back at least to the Middle Assyrian period (reign of Tukultī-Ninurta I). Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources. This temple's exact location remains unknown and it is certain whether Ištar's Akītu Temple was located in the citadel or in the lower town.
Akragas/Agrigentum was an ancient city of Sicily and is now known as Agrigento. The Archaeological Area of Agrigento was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1997.

The Roman temple of Alcántara is a well-preserved structure located at one side of the Alcántara Bridge in Extremadura (Spain). The building, dating to A.D. 103, is the work of Caius Iulius Lacer. The lintel inscription indicates the structure was dedicated to Trajan.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D5 Allan
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Allonzier-la-Caille
A seventh- and eighth-century BC Punic sanctuary on the Awali river dedicated to Eshmun, the Phoenician god of healing.

name: Altar at the temple of Amphiaraos

props.description: An altar located to the east of the temple of Amphiaraos.
The Siwa Oasis was, in antiquity, the site of the oracular temple of Amun (Zeus Ammon).

The Anaktoron is an enigmatic megalithic building associated with the prehistoric Sicilian settlement of Pantalica; its polygonal blocks may date to the 12-11th c. B.C., but the current form of the building seems to be the result of Byzantine reconstruction.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D2 Andrianton
A Minoan temple (?) located on northern end of Mount Iuktas.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Angdisseon, T.
The principal temple of Angkor, the capital city of the Khmer empire, Angkor Wat was constructed in the 12th century CE. Angkor was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1992.

A sacred precinct at the western end of Villa Hadriani, comprising two temples at opposite (north and south) ends of a plaza or garden, and (on the western edge of this complex) a small colonnaded hemicycle. Here, Hadrian may have planned to build a large, semi-circular temple-tomb for Antinous, in place of which his successors built the colonnaded hemicycle.

Located outside the city walls of Pergamon, this temple, according to an inscription (OGIS 273-9), was supposedly the site of a victory of Attalos I over the Tolistogi, Galatians, and Antiochus Hierax. It was likely later destroyed by Philip V.
Temple dedicated to Aphrodite constructed in the 6th century BCE at the Greek colony of Akrai.
A sanctuary of Apollo near Punta Alice. Tradition ascribed its foundation to Philoctetes and it is said he dedicated his bow to Apollo.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 H4 Apollo Axyros, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 G3 Apollo Erethimios, T.

A multi-phase sanctuary of Apollo that begins in the archaic period. Pausanias visited the sanctuary and observed two cult statues of the god. One of these, sacred to Apollo Corynthus, was of wood. The other, sacred to Apollo Argeotas, was of bronze.
A sanctuary dedicated to Apollo Nasiotas located on the islet of Apsiphia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 E3 Apollo Tegyraios, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Apollo Thourios, T.
A Roman temple in Faimingen near Lauingen in Bavaria.
The likely site of the original sanctuary to Apollo at Rome. The visible remains belong to the Augustan phase.
The oracular shrine of Apollo Koropaios.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 E4 Apollo, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 F3 Apollo, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 G3 Apollo, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C4 Apollo, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Apollo, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D1 Apollo, T.
Aqaba - now Aaqbe - is a place located in the Beqaa Governorate in Lebanon. It is the site of a Roman temple that has been classified as belonging to the "Mount Hermon" temple group.
A fourth to third century B.C. sanctuary at Tarquinia.

The "Altar of Augustan Peace" was located in the Campus Martius of Rome. It was originally situated to the west of the Via Lata, near the eastern end of Augustus' Horologium. The reconstructed fragments of the altar are now housed in a museum located on the Tiber's banks in the Piazza Augusto Imperatore, a square created during the Fascist era in order to spotlight the ruins of the Mausoleum of Augustus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Archegetes, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H4 Aron
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Artemis Diktynnaia, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Artemis Elaphebolia, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D3 Artemis Parthenos, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 C2 Artemis Tauropolos, T.
The possible site of the temple of Artemis Proseoa beneath the Ag. Georgios chapel, Artemision (Kourbatsi) Euboea.
A circular Roman temple that stood on the river Carron until it was destroyed in 1743.
A Doric temple dated to the sixth century BCE.
A major, ancient sanctuary of Asklepios located on the Aegean island of Kos.
A sanctuary complex on the northern margins of ancient Corinth near the theater.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A5 Athena, Zeus?, T.
Athribis/Triph(i)eion in Upper Egypt, located outside modern Sohag, was the site of a temple built for the goddess Repyt (Triphis) in the Ptolemaic period.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 4 B2 Athroula/Laththa

name: Augusteum at Carthago Nova/Col. Urbs Iulia

props.description: A temple dedicated to the imperial cult during the reign of Augustus.
Temple dedicated to Osiris located 3km west of the modern village of Dush.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Bailleul-sur-Thérain
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Balmarcodes, T.

Buddhist temple at Angkor, located approximately 20 km northeast of the main temple complex. Dedicated to Shiva, the temple was constructed during the 10th century CE during the reigns of Rajendravarman II and Jayavarman V.
Archaeological site in northern Bahrain associated with the Dilmun culture. Three successive temples were built on the site during the 3rd-2nd millennia BCE.

Bard-i Nishande is the site of a ruined ancient landscape consisting of multiple complexes. According to K. Schippmann, these complexes include a ruler's residence or "castle", a terraced platform used for ritual activity, and a lower town.

The Temple of Apollo Epikourios (often Latinized as Epicurius) at Bassae is an innovative mid-5th century B.C. Doric temple in Arcadia. The temple preserves the oldest Corinthian capital yet found. It was added to the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1986.
A temple in the Khmer capital of Angkor Thom, constructed in the late 12th or early 13th century CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B3 Beaulieu
Beit el-Wali, meaning "House of the Holy Man", was a site in Nubia where Ramesses II built a temple sacred to Amun-Re, Re-Horakhti, Khnum and Anuket. The temple was relocated in the 1960s.
A fifth century B.C. temple that was likely sacred to Tinia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Beni Guedal
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 86 E3 Beşdut
A monumental sanctuary (26 x 20 m) dedicated to Iuppiter.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Bir Nesma
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 Boades
The ancient city of Bostra (modern Busra in Syria), where extensive ancient Nabataean, Roman, Byzantine and Umayyad structures still visible. Inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980.

Brauron was a sacred site in Attica dedicated to Artemis Brauronia. The sanctuary was active from the Middle Bronze Age period, although a gap in activity stretches from the Final Bronze Age to the eighth century B.C.
Brean Down is the site of a Romano-British temple that was built ca. A.D. 340 and destroyed about 50 years later. The site was excavated between 1957-8.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H3 Brides-les-Bains
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 Brigstock
11th century Hndu temple Tanjuvar dedicated to Shiva with smaller shrines around it.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 Bu Darag

An apsidal structure on the Acropolis of Athens, built in the late 6th-century BC, destroyed in the Persian Sack of 480 BC, and perhaps repaired in the first half of the 5th century. It was probably located on the site of the northwest wing of the later Propylaia, which would have superseded it in 437 BC.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D2 Bully
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 E3 Burj al-Qa'im
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 A4 Büyük Çelebi
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Bziza

Hellenistic temple constructed by Cleopatra VII and dedicated to Julius Caesar, and later rededicated by Augustus to himself. In 13 BCE Augustus relocated two obelisks from Heliopolis to the site. The temple was converted to a Christian church in the mid-4th century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Calalzo
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G1 Camp-Ferrus
The so-called Capitolium of Aquinum is found in the northwest part of the site. It may predate Roman colonization, and it is perhaps sacred to Iuno Regina.
The Capitolium temple at Mactaris likely dates to the reign of Marcus Aurelius.
The Capitolium at Thugga dates to the middle of the second century and carries epigraphic dedications to Lucius Verus and Marcus Aurelius dating to the late 160s.
The Capitolium of Brixia was built in A.D. 73.
The so-called Capitolium temple of the Latin colony of Cosa.
Tetrastyle Corinthian temple located in the Roman forum at Sala (modern Chellah) dating from the early Hadrianic period (c.120 CE).
The remains of a first-century BCE temple podium discovered at Sarsina have been identified as the Roman settlement's Capitolium.
A Tuscanic podium temple that is often referred to as being of the Capitolium type was built at Terracina in the middle of the first century BCE.

Excavations have determined the location of the capitolium of Roman Verona, part of an extensive, terraced complex that underpins medieval and later structures of the city. The capitolium itself stretches from the northern end of the modern Piazza Erbe to the via Emilei, lying beneath the Palazzo Maffei, church of San Benedetto, Palazzo Malaspina and surrounding structures.

The Capitolium Vetus was located on the Quirinal Hill and was an earlier shrine to the triad of Iuppiter, Juno, and Minerva than the famous Capitoline Temple. It was likely perched on the northern edge of the Quirinal.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 46 F4 Capo Cimiti
Remains of a fourth-century B.C. sanctuary situated at a spring known as Caput aquae, from which the modern toponym is most likely derived.
At modern Casalbore, Italy, archaeological evidence includes a significant temple structure and associated material from the Samnite and Roman periods.
The southernmost cave at Udayagiri, dedicated to an unidentified god.
Cave 13 at Udayagiri, known as the Nārāyaṇa cave, contains a large relief panel of Vishnu's Nārāyaṇa avatar.
The largest cave temple at Udayagiri, known as the Amrita or Viṣṇupada cave.
Cave 5 at Udayagiri is dedicated to Vishnu and contains a monumental relief panel of Vishnu's Varaha avatar.
A likely Roman fort and possible Urartian fire temple at the modern village of Cengerli in the Refahiye district of Erzincan province, Turkey.
The Roman temple dedicated to Ceres and Faustina received patronage from Herodes Atticus. Its remains were eventually repurposed in the construction of the church of Sant'Urbano.
A Samnite temple dedicated to Flora.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 D3 Cerro de los Santos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 A3 Chahar Deh
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Chalencon
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A5 Chanac
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F1 Chanceaux-sur-Choisille
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Changiyeh
Chapel for the Sacred Barque of Amun-Ra in Karnak, established by the Egyptian pharaoh Hakor of the Twenty-ninth dynasty who reigned from 392/1 to 379/8 BCE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Château de Chatillon
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 E2 Château-Bas

A mithraeum adjacent to the Circus Maximus and facing the carceres of the same was located at the margin of the Forum Boarium and now situated beneath a building in the via dell'Ara Massima. The site was excavated in 1931.
A set of buildings and a stoa built in the Athenian Agora in the 2nd century AD, to provide space for official business in addition to the Metroon and Tholos.

Distyle in antis temple of the fourth century BCE, built from the salvage of the large archaic temple, likely destroyed in the earthquake of 373 BCE. This temple remained in use until the first or second centuries CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E1 Cocullo
A heptagonal Gallo-Roman temple.
This stoa was built against a fourth century BCE seawall sometime in the Hellenistic era. The seawall shows evidence of later Roman repairs.

An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A1 Coste Guizon

The Arval Brethren cultivated the cult of the Dea Dia. The remains of this Augustan era temple, located at La Tavernaccia restaurant in the Magliana Vecchia neighborhood of Rome, are subterranean and not open to the public.
A small temple dedicated to Isis on West bank of the Nile at Luxor. Its ancient name is unknown.

A sanctuary of Apollo Delphinios in Athens, which was closely associated with Theseus and served as a law court for cases of justifiable homicide. It is perhaps to be identified with the Classical Doric temple, whose remains are located south of the Olympieion.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Demeter, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G4 Deneuvre
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Desaignes
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D4 Deuilly
Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava cave temple in Mahābalipuram.
Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temple in Mahābalipuram.
The Fanum Dianae Tifatinae located on the slopes of Mt. Tifata in Campania.
The ancient sanctuary of Apollo near Miletos (modern Didim, Turkey).
A sanctuary located at Menies bay in Chania, Crete, with remains ranging in date from Hellenistic period to Late Antiquity.
20th century BCE Dilmun temple on the highest part of the ancient settlement mound at Saar in modern Bahrain.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Dimri
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Dionysos Bresagenes, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Dionysos, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 G1 Djedars
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D1 Domburg
A sanctuary of the god Mercury in the Vosges Mountains.
A temple of the Doric order (VIII,7,30-34) located in the so-called Triangular Forum of Pompeii.
An Archaic Greek temple at Corfu built in the Doric order ca. 500 B.C.
The Doric Temple at Caulonia has been dated ca. 420 B.C.
The Doric temple of Segesta is an incomplete (6 x 14) temple of the Doric order begun in the 420s B.C.

The foundations of a 6th century BC temple on the Acropolis of Athens, dedicated to Athena Polias. It is disputed whether these foundations belong to the same structure as the 6th-century BC architectural fragments known as the Bluebeard temple. At the end of the 6th century, the foundations were reused for another temple of Athena Polias, the Archaios Neos, which was destroyed by the Persians in 480 BC.

An unfinished Doric stoa with colonnades on both long sides, built at Thorikos in the late fifth century BC, of uncertain function, perhaps a gateway to a sanctuary of Demeter. Material from the structure was spoliated in the Augustan period to build the southwest temple in the Athenian Agora.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 C1 Drion
An ancient ceremonial site located in County Kildare, Ireland. The circular enclosure once covered ca. 13 ha.
The temple of the god Uraš at Dilbat. The Akkadian ceremonial name E-ibbi-Anum means "House, Anu Called It into Being."
As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ea.

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Ea and the goddess Damkina. Although its location is not known, the Ea-Damkina temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.
The temple of the goddess Ningal at Kissik. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eamaškuga means "House, Pure Sheepfold."

The temple of the goddess Ištar of the Star, Eandasaya (“House Which Rivals Heaven”), was located in the New City district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two temples in that part of Babylon.
The temple of the god Anu and the goddess Ištar at Uruk. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eanna means "House of Heaven."
First phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.
The temple of the god Šamaš at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ebabbar means "Shining House."
The temple of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ebabbar means "Shining House."

The temple of the god Šara, Ebursasa (“House of Beautiful Jars”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.
The temple of the god Ištarān/Anu-rabû at Der. The Sumerian ceremonial name Edimgalkalama means "House, Great Bond of the Land."
The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eduranna means "House, Bond of Heaven."
The temple of the goddess Gula (as Ninisinna) at Isin. The Sumerian ceremonial name Egalmah means "Exalted Palace."
The Giparu-House at Ur was the temple of the high-priestess of the moon-god.

The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Ninūa, Egišhurankia (“House of the Ordinances of Heaven and Underworld”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Egišhurankia seems to have been located near Imgur-Enlil, along the northern wall.

The temple of the goddess Ašratu, Ehilikalamma (“House of the Luxuriance of the Land”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Sahn) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon.
The temple of the god Sîn at Harran. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ehulhul means "House which Gives Joy."

The temple of the god Ninurta, Ehursagtilla (“House Which Exterminates the Mountains”), was located in the Šuanna district (modern Ishin Aswad) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of two temples in that part of the city, which was located in the southern part of the eastern half of Babylon. The building was rebuilt by the Neo-Babylonian king Nabopolassar (r. 625–605 BC).
The temple of the god Lugal-Marda at Marad. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eigikalamma means "House, Eye of the Land."

The temple of the god Belili, Ekadima (“House Which Creates ...”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.
The temple of the god Ea within the Esagil(a) complex at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekarzagina means "Pure Quay"or "Quay of Lapis Lazuli."
The temple of the goddess Aya at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekinu means "House, Bed-Chamber."

The temple of the moon god Nanna/Suen/Sîn at Ur from the third to first millennium B.C. Over the course of its long history, it was rebuilt (and expanded) numerous times. A few of its more famous builders were Narām-Sîn of Agade, Ur-Nammu of Ur, and Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon.

The temple of the god Sîn, Ekišnugal (“House of the Great Light”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon. The temple is mentioned in Old Babylonian Period texts and was (re)built by the Old Babylonian king Abī-ēšuh (r. ca. 1711–1684 BC) and the Neo-Babylonian ruler Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC). The temple survived into the Hellenistic Period.

The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Eanna, Ekitušgarza (“House, Abode of the Regulations”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Ekitušgarza was rebuilt by the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–652 BC).

The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Eanna, Ekitušgirzal (“House, Abode of Joy”), was located in the New City district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two temples in that part of Babylon.

A ziggurat dedicated to the goddess Ištar was built near the center of the citadel of Nineveh, not far from the city’s main temple Emašmaš. This structure was given the Sumerian ceremonial names Ekituškuga ("House, Pure Abode") and Ekibikuga ("House whose Site is Pure").
The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekunankuga means "House, Pure Stairway of Heaven."
The temple of the god Bunene at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekurra means "House of the Mountains."
An ancient place, cited: None
A ruined temple built in Cipollino marble located about 10 km south of Sabratha.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 B1 el-Garrusc
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 el-Hosn
An ancient place, cited: None
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 el-Nadura
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 el-Qasr
Structure in the the necropolis of Carmona (near Seville) named for a small sculpture of an elephant found inside it. Rather than a tomb, it may have been a sanctuary or temple.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 D1 Ellès

The ziggurat of the god Sîn at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Elugalgalgasisa means "House of the King who Lets Counsel Flourish". The platform temple was originally built during the Early Bronze Age and was then restored in the sixth century B.C. by King Nabonidus.

The temple of the goddess Ninmah/Bēlet-ilī, Emah (“Exalated House”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Kasr) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon. Emah was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC).
The temple of the goddess Ištar of Agade at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Emašdari means "House of Animal Offerings." The Neo-Babylonian king Nabonidus rebuilt/renovated it.

The most important temple in the citadel of Nineveh was dedicated to the city's tutelary deity, the goddess Ištar. The temple was located in the center of the citadel and went by the Sumerian ceremonial name Emašmaš (meaning uncertain).

The temple of the goddess Šarrat-Larsa, Emekiliburur (“House which Gathers All the Me’s”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.

The temple of the god Amurru, Emesikila (“House of Pure Me’s”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Emesikila is mentioned in Old Babylonian sources.
The temple of the god Nergal at Cutha. The Sumerian ceremonial name Emeslam means "House, Warrior of the Netherworld."

The temple of the goddess Nanāya, Emeurur (“House which Gathers the Me’s”), was located in the TE.E (read as either Tê or Kisīri) district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was the only temple in that part of Babylon.

The temple of the god Adad, Enamhe (“House of Plenty”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Enamhe was (re)built by the the Old Babylonian kings Sūmû-lā-El (r. ca. 1881–1845 BC) and Hammu-rāpi (r. ca. 1792–1750 BC) and the Neo-Babylonian ruler Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC).
The temple of the gods Adad and Apla-Adad at Anat (Anatho). The Sumerian ceremonial name Enamhe means "House of Plenty."

The temple of the god Bēl-mātāti (Enlil), Enamtila (“House of Life”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Enamtila is mentioned in Old Babylonian and Kassite Period texts.
The temple of the god Nabû of the ḫarû at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eniggidrukalamasuma means "House which Bestows the Scepter of the Land."

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Enlil. Although its location is not known, the Enlil temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.
The ḫilṣu-building of the goddess Ningal inside the Egišnugal temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Enunmah means "House of the Exalted Prince."

The temple of the god Nusku, Enunmah (“House of the Exalted Prince”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 F3 Epoissum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Ermita de S. Valero
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 F4 es-Soumaa

The temple of the goddess Gula, Esabad (“House of the Open Ear”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Esabad was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–652 BC). The temple survived into Arsacid times (247 BC–AD 224).

The temple of the god Lugalbanda, Esag (“Foremost House”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.
The temple of the god Marduk at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Esagil(a) means "House, Whose Top is High."
The temple of the god Nergal at Me-Turnat. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ešahula means "House of the Happy Heart."

Around 690 B.C., the Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib started construction on a New Year’s Temple for Nineveh’s tutelary deity Ištar. Ešahulezenzagmukam, whose Sumerian ceremonial name means "House of Joy and Gladness for the Festival of the Beginning of the Year" was never completed and work on it appears to have come to a complete stop ca. 688 B.C., when Sennacherib decided to rebuild the Akītu Temple at Ashur instead.

The temple of the god Išhara, Ešasurra (“House of the Womb”), was located in the Šuanna district (modern Ishin Aswad) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of two temples in that part of the city, which was located in the southern part of the eastern half of Babylon.

The temple of the god Nabû, Ešiddukišara (“House of the Director of the Universe”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.
The Domu de Orgia at Esterzili is the largest nuragic "megaron" structure thus far discovered in Sardinia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Estrées-St-Denis
The ziggurat of the god Marduk at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Etemenanki means "House, Foundation Platform of Heaven and Underworld."

The multi-phase acropolis of Etruscan Volaterrae has numerous important phases, beginning in the Archaic period. The monumental site continued to develop and grow during the Hellenistic period. It was identified archaeologically only in the nineteenth century. In the twentieth century it was the subject of major investigations under M. Cristofani and, later, M. Bonamici.
The temple of the goddess Anunnītu at Sippar-Anunnītu (=Sippar-Amnanum).

The temple of the god Pisangunuk, Eurguba (“House, Established Foundation”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.
The ziggurat of the god the god Nabû at Borsippa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eurmeiminanki means "House which Gathers the Seven (Me's) of Heaven and Underworld."
The temple of the god Marduk as Tutu, later of the god Nabû, at Borsippa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ezida means "True House."

One of the temples in the citadel of Nineveh that was dedicated to Nabû, the god of scribes; it is located north of the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš). The Sumerian ceremonial name Ezida means "True House."

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Nabû. Unlike most of the religious buildings constructed at Kalhu, the Ezida ("True House") was built in the southeast corner of the Citadel.

The temple of the god Pisangunuk, Eʾesirkalama (“House of the Street of the Land”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.

The temple of the god Ea, Eʾešmah (“Exalted House”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 H4 Fakhrikah
The Fanum of Pithiviers-le-Vieil.

A Gallo-Roman site lying along the Roman road from Lyon to Saintes.
The Five Rathas is the modern name of a group of five seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temples at Mahābalipuram.
Originally the site of a shrine dedicated to the river god Clitumnus and later a paleochristian shrine. The temple was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A4 Fontans
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 C2 Forggensee

The Friars Wash Roman Temple was noted first in 1965. Aerial photography and subsequent archaeological investigation identified a system of ditches and two rectangular structures that were considered to be Romano-British temples.
An Umbro-Roman temple of Iuppiter on the slopes of Monte Catria.
Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Amenirdis I, daughter of King Kashta.
Presumed remains of the funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Ankhnesneferibre.
Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Shepenupet I, daughter of King Osorkon III.

name: Funerary chapel of Shepenupet II, Nitocris I and Mehytenweskhet

props.description: Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Shepenupet II, daughter of King Piankhi. Enlarged by her successor, the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Nitocris I, daughter of King Psamtik I, to accommodate her cult and that of her mother Mehytenweskhet.
Temple dedicated to the cult of King Amenhotep II located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Ay and Horemheb

props.description: Temple dedicated to the cult of Kings Ay and Horemheb located on the west bank of Thebes.
Temple dedicated to the cult of King Seti I located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Siptah and Twosret

props.description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Siptah and the Queen Twosret located on the west bank of Thebes.
Temple dedicated to the cult of King Thutmose III located on the west bank of Thebes.
Temple dedicated to the cult of King Thutmose IV located on the west bank of Thebes.

Remains of a Gallo-Roman at Vannes.

props.description: A Gallo-Roman temple built ca. mid-first century AD that remained in use until the fifth century.

Located along the course of the Via Appia between Fondi and Itri are the remains of a likely sanctuary of Apollo that dates to the fourth and third century BCE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Görresburg
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Görtschach
The Great Temple of Hera, or Polycrates Temple, is the third temple constructed at the Heraion of Samos between 540 and 530 BCE.

The Great Temple of Petra is one of the major archaeological and architectural components of central Petra. It is located to the south of the Colonnaded Street and southeast of the Temenos Gate and covers 7560 square meters.
A Doric temple dating to the sixth century B.C., located in the modern piazza Castello in Taranto, Italy. It was initially attributed to Poseidon, but may have been dedicated to a different deity.

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the goddess Gula. Although its location is not known, the Gula temple at Kalhu is thought to have been part of the Ninurta temple complex (in the northwest corner of the citadel), east of the Šarrat-niphi temple and north of the Kidmuri temple.

The Hekateion is a monumental temple for the goddess Hekate constructed in Lagina ca. 125 BCE.

The so-called 'Tavole Palatine' is the site of the ruins of a sixth century B.C. Doric temple sacred to Hera and associated with the city of Metapontum.
A sanctuary of Herakles located on the island of Lemnos, active from the Archaic period through to the time of the Roman empire.

Husn Niha is the site of four Roman temples that were constructed between the first and third centuries AD.

Sanctuary of Apollo Hyperteleatas, partially excavated in 1885 and 1968. In ancient literature the sanctuary is only mentioned by Pausanias, in whose time it was called the Hyperteleaton and was dedicated to Asclepius. The site is located south of Phoiniki in the southeastern Peloponnese at a place known in the 19th century called Chasanaga.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D3 Idomene Minor
An Ionic stoa with four dining rooms on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens, built ca. 420 BC. It may have been part of the Asklepieion complex.
Temple dedicated to the goddess Ishtar located in the western section of the city of Mari.

name: Italic temple of Castel di Ieri

props.description: The remains of an Italic temple, in the locality known as "Pié di Francia". The sanctuary dates from the fourth to first centuries B.C.

Jandial is the site of a semi-Classical temple of Ionic Greek design constructed in the first or second centuries BCE. It is the farthest east of any known Hellenic temple.

The temple-tower of the god Ninurta, which was started by the ninth-century-BC Assyrian ruler Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) and completed by his son and successor Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC), is still the most prominent feature of Kalhu (modern Nimrud). This multi-staged ziggurat, whose remains are now a pyramid of eroding mud-brick, was constructed in the northwest corner of the citadel, next to Kalhu's principal sanctuary, the Ninurta temple.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 inset Kalogeros
A Doric-order temple, hexastyle in antis (23.20x11.10 m). The building utilizes stone from both Aegina and Paros.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 C6 Kasnakovo

A complex of structures dating to the Greek and Roman period, with ceramic evidence of prior occupation of the vicinity, on a spur of Palióvouno, overlooking a valley leading to the bay of Saránda on the Korinthian Gulf. The excavators associate this site with the attested classical city of Khorsiai on the basis of epigraphic evidence recovered at the site.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Kazerun
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 97 B2 Kelisaye Espakho
A complex of 555 megaliths arranged in 13 rows.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G3 Khayrabad Bala
An unexcavated Old Kingdom temple, attested by epigraphic finds at Abydos in Egypt. Its remains are presumed to underlie later Osiris sanctuary structures and part of the modern town.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 E4 Khirbet el-Bilaas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Khirbet el-Knese
Located some 70 km from Petra, Khirbet et-Tannur is the site of a well-preserved, open-air Nabataean sanctuary.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 E4 Khirbet Semrin
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Khontro Bigli

The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple-tower built on the palace terrace of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). The ziggurat was located at the northern end of the temple sector, which was accessible from Sargon’s palace, through the Courtyard XV.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Khortata
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B3 Khrysovitsa
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Khurramdasht
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D2 Kiboreia

The bīt Kidmuri is one of the temples located in the citadel of Nineveh. Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources. Although the temple's exact location remains unknown, it is generally believed to have been between the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš) and the Citadel Gate (East Gate).

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the divine Kidmuri (probably a collective name for a group of gods).

The so-called Kiosk of Qertassi is an unfinished Roman kiosk that may date to the reign of Trajan. Originally located at Qertassi, the remains of the temple were relocated to New Kalabsha in Southern Egypt as part of the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia in the twentieth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Kiratah
The site of a temple of Apollo administered by Colophonians and active from the seventh century B.C. to the fourth century A.D.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Kobosh
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Koçak
Koi Krylgan Kala is an archaeological site in Uzbekistan. At the site are the remains of a temple complex associated with the Chorasmian Dynasty, which was destroyed approximately 200 BC.
An archaeological site on the island of Aegina that shows multiple settlement phases from the Bronze Age to the Classical period.
Area south of the main city of Amarna that was the site of a temple during the Amarna period. The structure was overbuilt by a Christian monastery in late antiquity.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 M2 Komosarue
Megalithic temple in Malta constructed during the Mġarr phase (ca. 3700 BCE) and abandoned ca. 2500 BCE.
A group of megalithic temples on Malta constructed during the Neolithic period.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B4 Krounoi
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Ksar Djaria
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 inset Kubaniya
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Kuh-i Ayyub
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Kuh-i Shahrak
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Kunar Siah
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Kushk Khainfar
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 F3 La Bauche
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 F4 La Motte-St-Martin
Labyrinthos is a name applied by Greek authors, notably Herodotus and Strabo, to describe a monumental funerary temple at Hawara.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Lamyatt
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 B3 Laneia
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G2 Lang-i Rud
A large rock-cut temple at Sikait in Egypt's Eastern desert.
Final phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D3 Laveyron
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B4 Le Bouchet-St-Nicolas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 7 D2 Le Pinnacle
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 F5 Le Serre d’Astier
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 F3 Lendorf
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D2 Lentilly
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 D2 Les Courents
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 B3 Leukothea
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Lloret

A temple sacred to Mithras in Londinium that was discovered in 1954. The visible remains of the temple do not sit on the original site, but have been reassembled at Temple Court, Queen Victoria Street, London.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Los Cuadriales
A settlement in Azania, located near modern Kato Lusi, that has been under excavation since the late 19th century.
An ancient Egyptian temple that was initially built ca. 1400 BCE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Lydney
A fourth century AD temple dedicated to Nodens.
A Shiva temple at Banavasi in India whose foundation may date as early as the 7th century CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Magoula
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C1 Magoula Kastri

One of the four main Buddhist pilgrimage sites, identified as the place where Gautama Buddha attained Enlightenment. The first temple on the site was built by Emperor Asoka in the third century B.C., and the present temple dates from the fifth or sixth centuries. It is one of the earliest Buddhist temples built entirely in brick, still standing in India, from the late Gupta period. The site was largely abandoned from the twelfth-eighteenth centuries. It was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2002.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Maimeh
A well-preserved Roman temple built ca. 16 B.C. and dedicated to Caius and Lucius Caesar.
A sacred structure with an 'in antis' plan located near Arzachena in Sardinia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Malla
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Manglisi
A peripteral temple built of basalt that includes an altar to Nemesis. Epigraphic data provides a chronology of late second to third centuries A.D.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Maqsudabad
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A4 Marathos
A Mithraic shrine of unusual size discovered at modern Marino, Italy in 1963.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 G3 Markesi

A temple sacred to Mars located on the Via Appia. The shrine was located between the first and second milestones and was dedicated in the fourth century BC. According to Livy, Titus Quinctius dedicated the temple on 1 June 387 BC.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Mavrovounoi
Roman sanctuary with two small temples.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D3 Mechel
The New Kingdom mortuary temple of Ramesses III at Medinet Habu, on the West bank of the Nile near Luxor.

A sanctuary of the "Great Gods" on the Aegean island of Imbros/Gökçeada. The remains of the sanctuary are located southwest of modern Kaleköy, to the west of the airport in the vicinity of a ruined monastery of Agios Konstantinos at a place formerly known as Roxado.
An ancient Greek sanctuary of Helen and Menelaos near ancient Therapnai in Greece.
A temple dedicated to Mercury and Maia that was located on the slopes of the Aventine Hill overlooking the Circus Maximus.
Meroë is an ancient city located on the east bank of the Nile river near Shendi, Sudan. The city was a center of the kingdom of Kush.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Meter Theon 1, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Meter Theon 2, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A5 Meter Theon, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C5 Metokhi
Temple at Olympia constructed in the late 4th or early 3rd century BCE
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 D3 Mezgura
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F1 Mézin
Second phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

The Middle Stoa, the largest structure in the Agora of Athens, was built after 183 BC, as the north side of the South Square. In the Augustan Period, the Odeon of Agrippa was built on its north side. It was destroyed during the Herulian Sack of 267 AD and used as building material for the Post-Herulian Wall.
Small temple with pitched roof presumably dedicated to Zeus, located in the military campus zone Block A1 at Dura-Europos.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E3 Minatiacum

name: Minerva temple at Punta della Campanella

props.description: An ancient temple of Minerva, located on the ancient cape that also was known by the goddess' name.

The so-called Minor Acropolis is located on the hill to the south of the Porta Maggiore. This area is surrounded by the urban enclosure wall and is terraced in polygonal masonry. Two platform temples of unknown assignation are to be found here.
A second-century Mithraeum is located in the area of the municipality of Mundelsheim in the Ludwigsburg district of Baden-Württemberg, Germany.
A temple once sacred to Mithras located in Riegel am Kaiserstuhl (Baden-Wuerttemberg, Germany).
A mithraeum was excavated at Segontium (modern Caernarfon) in 1959, ca. 137m from the east corner of the Roman auxiliary fort. The site was abandoned in the late fourth century A.D.
A temple sacred to Mithras located at the Roman fort of Vindovala (Rudchester) on Hadrian's Wall.
The Mithraeum of Lucretius Menander located in ancient Ostia dates to the third century CE.
Megalithic temple complex of the fourth millennium BC.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Möhn
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 B2 Mollis
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C3 Mont-Rivel
An Italic temple complex in Umbria of the first millennium B.C. that includes the so-called "Ara maior".
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 E1 Montreuil-Bellay
Temple constructed in the Theban Necropolis during the 18th Dynasty.
The Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahari was built for the Eighteenth dynasty Pharaoh Hatshepsut.
