The Mortuary temple of the Eleventh Dynasty pharaoh Mentuhotep II at Deir el-Bahari would inspire the later mortuary complexes of Hatshepsut and Thutmose III that are also found at the site.
Mortuary temple of the Nineteenth Dynasty in the Theban Necropolis.
Mortuary temple constructed for Senwosret III, a pharaoh of the 12th Dynasty in Egypt.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Msellata
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Muk

A Meroitic temple complex in modern Sudan, located 180 km northeast of Khartoum, 20 km north of Naqa and approximately 25 km south-east of the Nile. The complex dates to the early Meroitic period (third century B.C.). In 2011 the site was enrolled as a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple to the god Nabû, the god of scribes, built in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Building H is the largest temple in Sargon’s newly-constructed city and it was built on a platform in the western side of the citadel, rather than on the palace terrace. The Nabû temple was connected to the temple district, which formed part of Sargon’s royal residence, by a stone bridge.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C4 Nagos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Naisar
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B3 Naqsh-i Bahram
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D2 Narmouthis
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Natanz
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Naudaran
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 F2 Navish
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Nebi Safa
An ancient sanctuary of necromancy sacred to Hades and Persephone located on the banks of the Acheron River in Epirus, near the ancient city of Ephyra.
A significant cult center at Nettleton Shrub in Wiltshire was dedicated to Apollo Cunomaglus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H1 Neuvy-sur-Barangeon

name: New Year's Festival House at Assur

props.description: As part of his post-689-BC religious reforms, the Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib (704–681 BC) had a new New Year’s festival house (akītu-house) constructed outside the western wall of Assur. This was an attempt to replicate the cultic topography of Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial names of that holy building and its cella are Eabbaugga ("House Where Tiāmat Is Put To Death") and Edubdubabba ("House That Makes Tiāmat Tremble”) respectively.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C4 Neyrac-les-Bains
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Nihata
As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the goddess Ningal.

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. The most important of those was dedicated to the god Ninurta, the patron deity of city, which Ashurnasirpal had constructed between his palace and the ziggurat, a building also consecrated to Ninurta.
As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ninurta.
The Temple of Apedemak, also known as the Lion Temple, is located to the west of the temple of Amun.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 B4 Nymphaion/Balaneion Tiberinon
The modern Polish city of Odry is the site of a major "stone circles" cemetery of the iron age Wielbark Culture.

A large temple on the Acropolis of Athens, begun in the 480s BC and destroyed during the Persian Sack of 480 BC, while still under construction. Parts of the structure were incorporated into the north wall of the Acropolis and other structures in the mid-fifth century BC and the Parthenon was built on the same site, using some of the older structure's foundations, beginning in 447 BC.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 A2 Orsingen
The Osireion at Abydos is located to the rear of the temple of Seti I. It is an integral part of Seti I's funeral complex and was built to resemble an 18th Dynasty Valley of the Kings tomb.
A Romano-British temple excavated on Pagans Hill at Chew Stoke, Somerset.
The ruins of a colonnaded structure in marble, perhaps a temple; a well / cistern is also observed by Sealy.
A sanctuary with votive deposits dating to the Hellenistic period at Panetelle di Mondragone.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 24 D3 Panóias
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Paptoulis?
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Parembole?
The temple of Athena Parthenos located on the Acropolis of Athens, which was constructed at the height of Athenian power between 447 and 432 BCE. It is the largest Doric temple in mainland Greece.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H2 Passy
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 Patriacum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B2 Pecë
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Pelm

The site of Per-Ptah, meaning "House of Ptah", lies some 90 km south of Aswan and received a temple dedicated to pharaoh Ramesses II. Some of the architectural and sculptural remains of the temple were dismantled and relocated to the site of New Kalabsha in the 1960s during the construction of the Aswan High Dam.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Pera Milous
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Pergamos
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Pescara
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 D1 Petit-Mars
A large ancient sanctuary of Apollo, located on the southwest coast of the island of Chios.

An ancient island located just above the First cataract of the Nile River that was flooded by the construction of the Aswan High Dam. The island was home to a multi-period temple complex, which was moved to the nearby island of Agilkia and declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in the 1970s.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Phokikon
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C4 Pithiviers-le-Vieil
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 G4 Porto Cesareo
The extraurban sanctuary of Minerva at Portonaccio is an important Etruscan cult center. The temple dates ca. 510 B.C. and was adorned with acroterial sculptures, notably the famous "Apollo of Veii".
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A4 Poseidon Phytalmios?, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 E4 Poseidon Porthmios, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A3 Poseidon?, T.
Poseidon?, T. a sanctuary of Poseidon on the island of Samos.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 F2 Potamos, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 E2 Pouzauges
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B2 Prdejci

A small temple on the Acropolis of Athens that might have existed from the 470s B.C. until replaced by the Erechtheion, perhaps in the 420s B.C. It might have hosted the cult of Athena Polias and/or Erechtheus.
Temple at Angkor dedicated in 1191 CE under Jayavarman VII. The temple entered a period of decline beginning in the 15th century.
Temple constructed in the city of Hariharalaya, dedicated in 880 CE by the Khmer king Indravarman I.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E5 Prepelaion?

An Iron Age settlement on the Patela plateau north of the modern village of Prinias; its ancient name is uncertain. The site is notable for its occupation from the end of the Bronze Age through to the Archaic period, as well as for the monumental architecture and Orientalizing sculpture of its Buildings ('Temples') A and B.
An ancient temple of Poseidon, known today as the "Tomb of Homer", located on a height at the northern end of the Aegean island of Ios. Some funerary epigraphy has also been noted at the site.
A coastal Etruscan sanctuary that dates ca. 540-530 B.C. with additional architectural phases dating to the fourth through second centuries B.C.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Pusht-i Arisht
Pyra is a place with preserved ruins of a Doric temple dating to the third century B.C. The sources connect Pyra with the pyre of the hero Herakles.
Funerary complex of King Senusret III at Dahshur including a pyramid.

Pyrgi is an ancient Etruscan port city on the Tyrrhenian coast of Italy. A Roman colony was established at the site by 191 B.C. In 1964, the bilingual 'Pyrgi Plaques' were discovered during the course of excavations.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 D4 Qadamgah
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B3 Qadboun
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Qafr Zebad

A Roman-era sanctuary complex in Lebanon with multiple altars and two temples. On the basis of epigraphic evidence, one of these temples seems to have been dedicated to Atargatis and the other possibly to Zeus Beelgalassos. There is some evidence for worship also of Zeus Heliopolis and for the Roman imperial cult. A Byzantine basilica was later built on the site.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Qanat-i Bagh
Site in Luxor (Egypt) associated with a Ptolemaic temple dedicated to the god Thoth.
The remains of an ancient temple at Qasr el Banat, Lebanon,
Temple located north of Lake Qarun in the Fayyum Depression. Tentatively dated to the Old Kingdom period, the temple may have been dedicated to the god Sobek.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Qasr Naous
Qasr Neba is the site of a Roman temple in the Bekaa valley of Lebanon.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D2 Qasr Nemrud
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 B1 Quarrush
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Raiano
A thirteenth-century temple that was built during the period of the Kakatiya ruler Ganapati Deva.
The funerary temple of Ramesses II, built within the area known as the Ipet-isut, "The Most Selected of Places", along the Nile River.
A Punic-Roman sanctuary site located on the western coast of Gozo (Gaulos), Malta.

Excavations on site of post office at 49 Ben Ami St. revealed Hellenistic remains, including a dedication to Zeus Soter on behalf of Antiochos VII by a courtier (SEG XIX 9094). This inscription was associated with Hellenistic architecture of a temple-like character. Nearby workshops also uncovered.
Site of a Roman temple and possible small town near the confluence of the rivers Soar and Trent near Ratclifffe-on-Soar, Nottinghamshire. Findspot of at least three curse tablets (defixiones).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B4 Resras

Ribemont-sur-Ancre is the site of a Celtic and Gallo-Roman sanctuary located on the right bank of the Ancre river, a tributary of the Somme. Roger Agache discovered the site in 1962. The site is vast in its area, covering some 80 ha.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 C4 Rijelat Umm Kubar
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E1 Rijsbergen
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 A2 Roda
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Rogeban

A two-aisled Doric stoa ran along the south side of the Panathenaic Way from the Stoa Basileios towards the Dipylon Gate, which contained shops. It was constructed over a long period beginning in the early second century BC and repeatedly modified. The building was destroyed in the Herulian Sack of 267 AD, rebuilt, damaged in the Gothic Sack of 396 AD, rebuilt again, and was finally destroyed in the Slavic Sack of 582 AD.
Site of a Roman temple overlooking Jounieh Bay
A pseudodipteral, hexastyle, Corinthian order temple begun under Claudius and completed under Domitian. The temple is likely sacred to the imperial cult.
The Roman Temple of Évora, also referred to as the Temple of Diana, is a Roman temple of the first century A.D.
The Roman temple of Vic dates to the second century A.D.
The remains of an early Imperial Roman temple on Sicily's Cape Mulini.
Structures thought to be temples set within a sacred area that includes a sacred well.

A brick rotunda of the fourth century AD ordered by the emperor Galerius for use as either a tomb or temple; later incorporated into the Christian Church of Agios Georgios. Listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1988
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Rouvroy-les-Merles

The Stoa Basileios or Royal Stoa was an important Doric-order stoa originating in the sixth century BCE and located at the northwest corner of the Athenian Agora. It was the administrative seat of the magistrate known as the archon basileus ("king archon").
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Ruus el-Aslab
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 83 B4 Ruwwafah
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 I4 S. Cristina d’Aro
Located some 16 km from Lugo, S. Eulalia de Bóveda is the location of a vaulted, three-aisled, underground building, likely dating to the middle Imperial period and functioning as a nymphaeum.
Site of a small Samnitic sanctuary atop Colle Rimontato (709 m. a.s.l.)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 S. Jorge

The so-called "Sacellum of the Augustales" at Misenum is an architectural complex located near the theater that was first built during the Iulio-Claudian period and dedicated to the Imperial cult. The complex was renovated during the second century, at which time one Cassia Victoria re-dedicated the shrine in honor of her husband, L. Laecanius Primitivus, who was a priest of the Augustales during the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

An archaeological area in the Forum Boarium comprising an ancient ritual precinct with twin temples ranging in date from the Archaic period through the time of the Roman Empire. The Medieval church dedicated to S. Omobono stands atop the remains of one of the two ancient temples.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B2 Sail-sur-Couzan
The Chapel of Saint Jean is built atop a temple once dedicated to Iuppiter Optimus Maximus.
Remains of a rustic temple and a menhir were discovered in the early 1960s near Boutaran.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H3 Salins-les-Thermes
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C2 Salt-en-Donzy
As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Šamaš.

A sanctuary on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens, dedicated to Aphrodite in the Classical period and associated with Isis from the late first century BC. A temple was built in the Hadrianic period.
A sanctuary at Cyrene dedicated to Apollo Karneios. The sanctuary was in use between the seventh century BC and the second century AD, and included shrines to other gods, including Artemis and Isis.
The Sanctuary of Apollo at Halieis is a temple complex dedicated primarily to Apollo, constructed outside of the city of Halieis and dating between the seventh and fourth centuries BCE.
A partially excavated artificial platform and temple at modern Kato Phana on the Greek island of Chios (ancient Phanai).
A small temple of Artemis Aristoboule ("of best deliberation") erected by Themistokles
A major sanctuary complex located west of the Theatre of Dionysus Eleuthereus on the south slope of the Athenian Acropolis.
A pan-thessaliote sanctuary, located at modern Philia Karditsis in Greece.
The Sanctuary of Fortuna Primigenia at Praeneste is a terraced sanctuary of monumental proportions. The phase visible today dates to the late second century B.C.
The Sanctuary of Iuno Gabina at Gabii.
Described by Pausanias, this sanctuary was located near Akraiphiai in Boeotia.
A sanctuary of Themis (order, righteousness), located on the south slope of the Acropolis of Athens.

A first century B.C. temple dedicated to Minerva by Gneus Caestius and Titus Caesius Priscus, who were two of the Assisi's quattuorviri. The temple was converted to the church of Santa Maria Sopra Minerva in 1539.

The Roman sanctuary of Santalla de Bóveda dates to the second through fourth centuries A.D. It is located in the parish of Santalla de Bóveda de Meralocated near Lugo. Discovered in 1926, the site is a protected national monument.
A sanctuary with twin temples located at the Etruscan port. The Pyrgi sanctuary was initially excavated by Massimo Pallottino in 1957. The so-called Pyrgi Plaques were discovered here in 1964.
Site of a temple dedicated to the Celtiberian/Lusitanian god Endovelicus.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 98 F2 Sar-i Pul
The earliest of the three shrines sacred to Serapis identified by archaeologists working at Delos in the nineteenth century.
One of three shrines sacred to Serapis identified by archaeologists working at Delos in the nineteenth century.

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the goddess Šarrat-niphi (an aspect of Ištar).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 L2 Satyrou Mnema
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E2 Scanno
Twin Italic temples located atop the Colle della Torre.

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the divine Sebetti. The location of this building is not known.

A temple dedicated to the Sebetti, the divine seven, was excavated by Iraqi archaeologists in 1957. This temple at Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad) is located just west of the citadel, near Citadel Gate A and City Gate 7.
The so-called "Second Temple" in Jerusalem stood on the northern edge of the Dome of the Rock and was destroyed in A.D. 70 during the Roman siege.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Seh Pa Aivan
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Septeuil
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 B2 Serapeion
The Serapeum in Ostia's Regio III was built between 123 and 126 CE thanks to private patronage.
One of three shrines sacred to Serapis identified by archaeologists working at Delos in the nineteenth century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Serapis, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Sghedeida
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B2 Shanhur

Large temple at Tell Jokha/Umma dedicated to the Sumerian god Shara. The temple was constructed by king Šu-Suen of the Third Dynasty of Ur (2035-2027 BCE), and was used during the Ur III-Old Babylonian periods.
The so-called "Shore Temple" of Mahabalipuram is a granite-built tomb at the "Group of Monuments at Mahabalipuram" UNESCO World Heritage Site. It dates to the eighth century CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 32 D2 Sidi-Mechrig
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Sillingy

When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the moon-god Sîn. Although its location is not known, the Sîn temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.
As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Sîn.

One of the temples in the citadel of Nineveh that was dedicated to the moon-god Sîn and the sun-god Šamaš. Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources and its exact location remains unknown. The temple is generally believed to have been east of the temples of Ištar (Emašmaš) and Nabû (Ezida) and south of the North Palace (palace of Ashurbanipal).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 86 C2 Sipahiler
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 F4 Sirenae, T.
A Hellenistic temple, possibly of Zeus, the foundation of which was excavated on the southern outskirts of the residential area of modern Skydra in Greece.

Homer mentions the temple of Apollo Smintheus as the shrine where Chryses was priest. The second-century B.C. remains of an Ionic sanctuary with an adjacent town are located in the southwestern Troad near modern Gülpinar.
A temple dedicated to Octavian by a Lucius Calpurnius. It replaced a temple of ca. 194 B.C. that had in turn been restored by Sulla.

A small, rectangular Greek temple-type building dated to the first century B.C. and located in the upper agora of Agrigento, on the Poggetto di San Nicola. The name "Oratory" comes from its later use as a Christian chapel.
Temple 1, also referred to as the "Great Temple", is located in the lower city of Hattusa. It dates to the thirteenth century B.C.
A temple located on the acropolis of Cyrene.
A recently discovered temple on the acropolis of Cyrene.
A temple on the acropolis of Cyrene, probably built around the seventh century BCE.
A Ptolemaic temple to the god Sobek.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 D3 Sofuentes
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 A5 Soleb
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F2 Souma el Kiata
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 E2 Souma n'Ikhetteben
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 B2 Soumat el Kheneg

South Stoa I is the modern name for the stoa that bounded the southern edge of the Agora of Athens from the late 5th century BC until the mid-2nd century BC, when it was demolished and replaced with South Stoa II. It contained rooms for communal dining and was probably the headquarters of the metronomoi and other officials.

South Stoa II is the modern name for the stoa that formed the south side of the South Square of the Agora of Athens. It was built in the mid-second century BC, on the site of South Stoa I using material from the Square Peristyle, and destroyed in the Sullan Sack of Athens in 86 BC.

The Southeast Stoa lined the east side of the Panathenaic Way at the southeast corner of the Athenian Agora, between the Library of Pantainos and the City Eleusinion. It was built in the mid-second century AD and incorporated into the Post-Herulian Wall after 267 AD.

name: Southeast temple of the Athenian Agora

props.description: The modern name for a temple built in the south-eastern corner of the Athenian Agora next to the Panathenaic Way in the early second century AD, using material from the Temple of Athena at Sounion. A colossal cult statue of a female deity was located inside. It was demolished in the late third century AD and used as building material for the Post-Herulian Wall.

name: Southwest Temple in the Agora of Athens

props.description: A tetrastyle Doric temple in the Athenian agora, built in the Augustan period using material from a Classical stoa at Thorikos and unidentified Hellenistic structures.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 E4 Soyans
Sparta was a prominent city-state (polis) of ancient Greece.

A temple in Acarnania, located between Stratos (modern Sourovigli) and modern Lepenou. The temple seems to have been constructed in the Greek Archaic period on the site of an earlier Helladic settlement. The temple continued in operation into the Roman period.
The Old Kingdom temple associated with the Great Sphinx of Giza and assigned to the reign of Khafre.

Main temple in Ahobilam dedicated to the man-lion form of the Hindu god Vishnu built or expanded in the 14th century, sacked by the Mughals in 1578, then restored and expanded at various times up to the present day.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B1 St-Alban-les-Eaux
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 I4 St-Andéol
St-Aubin-sur-Gaillon was the site of a Gallo-Roman sanctuary.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C2 St-Galmier
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 D2 St-Gervais
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G2 St-Goussard
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B4 St-Laurent-les-Bains
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C5 St-Maurice-d’Ibie
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 St-Victor
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C4 Ste-Abeille
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 E1 Ste-Barbe-du-Tlélat
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Ste-Ruffine

The Stoa at Brauron, within the larger Artemis Brauronia complex, housed dining spaces as well as votive offerings to the goddess Artemis. The Stoa was Π-shaped, wrapping around a courtyard that separated it from the temple of Artemis at Brauron, which it faced. Worship at the cult site began ca. the eighth century BCE. The site was abandoned after a flood in the third century BCE.
A large U-shaped stoa dating to the seventh and sixth centuries BCE.

name: Stoa in the sanctuary of Zeus Lykaios

props.description: A 62 m long stoa, probably of the fourth century BC.

The Stoa of Attalos, located on the eastern side of the ancient Athenian agora, is a long, rectangular, two-story building of limestone and marble, with an external and internal colonnade. It was originally constructed around 150 BCE and then reconstructed in the 1950s.

Eumenes II, king of Pergamon, built this eponymous stoa on the South slope of the Athenian Acropolis ca. 160 BCE. Its position lies between the Theater of Dionysus and the later Odeon of Herodes Atticus.

The Stoa of Zeus Eleutherios was built on the west side of the Agora of Athens between ca. 430-425 BC, in honor of Zeus "of Freedom"; the stoa, Doric in its external order, housed the shields of Athenians who had died fighting for the city.

The Stoa Poikile or Painted Stoa, named for a set of paintings inside depicting Athenian military victories, was located in the northwest corner of the Agora of Athens and was the namesake of the Stoic philosophical school. It was built around 460 BC and remained in use until it was spoliated in the sixth century AD. Archaeological excavation has uncovered the western and eastern ends of the building.
Temple dedicated to the god Ra in the 5th dynasty by the pharaoh Niuserre (Nyuserre).

name: Surviving colonnade of the imperial temple at Mérida

props.description: The incorrectly so-called "Temple of Diana."

The Dura-Europos synagogue is one of the oldest known synagogues in the world. According to an Aramaic inscription, its final phase of construction took place in A.D. 244. It was uncovered during excavations in 1932.
Khmer temple at Angkor constructed in 1186 CE under Jayavarman VII.
A Romano-Celtic temple complex located near the junction of Stane Street and Watling Street in modern Southwark.
A Kushite site located on Argo Island in Nubia, the location of a large Amun temple.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 90 C4 Takht-i Bilqis
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Tal-i Hakavan
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Tang-i Qandil
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 93 G3 Tang-i Sarvak
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Taq-i Gunbad

A megalithic temple complex that was named as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1980. This temple dates to the later Neolithic period on Malta and remained in use for several millennia. The first usage of the site occurs ca. 4100 BC and the temple complex develops across the fourth millennium.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 B3 Taxiarkhis
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 Tchonemyris
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 F5 Tekmoreioi
The Telesterion (ca. 435 - 421 B.C.) was a sacred hall at Eleusis used for the annual initiation ceremonies for the cult of Demeter and Persephone known as the Eleusinian Mysteries.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 D2 Telfes
Tell on the southwestern corner of Failaka Island containing the remains of two large buildings, tentatively identified as a palace and a temple, dated to 1900-1400 BCE.
A Gallo-Roman temple complex dated to the 1st-4th centuries CE.

The so-called "Tempio della Tosse" is an ancient Roman building along the river Anio. Its dating is problematic, but it is likely a building of late antiquity that is built on the site of an earlier villa. It has been interpreted as a nymphaeum, a temple, and also as a tomb.
A major urban sanctuary at Vulci with a long period of use, stretching from the archaic period into the Roman period.
Temple structure located south of the main Amun Temple complex at Naga, constructed during the reign of King Nebmaatre Amanakhareqerema (1st century CE).

So-called "Temple A" is a structure of the third century B.C. and is likely the Temple of Juturna that was vowed by Caius Lutatius Catulus following his victory against the Carthaginians in 241 B.C. It was later converted into a church, the apse of which remains visible.

name: Temple at Santa Maria in Canale

props.description: A temple of Tuscan type with cella and lateral alae built of ashlar masonry and dated to the 3rd century BCE, now built into a rural church and farmstead complex. The temple is situated along the Via Amerina between Ameria and Tuder, and marks the boundary between those two Umbrian communities. A fragmentary Umbrian bronze inscription (Vetter 229) of around 300 BCE mentioning a deity characterized as "Iovia" was discovered here in the 18th century CE.
A temple vowed by Q. Lutatius Catulus at the battle of Vercellae (30 June, 101 B.C.). The sanctuary is a tholos-type temple and was sometimes referred to as 'aedes Catuli'.
The so-called "Temple C" of the Largo Argentina sacred area may be dedicated to the Italic goddess Feronia.
The so-called Temple C at Selinus is a Doric temple that dates to the second half of the sixth century B.C.

Multi-phase temple site in the city of Kommos in southern Crete. Temple A was constructed by the late 11th century CE. A larger structure, Temple B, was built over it in the 9th century CE. The temple complex reached its maximum extent when Temple C was built over the site and its surroundings in the 7th century CE.

The temple referred to as "Temple D" in the Largo Argentina is likely the Aedes Lares Permarini, sacred to the lares who protect sailors. The largest of the temples in this sacred area, "Temple D" dates to the second century B.C. and remains largely unexcavated.
The ruins of a Roman-period temple dedicated to Apollo located at the Oppidum de Gaujac (Gard, France).
A fifth century B.C. Doric temple, sacred to perhaps Hera or Dionysus, located on the eastern hill at Selinus.
From the 5th century BC.

Temple F at Selinus is a Doric temple located on the site's so-called "East Hill". Dedicated either to Athena or Dionysus, the temple dates to the middle of the sixth century B.C. The non-canonical ground plan of 6 x 14 columns gives the temple an elongated aspect.
Remains of an octastyle Doric temple begun in the sixth century B.C. but left unfinished.
Small temple located to the northeast inside the military sector at Dura-Europos.

A classical/hellenistic temple located in the likely territory of Acarnanian Alyzeia. The temple site lies over 6km southeast from the ancient walled city (about a kilometer south of modern Paliovarka, Greece) at a site known in modern times as Drymonas Archontochoriou. It is perched on rising ground with a view westward to the channel and the island of Karnos (modern Kalamos). Inscriptions have been recovered from the site, and an unlooted portion of a votive deposit was scientifically excavated in the 1990s.
Temple located in block L5 at Dura-Europos.

A temple sacred to the goddess Al-Lat in Palmyra. The earliest shrine on the site was constructed in the mid-first century BCE, and was greatly expanded during the second century CE. The temple was closed and its altar destroyed during the 380s CE.

The Temple of Amada is the oldest Egyptian temple known in Nubia. Founded by Pharaoh Thutmose III (18th dynasty), the temple was dedicated to Amun and Re-Horakhty. The decorative program is attributed to Amenhotep II, his son and successor. The temple was relocated to its current position in 1964 along with the Temple of Derr.
Amenhotep II built a temple at Giza that he dedicated to the god Horemakhet, a sun-god identified with the Great Sphinx. It lies northeast of the Sphinx itself.

name: Temple of Amenhotep, son of Hapu

props.description: A mortuary temple located in the Theban Necropolis that was dedicated to one Amenhotep, son of Hapu, an official in the court of the 18th Dynasty pharaoh Amenhotep III. Numerous roles are attributed to Amenhotep, son of Hapu, including those of priest, scribe, and architect. Likely he received special dispensation for the construction of the mortuary temple.
Middle Kingdom temple of Amon at the heart of the Karnak Temple Complex.

The Temple of Amphiaraus is an oracular temple dedicated to the hero/god Amphiaraus, located within the sanctuary of the Amphiareion. The site was believed to have healing powers and was particularly important in the Late Classical and Hellenistic periods (fifth to third centuries BCE).

name: Temple of Amun at Jebel Barkal

props.description: Large temple dedicated to the Amun located at the base of Jebel Barkal. Designated temple B500, the structure was likely constructed by the Napatan king Piankhi in the 8th century BCE.

name: Temple of Anu and Adad at Assur

props.description: A distinctive feature of the city of Assur is the double-temple of the gods Anu and Adad. This building, together with its twin ziggurats, was constructed no later than the Middle Assyrian period; it underwent a major rebuilding in the reign of Tiglath-pileser I (1114–1076 BC). The existence of an (unnamed) Adad temple at Assur, however, is known from inscriptions of the Old Assyrian rulers Erišum I and Ikūnum. The various parts of this axisymmetric, double-temple was given Sumerian names: Anu’s temple and ziggurat were called Ešaan and Emelamanna; and Adad’s temple and ziggurat went by the names Ešuddegeštuku and Egišlamšaršar.
An ancient sanctuary on the island of Aegina, dedicated to the autochthonous deity Aphaia.
Temple building located in the southwest corner of Dura-Europos.
The remains of a marble temple executed in the Ionic order.
The temple of Aphrodite Euploia is a tholos constructed in the fourth century BC in Knidos, a coastal city on the Datça Peninsula. It was built to house Praxiteles' famous nude statue of Aphrodite.
The Temple of Apollo (VII,7,32) in Pompeii is one of the city's oldest cults, dating to the sixth century BC. The sanctuary assumed its present appearance in the second century BC.
The second temple of Apollo at Corinth whose reconstructed remains are visible on the site today. The structure dates ca. 540 B.C.

The Temple of Apollo situated in the center of the oracular sanctuary of that god at Delphi. Visible remains today are a 20th century reconstruction of the temple rebuilt in the fourth century BCE following a destructive landslide (repaired and altered multiple times thereafter). The date and cause of the temple's eventual destruction sometime not earlier than late antiquity remain undetermined.
The Temple of Apollo at Hierapolis, built during the Hellenistic period.

A peripteral Doric temple built in the mid-6th c. BCE and destroyed by fire in the 2nd c. BCE. The temple faces east, and was built of mudbrick on foundations of local sandstone, with sandstone column drums and a terracotta roof. A 4th c. BCE dedicatory inscription found in the cella attests the worship of Apollo. The structure was discovered in 1994 following the activity of looters.
Temple of Apollo at Syracuse on the island of Ortygia.
A temple to the Apollo was built in the late seventh century B.C. atop the ruins of the previous megaron in Thermos.
The Temple of Apollo Daphnephoros at Eretria dates originally to the late archaic period.

The Temple of Apollo Patroos on the west side of the Agora of Athens was built at the end of the fourth century BC. It contained a famous statue of Apollo by Euphranor. The structure wraps around the tiny fourth-century BC shrine of Zeus Phratrios and Athena Phratrios. The existence of an earlier temple, destroyed during the Persian Sack of 480 BC, is disputed.
The Hellenistic Temple of Apollo Pythios on the Acropolis of the city of Rhodes.
The Temple of Apollo Zoster is a sanctuary of the god Apollo located in Vouliagmeni in Attica, Greece. The temple's foundations date to the sixth century BC.

The sanctuary with a temple dedicated to Ares is located south of the Altar of the Twelve Gods in the Athenian Agora. The temple was originally erected at Pallene ca. 430 BC and sacred to Athena Pallenis at Pallene. The structure was moved to the Agora in the early first century.
Archaic temple to Artemis at Aulis, Boeotia. In mythology, said to be the place of death of Iphigenia. Mentioned in Euripides' 'Iphigenia at Aulis.'
Temple of Artemis on Thasos Island with remains from the Archaic period to the 1st century CE.
The Temple of Artemis, the central element of the extramural sanctuary of Artemis at Ephesus, was considered one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World.

A large temple dedicated to Artemis at the city of Sardis in western Asia Minor. A sanctuary to Artemis was constructed on the site during the Persian Era (ca. 547-334 BCE), and construction on a new temple on the site began during the third century BCE. The temple was never fully completed. A small church was built on part of the site in the 5th century CE, but the structure declined from the 7th century onwards, and many of its stones were reused or burned.
The temple of a syncretized incarnation of Artemis built at Dura-Europos in the Hellenistic period.
Archaeological phase (c. 13-160 CE) of the Temple at Dura-Europos dedicated to Artemis Azzanathkona, probably the tutelary deity of the Euphrates island settlement Anath.

Temple at Dura-Europos dedicated to Artemis Azzanathkona, tutelary deity of the Euphrates island settlement Anath; building complex later used for Roman military record keeping in addition to worship.
Temple located in block H4 at Dura-Europos.
Remains of a sanctuary located on Mt. Kotilon northwest of the Bassai temple.
An Archaic temple dedicated to Artemis Orthia within the sanctuary of this goddess at Sparta. The site contained rich deposits of votives and masks.
The Temple of Artemis Orthosia at Epidauros was founded in the Archaic period and continued in use in the Roman period. Pausanias mentions it as being on the city's temples.
The Temple of Artemis in Jerash.
The Temple of Asclepius (VIII, 7, 25) in Pompeii is located at the intersection of the Via del Tempio di Iside and the Via Stabiana. It is also referred to as the Temple of Jupiter Meilichius.

From the second half of the third millennium BC until 614 BC, there was a temple in Assur dedicated to the city’s tutelary deity Aššur. The temple was excavated by W. Andrae in 1903–05 and 1909–12. The earliest and latest textual records for construction work on Aššur’s temple date respectively to the time of Šalim-aḫum, an early old Assyrian king, and the reign of Ashurbanipal (668–631 BC), Assyria’s last great king. According to the Middle Assyrian king Shalmaneser I (1263–1234 BC) and the Neo-Assyrian ruler Esarhaddon (680–669 BC), Ušpia, a tribal ancestor of the Old Assyrian king Šamšī-Adad I (ca. 1808–1776 BC), was the building’s founder. In 614 BC, when the city was captured by the Medes, the temple was destroyed.
Temple located in block H2 at Dura-Europos.

The Temple of Athena at Ilion was a peripteral temple executed in the Doric order with a 12 x 6 columnar arrangement. Its creation is traditionally ascribed to Lysimachus (ca. 301-281 BCE), though it was later restored under Roman influence, likely during the principate of Augustus. Little of the temple, save its forecourt, remains in situ. It is mostly known from membra disiecta, including metopes uncovered during Schliemann's excavations.

The Temple of Athena in Miletos dates originally to the sixth century BCE, although the visible remains on site today belong to the Classical rebuilding of MIletos (ca. 479-450 BCE). The temple had a peripteral plan and was executed in the Ionic order. Unusually, its axis is oriented north-south.

An archaic temple of Athena on the acropolis of Thasos was dismantled in the 5th century BCE. Planning, procurement of materials, and construction of its replacement seem to have begun shortly thereafter, but were never finished.

name: Temple of Athena Alea at Tegea

props.description: An ancient sanctuary at Tegea dedicated to Athena Alea. The extant phase of the temple is attributed to Skopas who was responsible for rebuilding following a fire in 394 B.C.
A Late Archaic peripteral temple in the Doric order with a 6 x 13 columnar arrangement.
A Late Archaic Doric Temple of Athena (ca. 500 B.C.) at Paestum.
The Temple of Athena at Sounion (ca. 470 B.C.) with its L-shaped colonnade was built on the site of an earlier temple (sixth century B.C.) at the same location.

A Doric temple dating ca. 300 BC located on the acropolis of Lindos. The sanctuary stands above a grotto that likely housed the earlier phases of the cult. The amphiprostyle temple has an elongated floor plan and measures 7.75 x 21.65 m.

A temple dedicated to Athena Nike, on a bastion at the southwest end of the Acropolis of Athens. A sanctuary existed from the early 6th century BC, a small shrine was built in the mid-5th century, and the temple was built between 449 and 420 BCE. It was dismantled by the Ottomans in 1686 and reassembled between 1835 and 1844.

The Temple of Athena Pallenis was built in the mid-6th century BC and rebuilt on a larger scale in the late 5th century BC. In myth, the location was the site of King Eurystheus' tomb and Theseus' victory over the Pallantidai. In 546 BC, it was the site of the battle by which Peisistratos finally established his tyranny. At the end of the 1st century BC, the structure was disassembled and rebuilt in the Agora of Athens as the Temple of Ares.
Pausanias describes the Temple of Athena Polias at Erythrai as containing a wooden image of the enthroned goddess.
The Temple of Athena Pronaia, located at Delphi, is a peripteral Doric building (6 x 12 columns). It was built ca. 500 BC and is the largest building in the sanctuary of Athena Pronaia.

name: Temple of Athena Skiras at Phaleron

props.description: This sanctuary, reported in Phaleron by Pausanias, is thought to have been located near the modern "old town" of Phaleron in Attica. It is not to be confused with the Temple of Athena Skiras on the island of Salamis, reported by Herodotus.
The Temple of Augustus in Pula is a Roman temple that dates to the time of Augustus. The tetrastyle Corinthian temple sites a high podium.

The Temple of Augustus and Roma at Ankara likely dates ca. 20 BCE. It is also known as the "Monumentum Ancyranum", highlighting the structure's role in preserving the most complete copy of the inscription known as the "Res Gestae Divi Augusti", which was promulgated in both ancient Greek and Latin following the death of emperor Augustus in 14 CE.

The so-called Temple of Augustus et Livia dates ca. 10 B.C. and was originally dedicated to Roma and Augustus. The temple was located on the ancient forum of the city. By the fifth century the temple had become a Christian church. It was extensively restored in the nineteenth century.
The Temple of Augustus dates to the second century A.D.
Temple of Ba'al (or Bel) in the ancient city of Palmyra.
Temple in Palmyra dedicated to the Canaanite god Baalshamin.
Part of the complex of Roman temples at Baalbek, the Temple of Bacchus is one of the best-preserved temples of the Roman world. It is conventionally dated to the second century CE.
Temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.
A temple building located on the northwest side of Dura-Europos.
Located at the southeast corner of the Forum Romanum, the aedes Castoris is a temple of ancient Rome vowed by the dictator Postumius in 499 BCE.

name: Temple of Castor and Pollux, Cora

props.description: The Temple of Castor and Pollux at Cora was originally built between the fourth and the second centuries B.C. The partially extant architrave carries an inscription of the first century B.C.
A large classical-style Roman temple built shortly after the conquest of Britannia in A.D. 43 by the emperor Claudius. Its remains lie beneath Colchester Castle.
The Temple of Concord, located in the so-called "Valle dei Templi" of Agrigento, dates ca. 440-430 B.C.
The Attalids built a temple at Pessinus to honor Cybele.
An ancient temple relocated in the 1960s before the completion of the Aswan dam.

An ancient Egyptian temple that was dedicated to Dedwen, the Nubian serpent goddess. It was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979, as part of "Nubian Monuments from Abu Simbel to Philae". It was relocated to its current location.
A Doric temple ascribed to Demeter with origins in the fourth century B.C.
A Punic and Roman temple sacred to Demeter and Kore located at Bagoi, Perdaxius, Sardinia.

name: Temple of Demeter at the Sanctuary of Demeter Malophoros, Selinus

props.description: The Temple of Demeter was used for the worship of Demeter (Malophoros), goddess of fertility, grain and agriculture. Built in the seventh century BC, the temple was used continuously until its abandonment ca. 250 BC

A rock-cut temple (speos) located in Lower Nubia, the Temple of Derr was built by Pharaoh Ramesses II (19th Dynasty). The temple was relocated to its current position in 1964 along with the Temple of Amada.
The temple in the forum of Augusta Emerita is conventionally known by the misnomer "Temple of Diana", yet was actually dedicated to the Imperial cult.
A Temple of Dionysos in the Ionic order begun in the Hellenistic period.
According to Cassius Dio, Tiberius dedicated a temple to the deified Augustus at a location marking the site of the princeps' death. Its precise location is not known.
The temple on the Sacra Via dedicated to the deified Iulius Caesar, authorized in 42 B.C. and built by Augustus, with its dedication occurring on 18th August, 29 B.C.

A small rock-cut temple dedicated to Amun, Horus, and Satis dated to the 18th Dynasty. Originally located near Qasr Ibrim/Premis, the temple was relocated to the Museo Egizio in Turin, Italy, in the 1960s prior to the area's flooding beneath the surface of Lake Nasser.
Pherai was the main cult center of the goddess Enodia. Today, the foundations of the temple of Enodia (or Ennodia) at found at the site.
Centerpoint of sanctuary at modern Jublains
A Corinthian order temple in Pompeii dedicated to Fortuna Redux and likely dating to the late first century BC (VII, 4, 1).

The Temple of Hadrian at Rome, also referred to as the Hadrianeum, was a temple located in the Campus Martius dedicated to the deified Hadrian by Antoninus Pius in 145 CE. Some of the temple's substantial remains were incorporated into a 17th-century building designed by architect Carlo Fontana in Rome's Piazza di Pietra.
A small, prostyle Roman temple located on Curetes Street in ancient Ephesus, the Temple of Hadrian was dedicated to the emperor Hadrian in 138 CE by Publius Quintilius.

The Temple of Hathor at Deir el-Medina dates to the reign of the Ptolemies. It was later repurposed as a Christian installation known as Deir el-Medina or "Monastery of the City", from which the site's modern toponym is derived.
A Punic temple founded in the first century B.C.
An Archaic Doric temple probably dedicated to Hera in the central urban sanctuary of Paestum/Poseidonia.
A fifth century B.C. Doric temple, the so-called Temple of Hera II is also sometimes referred to as the Temple of Neptune.
An archaic temple dating ca. 610 B.C.

The Temple of Hera at Olympia, also known as the Heraion, is a peripteral temple located in the northwest corner of the Altis precinct, dedicated to Hera. Built ca. 600-590 B.C., the Heraion is the oldest temple at Olympia and one of the earliest known examples of the Doric order in Greece.
The Temple of Heracles is one of the oldest temples in the Valley of the Temples in ancient Akragas, Sicily, dating from around the late 6th century BCE.
The Temple of Herakles at Cora was built during the 80s B.C. The temple is tetrastyle in its plan and built following the Etrusco-Italic temple tradition.
Remains of the fourth-century BCE Temple of Hercules are preserved in the edifice of San Marco d'Alunzio.
The Temple of Hercules is located within the Amman Citadel in Amman, Jordan. The temple was built during the administration of Geminius Marcianus, ca. 162-166.
A monopteros in the Forum Boarium at Rome built in the late second century BC, perhaps by L. Mummius Achaicus.
The Temple of Hercules at Lanuvium.
Temple of Hermes at Çatıören
A large temple located in the Kharga Oasis that is dated to the Saite-Persian period.
Monumental Ptolemaic temple dedicated to Horus, constructed between 237 BCE and 57 BCE at the settlement of Apollinopolis Magna.

From the late Early Dynastic period (ED III, ca. 2500/2400 BC) until 614 BC, there was a temple in the city of Assur dedicated to Ištar, a goddess who was revered in that important northern-Mesopotamian city in a few different forms. Several building phases (designated “Temples H–A,” from earliest to latest) were excavated by W. Andrae between 1905 and 1914. Periodically the location and orientation of the Ištar temple changed, especially during the reigns of the Middle Assyrian kings Tukultī-Ninurta I (1243–1207 BC) and Aššur-rēša-iši I (1132–1115 BC). The earliest and latest textual records for the existence of the temple date respectively to the time of Ititi, a local ruler of Assur during the Old Akkadian Period, and the reign of Sîn-šarra-iškun (626–612 BC), the penultimate king of Assyria.
The Temple of Isis in Pompeii (VIII, 7, 28) is a sanctuary sacred to Isis that dates from the second century BC.

name: Temple of Iuno Moneta at Signia

props.description: The remains of the Temple of Iuno Moneta on the acropolis of Segni support the later (tenth century) church of St. Peter.
A celebrated cult-place sacred to Iuno Sospita that retained its sacred significance even after a cult to the same divinity was dedicated in the Forum Holitorium in Rome in 194 BCE.
A monumental first-century A.D. Romano-Celtic sanctuary located in Autun, Saône-et-Loire, France, to the northwest of the ancient city of Augustodunum.
A temple of the Doric order that was built at Akragas ca. 450 BCE.
Juno Caelestis, a successor divinity of the Punic Tanit, is celebrated by a sanctuary built at Dougga ca. 222-235 CE, and sponsored by Q. Gabinius Rufus Felix.
The Temple of Jupiter (VII,8,1) in Pompeii sits at the north end of the forum. It was built in the middle of the second century BC.
A temple dedicated to the Capitoline triad is located on the forum of Saepinum.
A second-century CE temple sacred to Jupiter.
The Temple of Jupiter Heliopolitanus at Baalbek is considered the largest temple of the Roman world. It served as an oracular shrine.
The chief temple of the city of Rome was located on the Capitoline Hill.
The Temple of Jupiter in Damascus was begun during the rule of Augustus and completed during the rule of Constantius II.
A temple located at the fourth mile of the Via Appia.

An ancient temple dated ca. 30 BCE was originally located at Bab al-Kalabsha (Gate of Kalabsha), some 50 km south of Aswan. It was relocated to its present site during the construction of the Aswan High Dam with sponsorship from the government of Germany.
A temple dedicated to the god Khnum constructed during the Ptolemaic and Roman period in the city of Esna (Latopolis).
New Kingdom temple to the god Khonsu, constructed by Ramesses III and expanded by Ptolemy III Euergetes.

A sanctuary dedicated to Kronos and Rhea in the area of the Olympieion in Athens, attested from Classical times into the Roman Imperial period. A 2nd-century AD Doric temple is probably associated with it.

The Temple of Magna Mater, also known as the Temple of Cybele, was dedicated near the Clivus Victoriae on the Palatine Hill on 11 April 191 BCE. The original temple was destroyed by fire in 111 BCE. Following another series of destructive fires, Augustus restored the temple, as he records in his Res Gestae Divi Augusti.
A temple sacred to Isis and Osiris of Roman date, relocated before the completion of the Aswan dam in the 1960s.
The Temple of Mars Ultor was dedicated in the Forum of Augustus in 2 B.C., fulfilling a vow that Octavian had made to the god in 42 B.C.

name: Temple of Mater Matuta at Satricum

props.description: A sanctuary dedicated to Mater Matuta that was in use between the seventh and fifth centuries B.C.
A Gallo-Roman temple of Mercurius, located atop the modern Puy-de-Dôme, commune Orcines in France.

This temple is located at the modern Egyptian village of El-Qala, which is near Coptos (modern Qift). It was built likely under Augustus, expanded by Caligula and Claudius, and survived into the Coptic period.
The Temple of Minerva at Theveste dates to the third century A.D.
Egyptian temple dedicated to the god Montou in Armant, built and enlarged from the Middle Kingdom to the Roman period.
Egyptian temple dedicated to the god Montou in Medamud, built and enlarged from the Middle Kingdom to the Roman period.
A temple sacred to Montu located at El-Tod, Egypt. The temple's foundations were laid during the Fifth Dynasty and the sanctuary continued in use under the Ptolemies.
A temple dedicated to the Babylonian god of wisdom.

name: Temple of Nabû of the ḫarû at Assur

props.description: Sîn-šarra-iškun (626–612 BC), Assyria’s penultimate king, built a new temple for the god Nabû of the ḫarû at Assur. The temple was constructed in vacant lot that was next to the temple of the Assyrian Ištar. That Neo-Assyrian king modelled the temple’s plan on that the Nabû temple at Kalhu (Nimrud) and named it after the Nabû temple at Babylon. Egidrukalamasumu (“House Which Bestows the Scepter of the Land”) was destroyed in 614 BC, when Assur was destroyed by the Medes.
A Doric peripteral temple was built in the sanctuary in the middle of the fifth century BCE. Construction began around 460-450 BCE and continued until 430–420 BCE.

The Temple of Peltuinum is located on the forum square; its final iteration was that of a Corinthian hexastyle prostyle sanctuary of the Roman phase of the city when the shrine may have been dedicated to Apollo. The sanctuary also may have had a pre-Roman phase connected with a sacred spring.
A tetrastyle temple dedicated to the god Portunus and dating to the late second century or early first century BC.

name: Temple of Poseidon and Amphitrite on Tenos

props.description: A peripteral temple.
The Poseidon sanctuary at Isthmia lies 16 km to the east of Corinth. The initial phase belongs to the seventh century BC, although the sanctuary was destroyed ca. 470 BC and rebuilt in 440 B.C.
The hexastyle temple of Poseidon at Sounion was built ca. 444-440 B.C. atop the ruins of an Archaic period temple.
Originally constructed by the New Kingdom pharoah Thutmose III, this component of the temple complex in Karnak was repaired and embellished multiple times through the Roman period.

The Temple of Quirinus was located near the Capitolium Vetus. The temple was built ex manubiis by Lucius Papirius Cursor in 293 B.C., fulfilling the vow made by his father in 325 B.C. Tradition holds that the republican-period temple was sited on an even earlier altar dedicated by the Sabines. The topography and location of the temple are both matters of significant debate.
The Temple of Ramesses II at Abydos in Egypt.
The Temple or Altar of Roma and Augustus on the Acropolis of Athens was erected shortly after 27 BC near the east front of the Periklean Parthenon.

name: Temple of Roma and Augustus, Leptis Magna

props.description: The Temple of Roma and Augustus at Leptis Magna is a large temple (ca. 46 x 20 meters) situated on the so-called "Old Forum". It was initially erected between 8 BCE and 19 CE using limestone from Ras el-Hammam. The temple has a pseudo-peripteral temple and is oriented to the southeast. Reconstruction and restoration in the second century CE used marble instead of the original limestone.
A temple along the Sacra Via dedicated to Valerius Romulus, son of Maxentius.
A Punic-Roman temple dedicated to Sardus Pater Babai located near Fluminimaggiore in southern Sardinia. The original phase of the temple dates ca. 500 BC.
The Temple of Seti I at Abydos is also known as the "Great Temple of Abydos".

name: Temple of Sîn and Šamaš at Assur

props.description: A double-temple dedicated to the moon-god Sîn and the sun-god Šamaš existed at the city Assur from the Old Assyrian period onwards. The plan of this holy structure changed several times over the course of its long history, most notably in the Neo-Assyrian period, in the reign of Ashurbanipal II (883–859 BC). The Sîn temple was called Eḫulḫuldirdirra and the Šamaš temple went by the name Ebabbarra.

A temple dedicated to a local form of the Egyptian god Sobek, Soknebtunis, at Tebtunis. The temple was built by Ptolemy I in the late fourth-early third centuries and was greatly expanded by Ptolemy XII in the first century BCE.
1st century CE temple to Minerva at the Aquae Sulis in Bath, England in use roughly until 410 AD.

An Etruscan temple of the fourth century BCE, associated with the ancient settlement of Telamon. It lies on Mount Talamonaccio, across the bay from the modern village of Talamone, north of Orbetello, Italy.
The temple at Dura-Europos was named by excavators according to its association with the city's Gadde/Fortune, but it has been more recently recognized as a temple principally devoted to Malakbel.
The Temple of the Oxus is a Seleucid-era temple situated within the citadel of Takhti-Sangin, a Graeco-Bactrian town between the Vakhsh and Panj rivers.
Temple situated on a raised podium and accessed by stairs, dedicated to an unknown deity, and located in Block A1 at Dura-Europos.
Near a bath complex built under Commodus, a Severan complex that included a plaza, porticoes, and a temple dedicated to the Severan family was added.

name: Temple of the Severan family at Cuicul

props.description: A temple dedicated to the genius of the Severan family.

A second-century B.C. Roman temple located on the acropolis of ancient Tibur. The so-called Temple of the Sibyl is a pseudo-peripteral building executed in the Ionic order. Some portions of the structure, including the side walls and rea wall, survive to the height of the capitals.

name: Temple of the Storm God at Aleppo

props.description: Bronze Age temple dedicated to the weather god Hadad in the citadel of Halab (modern Aleppo).
The Temple of the Winged Lions is a colonnaded temple built in Petra in order to honor the Arabian goddess al-Uzza.
A well-preserved Roman period temple located in modern Dmeir, Syria. The sanctuary was later converted into a fortification.
The Temple of Thutmose III at Deir el-Bahari was built ca. 1435-1425 BCE.
A sanctuary dedicated to Trajan in the 'urbs nova' of Italica that flourished under the patronage of Hadrian.
The Temple of Trajan at Pergamon, also referred to as the Traianeum, was dedicated to Trajan and Zeus Philios and completed during the reign of Hadrian.

The Temple of Veiovis, also attested as Aedes Vediovis, was a temple of ancient Rome located on the Capitoline Hill in a location known as "inter duos lucos" that is interpreted to mean a saddle between the two summits of the hill. The remains of a temple podium were discovered beneath the northwest corner of the Palazzo Senatore in 1938-1939.

The Temple of Venus (VIII,1) is located adjacent to Pompeii's Porta Marina. The site was cleared in the early years of the colony to make way for the temple. Seriously damaged by the AD 62 earthquake, the sanctuary was still being rebuilt at the time of its ultimate destruction.
The Temple of Venus Genetrix located in the Forum Iulium was dedicated ca. 46 BCE.

The "Temple of Vespasian" (VII, 9, 2), also referred to as Aedes Genii Augusti, is situated between the Temple of the Lares Publici and the Building of Eumachia on the eastern side of the forum of Pompeii. Its precise attribution is still a matter of debate.
A temple in the Roman Forum dedicated to the deified Vespasian that was begun by Titus and completed under Domitian.
The Temple of Vesta, located at the east end of the Forum Romanum.
The Temple of Vesta in Tivoli dates to the early first century B.C. It was once situated on the acropolis of the ancient city. Its ruins now lie within the grounds of the Villa Gregoriana.

A Greek temple of the Doric order (peripteral hexastyle), built in the fifth century BC, possibly to celebrate a victory against the Carthaginians. Its ruins are still visible in the archaeological site of Himera.
The Temple of Zeus is Aizanoi's main sanctuary. The temple complex was built under Domitian.
The Doric Temple of Zeus at Cyrene.
The foundations of a temple sacred to Zeus at Lebadea. Pausanias reports that the temple was incomplete, owing perhaps to its over-ambitious size or to the nagging costs associated with warfare.
A partially reconstructed Doric temple, dating to the 4th century BCE.
The Temple of Zeus at Olympia was built in the second quarter of the fifth century B.C. Libon of Elis served as architect.
The foundations of an ancient temple located to the west of ancient Astakos/Dragomestre in Aitoloakarnania.
Temple building located on the southwest side of Dura-Europos.

name: Temple of Zeus Lepsinos at Euromus

props.description: The Temple of Zeus Lepsinos at Euromus is located southeast of the ancient settlement. The well-preserved Corinthian temple dates to the reign of Hadrian.
Temple located in block C4 at Dura-Europos.
A tetrastyle temple in the Corinthian order sacred to Zeus Megistos was built at Canatha by the first century CE.
Temple located in block B3 at Dura-Europos.
An epigraphically attested temple at or near ancient Atyochorion (modern Akkent in Turkey).

name: Temple or Large Administrative Building at Sikait

props.description: A significant, large structure at Sikait in Egypt's Eastern Desert that has been variously identified as an administrative building and, most recently on basis of finds from the first systematic archaeological excavation of the structure, as a temple.

name: Temple to Nefertari at Abu Simbel

props.description: The smaller of the two temples at Abu Simbel, dedicated to the goddess Hathor and the deified queen Nefertari. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Temple to Ramesses II at Abu Simbel

props.description: The larger of the two temples at Abu Simbel, dedicated to the triad of Amun, Ra-Horakhty, and Ptah, as well as the deified Ramesses II. The temple was relocated between 1964 and 1968 to prevent its submersion under Lake Nasser after the construction of the Aswan High Dam.
Temple of unknown dedication located in Block X9 of Dura-Europos.

A temple dedicated to the emperor Claudius who was declared divus (deified) after his death in 54 CE. The platform temple occupied a large area of the Caelian Hill in Rome. The construction project was ultimately completed by the Flavians.
A double temple sacred to Venus Felix and Roma Aeterna built by Hadrian on the slope of the Velian hill to the north of the Sacra Via beginning in 121 CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Thalfang-Bäsch

Monumental religious building carved out of rock in Petra, Jordan which was used for a variety of complex religious ceremonies and customs after the time of its completion in the mid-first century CE.
A sanctuary of the Pentrian Samnites on the flanks of Monte Capraro, north of the modern Italian village of Vastogirardi.

name: The Temple of Athena Polias at Priene

props.description: The temple of Athena at Priene, designed by the architect Pytheos, is an especially good example of the Late Classical form of the Ionic order.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 D5 Theai kai Nymphai, T.

name: Tholos at the Sanctuary of Asklepios, Epidauros

props.description: The Tholos, a circular structure in the Sanctuary of Asklepios at Epidaurus, was constructed between 365 and 360 BCE. Polykleitos the Younger designed it, and it features Doric architecture and a constructed labyrinth beneath its floor.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 D2 Tigi
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Tininai
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 G4 Torricella
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Tossal del Moro
Touphion is located on the east bank of the Nile River about 20 km south of Thebes. The ruins of a multi-period temple initially built by Sesostris I were found there.
The remains of a Gallo-Roman fanum.

The ancient monument referred to as "Trajan's Kiosk" or the "Pharaoh's Bed" is an incomplete hypaethral temple that dates to the second century CE. The monument is currently located on Agilkia Island although it was originally built on the island of Philae. It was relocated in the 1960s as part of the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 D2 Trakhonas

A temple in the Doric order that begins in the sixth century BCE and continues in use until the Hellenistic period. The temple may be associated with ancient Rhypai, at Trapeza near Koumari, Aigio, Achaia.
A sanctuary in NW Kea located at Treis Ammoundas dating from the Archaic to Hellenistic periods.

Toutzis (Dendur) is located in Nubia, approximately 80 km south of modern Aswan. In 23 BCE the Roman provincial governor Petronius built a temple at the direction of Augustus. The temple was dedicated to Isis and the deified Nubian brothers Pedesi and Pihor. Imperiled by the Aswan High Dam project, the temple was relocated by UNESCO in 1963. Egypt gifted the temple to the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York City, where it has been exhibited since 1978.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Tzitzis
Complex of 20 cave temples in Madhya Pradesh dedicated to the gods Vishnu and Shiva, constructed from the 3rd-5th centuries CE.
An ancient place, cited: None

The site of an unnamed Gallo-Roman temple is situated in Thésée, Arrondissement of Romorantin-Lanthenay, Centre-Val de Loire, France. This temple site is located close to the ruins at Bâtiments des potiers de Pouillé.
An unnamed monument, settlement, or villa of Syrtica
An unnamed place of Tripolitana
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 E2 no. 62 (temple NW Anagnia)

M. Cristofani describes terracotta antefixes discovered at Tre Fontane, in the general location of the abbey of the same name. These antefixes form part of a set of evidence relevant to a discussion of the functioning of regional coroplastic workshops in central Italy in the late archaic period. They are dated from the later sixth to the early fifth centuries BCE.
An ancient temple located near Solforata, between Lavinium and the Alban Lake in modern Italy.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 no. 29 (Asgafa)
Unlabeled feature captured from the Barrington Atlas
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 C3 no. 52 (temple near Ardea)
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D3 no. 56 (temple at Algidus M.)
An Italic temple was identified in 1975 in the locality known as Porcareccia, Vacri (Chieti), Italy.
Temple constructed near the Udayagiri cave complex during the Gupta period (ca. 4th-5th century CE).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D5 Val-des-Nymphes
The Valley Temple associated with the pyramid of Khafre. The Valley Temple is linked to the pyramid and its mortuary temple via a causeway.
The modern name of a seventh-century Pallava cave temple in Mahābalipuram.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 F3 Varces-Allières-et-Risset
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Varnenum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 16 D2 Vaugrenier
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D3 Verbinum/Vironum
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 E5 Vercoiran
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G3 Vietheil
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 50 B1 Vojšanci
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Antar
The Wadi es-Sebua, also known as the Valley of the Lions, is the site of two New Kingdom Egyptian temples. This includes a temple built by Pharaoh Ramesses II (19th Dynasty).
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Wadi Lella
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Wadi Merteba
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Wadi Migdal
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 Wadi Senab
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Wanborough
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 War Pil

Weycock Hill is the site of a Roman temple excavated in 1847. According to Lewis, this was an octagonal temple with a portico. It dates to the early fourth century CE with abandonment in the fifth century.
A Romano-British temple of two phases dating to the first century.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Yanuh
The Yeha Temple is alleged to be among the oldest known extant structures in Ethiopia. It is executed in the Sabaean style and may date to the seventh century BCE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Zarnuqa
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Zekweh
A sanctuary of Zeus Aglaios located west of Metapontum.
A temple of Zeus Ainesios located atop mount Ainos on the island of Cephallania.
A sanctuary of Zeus Akraios on Mount Pelion.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Zeus Andreas, Ampeleites, T.
A sanctuary dedicated to Zeus Atabyrios located on the summit of Mt. Atavyros in Rhodes.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D4 Zeus Bennios, Soter, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E2 Zeus Bronton 1, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 Zeus Bronton 2, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D2 Zeus Bronton 3, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C1 Zeus Bronton, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C3 Zeus Bronton, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 E4 Zeus Hypatos, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E2 Zeus Melenos, T.
The temple of Zeus Olympios on Mt. Olympus
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 59 B2 Zeus Ombrios, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 59 C3 Zeus Ombrios, T.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A2 Zeus Pandemos, T.
An oracular mountaintop sanctuary of Zeus on the island of Aegina.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A4 Zeus Sabazios, T.
Zeus Thenatas, T. is an archaic Greek sanctuary built atop earlier Minoan cult remains. The open-air temple remained in use until the second century AD.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A3 Zeus Thimenos, T.
The temple of Zeus Ikmaios on Keos.
A Gallo-Roman temple of the Matronae Aufaniae.
‘Lavobrinta’? was a Roman fort on the east bank of the Severn river.
Digitized by eye in Google Earth on 13 July 2012 at an eye altitude of 683m. Dates are general settlement dates, following Foss and Mitchell in BAtlas.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B2 [Augusta] Ambianorum
*Argentomagus was a pre-Roman and later Roman settlement in the territory of the Bituriges tribe.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E2 *Supinum
Abellinum was a settlement of the Hirpini in Campania.
The deme theater of Acharnai was discovered in 2007.
A Roman amphitheater located at Agbia in what is now Tunisia (Henchir Ain Hedja).
A settlement located at the Yonne-Vanne confluence in the territory of the Senones.
Aginnum (modern Agen) was the capital of the Nitiobriges in Gaul.
An ancient town of either Oscan or Samnite origin situated in the Volturno river valley.
A Roman amphitheater at Berja.
Archaeological remains at 'Aldborough Studforth Hill' site may be the location of possible Roman amphitheater.

Discovered in late 2021 on the site of the Basler Ruderclub in Augst, this amphitheater lies just west of the Roman fort of Castrum Rauracense. It seems to have been built in an abandoned quarry sometime in the 4th century CE.
A Roman amphitheater located at Castra Vetera (modern Birten Germany)
A first century A.D. amphitheater at Alba Fucens built during the reign of Tiberius.
A Roman amphitheater located south and outside the city walls at Anazarbos (modern Dilekkaya in Turkey).

Intramural amphitheater located within the military sector on the north side of Dura-Europos. Built on the site of the former Bath F3, which was destroyed by fire. The amphitheater's dedication in 216 CE is attested by an inscription found fallen from above entrance C on the south side of the amphitheater.
A Roman amphitheater located in Cañaveruelas, Spain.
The Amphitheater of Micia.
The Amphitheater of Pompeii is the earliest known surviving example of a Roman amphitheater. It was built between 80 and 70 B.C.
The Roman Amphitheater of Sbeitla.
The Sanctuary of the Three Gauls (Tres Galliae) was a late first century B.C. sanctuary in Lugdunum; the amphitheater dates to 19 B.C.
The small Roman amphitheater of Thizica.
The ruins of the Roman amphitheater Saint-André-sur-Cailly are located in the hamlet of Bout Levé.
Third century A.D. Roman amphitheater; declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979.
A stone-built Amphitheatre at Thapsus.

The Amphitheatrum Castrense is a Roman amphitheater built in the third century AD. Its structure was later incorporated into the Aurelianic Walls. Adjacent to the amphitheater is the Christian church of Santa Croce in Gerusalemme.
The Flavian amphitheater at Rome (the so-called "Colosseum").

name: Ancient Greek theater at Palazzolo Acreide

props.description: The theater at ancient Akrai.
An ancient theater located at Amphilochian Argos. There are traces of the theater to be found at Ag. Ioannis near Neokhori in Aitoloakarnania, West Greece.
The Ancient Theater of Argos was a Hellenistic theater initially constructed in 320 BCE and later renovated by the Romans.
The ancient theater of Carthaea.
The theater at Chaeronea
The Ancient Greek theater of Eretria is located inside the so-called West Gate of the city walls in the northwest sector of the city. It dates from ca. 400 B.C. to ca. 150 B.C.
The Ancient theater of Kourion was first built in the second century B.C. It remained in use until the fourth century.
The ancient theater of Maroneia was constructed in the Hellenistic period and later remodeled in the Roman period.
Originally constructed during the Hellenistic period, the theater at Teos was extensively modified during the early Roman empire.
The "Anfiteatro di Bleso" is a second century A.D. Roman amphitheater at Tibur (modern Tivoli in Italy).
The "Anfiteatro Fausto" or "Amphitheater of Faustus" refers to a Roman amphitheater that was built during the reign of Tiberius (likely 30 or 32 CE) at Terni.
A Roman amphitheater located to the northwest of Nola and dating to the first century BCE.
The so-called "Anfiteatro minore" is a small amphitheater at Puteoli dating to the early Imperial period.

A city founded ca. 300 BC by Seleucus I Nicator, a successor of Alexander the Great. Antioch was a great trading center and numbered as one of the four cities of the Syrian tetrapolis. Justinian I renamed the city 'Theoupolis' in the sixth century AD.
The Aquincum Civil Amphitheater is the smaller of two Roman amphitheaters located in Budapest, Hungary. It was built between ca. A.D. 250 and 300.
The Aquincum Military Amphitheater dates to the reign of Antoninus Pius.
A Roman amphitheater of the second century that was enlarged during the course of the third, the so-called Arènes de Cimiez measures 67 by 56 meters.
A 12,000 seat Roman amphitheater located in Fréjus, France.
The Roman amphitheater of Lutetia (modern Paris).
The Arènes de Nîmes is a Roman amphitheater built at the end of the first century.
The Arles Roman amphitheater or Arènes d'Arles was built ca. A.D. 90 and could seat over 20,000 spectators.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Arnières-sur-Iton
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 12 B2 Arnsburg
Now the modern Ecija on the Genil river, Astigi was the capital of one of the four conventus of Baetica and became an Augustan colony after 27 B.C.
A city of Campania located between Capua and Neapolis.
The Roman amphitheater of Augusta Bagiennorum.
Augusta Vindelicum/Aelia Augusta (modern Augsburg, Germany) was the capital of Raetia. The city was established in 15 BC by Drusus and Tiberius.
Augustoritum (modern Limoges, France) in Gaul was founded as a Roman city ca. 10 B.C.
The Avenches Roman amphitheater.
A Roman amphitheater located in the modern Siliana Governorate of Tunisia.
Remains of a small theater or odeon at ancient Kelenderis.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 C4 Balsa
An ancient city of Caria, now known in Turkish as Varvıl. The site is located near the modern Turkish village of Boğaziçi in Muğla province.
An ancient settlement of the Batavi. Modern Nijmegen in the Netherlands.
The Beaumont-sur-Oise Gallo-Roman amphitheater.

A set of poros benches built on the east slope of the Kolonos Agoraios at the edge of the Athenian Agora in the fifth century BC, which seated 400-500 people and perhaps served as a meeting place for a council or court. They were truncated and rendered inaccessible by further construction in the fourth century BC.
A Roman amphitheater in eastern Thrace.
The Bobadela Roman amphitheater dates to the second century.

Modern Butrint (Butrinti) in Albania, ancient Bouthroton was an ancient Greek and later Roman city located in Epirus. The city became an important bishopric by the sixth century. The site was enrolled as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1992 because of its archaeological remains, which date from prehistoric times down through the late Middle Ages.
Site, in modern France, of a Gallo-Roman settlement whose ancient name is not known.
Amphitheatre of the legionary fortress at Caerleon
The Roman amphitheater at Cáparra (Oliva de Plasencia, Spain).
An important ancient city of Campania with Villanovan and Etruscan origins.
A Gallo-Roman site located in the Barguelonne valley that covers some 4,000 square meters. Excavation (1962-1964) suggested the site was likely a rural sanctuary that included a theater and baths.
Remains of a Roman amphitheater at Chassenon.
The ruins of a Roman amphitheater.
Colonia Iulia Augusta Paterna Faventia Barcino (Barcelona) was a Roman settlement in Tarraconensis founded by Augustus.
The Roman amphitheater at Conímbriga.

The origins of the theater at Corinth lie in the fifth century B.C. The complex was further expanded during the Hellenistic period. An elaborate renovation during the reign of Hadrian further improved the complex and added a new orchestra and scaenae frons.

The Crypta Balbi, mentioned by name only in the Regionary Catalogues, most likely refers to a vaulted passageway built in the Campus Martius by the proconsul Lucius Cornelius Balbus in 15 BC, who also dedicated a theater there in the same year.
The Cyaneae Theater dates to the Roman imperial period.
Divodurum/Mettis was a Celtic capital oppidum of the Mediomatrici tribe and was captured by Iulius Caesar in 52 BC.
The Drobeta Roman amphitheater.

name: Eastern theater at Laodicea ad Lycum

props.description: Digitized by hand in Google Earth in July 2012 at an eye altitude of 683m. Dates are general for the settlement, following Foss and Mitchell in BAtlas 65.
A building complex located in the city's agora, the Ekklesiasterion/Theater is dated ca. 625-300 B.C.
A fourth-century BCE theater at Euonymos with a rectangular orchestra.

Caius Gracchus dispatched a Roman colony to Fabrateria Nova in 124 B.C. The relationship between Fabrateria Nova and the earlier Volscian site of Fabrateria Vetus remains unclear. The favored location for Fabrateria Nova is at S. Giovanni Incarico, where the Liri and Sacco rivers intersect.
Remains of a Roman amphitheater at Fabrateria Nova.
The Falerii Novi amphitheater is located north of the city walls.
The Flavian Amphitheater at Puteoli is an amphitheater constructed in the port town of Puteoli under the Flavian dynasty around 80 CE.
An ancient settlement of likely Greek origin along the Via Appia. Cicero was assassinated near Formiae (now Formia) in 43 B.C.
The Gallo-Roman amphitheater at Grannum dates to the late first century B.C.
The Gallo-Roman amphitheater at Montbouy dates to the early Imperial period.
A Gallo-Roman amphitheater dating to the early third century CE.
A theater / amphitheater dating to the early second century.
A third and fourth century theater at Alba Helviorum.
The Gallo-Roman theater at Dalheim.
The Gallo-Roman Theater of Lillebonne was in use from the first to the third century.
The Roman theater of Mandeure dates to the first century CE.
An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D3 Gemellae
